topics.title: Different boundary conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET415 - ADVANCED MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Different boundary conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the mathematical theory of **Elasticity**, a **Boundary Value Problem (BVP)** is the complete specification of a physical problem which requires the determination of stresses, strains, and displacements at every point within an elastic body, subject to:
1. The governing **Equilibrium Equations** (Newton's second law in static form)
2. The **Constitutive Relations** (Generalized Hooke's Law)
3. The **Compatibility Equations** (Saint-Venant's compatibility)
4. The prescribed **Boundary Conditions** on the surface of the body

The **Boundary Conditions** are the prescribed conditions that the field variables (displacements $u, v, w$ or stress components $\sigma_{ij}$) must satisfy on the boundary surface $S$ of the elastic body. The solution of any elasticity problem is considered *complete* only when the field equations AND the boundary conditions are simultaneously satisfied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A solution that satisfies equilibrium, compatibility, and constitutive laws but violates the boundary conditions is a **trivial/invalid** solution. A valid KTU answer must verify all three conditions at the boundary.

### 1.2 The Three Primary Classifications of Boundary Conditions

**1. Displacement (Essential / Kinematic / Dirichlet) Boundary Conditions:**
The displacements (and sometimes rotations) are prescribed at the boundary. Mathematically expressed as:
$$u_i = \bar{u}_i \quad \text{on } S_u$$
where $S_u \subset S$ is the portion of the boundary where displacements are prescribed.

**2. Traction (Natural / Static / Neumann) Boundary Conditions:**
The surface tractions (forces per unit area) are prescribed at the boundary:
$$T_i = \sigma_{ij} \, n_j = \bar{T}_i \quad \text{on } S_\sigma$$
where $S_\sigma \subset S$ is the portion where tractions are prescribed, and $n_j$ is the outward unit normal vector.

**3. Mixed (Robin) Boundary Conditions:**
A linear combination of displacement and traction is prescribed (e.g., elastic foundation problems):
$$\alpha u_i + \beta T_i = \bar{f}_i \quad \text{on } S_m$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Theorem:** The complete boundary $S$ of the body must satisfy $S_u \cup S_\sigma \cup S_m = S$, and for **uniqueness** of solution, at least one displacement component must be prescribed somewhere on the boundary.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Leaky Bucket Problem**
> Imagine a bucket filled with water resting on a table.
> - If you **fix** the bottom of the bucket to the table (no movement allowed) → this is a **displacement boundary condition**: $u=0, v=0$.
> - If you only know that the water exerts a downward pressure of $\bar{p}$ on the table → this is a **traction boundary condition**: $\sigma_{yy} = -\bar{p}$.
> - If the bucket sits on a soft foam pad (resists movement proportional to displacement) → this is a **mixed boundary condition**: $k u = \bar{T}$.
>
> To fully solve the problem, you need to know **everything happening on the surface**, not just inside the body.

### 1.4 Saint-Venant's Principle (The "Relaxation" Concept)

In engineering practice, exact satisfaction of boundary conditions is often mathematically intractable. **Saint-Venant's Principle** (1863) states:

> *"The difference between the effects of two different but statically equivalent loadings (i.e., same resultant force and moment) becomes very small at sufficiently large distances from the points of application of the loads."*

This allows engineers to **replace complicated exact boundary tractions with simpler, statically equivalent systems**, provided we are interested in stresses away from the loaded region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** Saint-Venant's Principle is the *engineer's escape clause* and is heavily tested in Module 1 questions. State it verbatim, mention the **"sufficiently large distances"** caveat, and acknowledge that it gives only an **approximate** solution near the loaded region.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Formulation — The Boundary Operators

Let $\Omega$ be the elastic body and $S$ be its closed boundary with outward unit normal $\mathbf{n} = (n_x, n_y, n_z)$. The boundary operators are:

**Traction Vector (Cauchy's Stress Theorem):**
The traction on a surface with normal $\mathbf{n}$ is:
$$T_x = \sigma_{xx} n_x + \sigma_{xy} n_y + \sigma_{xz} n_z$$
$$T_y = \sigma_{yx} n_x + \sigma_{yy} n_y + \sigma_{yz} n_z$$
$$T_z = \sigma_{zx} n_x + \sigma_{zy} n_y + \sigma_{zz} n_z$$

In compact tensor notation: $T_i = \sigma_{ij} n_j$ (summation over $j$).

### 2.2 The Six Independent Types of Boundary Prescriptions

For a 3D problem, we have six independent boundary prescriptions (3 displacement components + 3 traction components):

| # | Component | Prescribed Quantity | Engineering Example |
|---|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $u$ | $u = \bar{u}$ on $S_u$ | Built-in support on a beam |
| 2 | $v$ | $v = \bar{v}$ on $S_u$ | Roller support restraining one direction |
| 3 | $w$ | $w = \bar{w}$ on $S_u$ | Fixed plate surface |
| 4 | $T_x$ | $T_x = \bar{T}_x$ on $S_\sigma$ | Applied shear on top face |
| 5 | $T_y$ | $T_y = \bar{T}_y$ on $S_\sigma$ | Applied normal pressure |
| 6 | $T_z$ | $T_z = \bar{T}_z$ on $S_\sigma$ | Distributed axial load |

### 2.3 Boundary Conditions on Principal Coordinate Surfaces

The traction formulas simplify dramatically on the three principal coordinate planes:

**On face with $n_x = 1, n_y = n_z = 0$ (i.e., the face $x = \text{const}$):**
$$T_x = \sigma_{xx}, \quad T_y = \sigma_{xy}, \quad T_z = \sigma_{xz}$$

**On face with $n_y = 1, n_x = n_z = 0$ (i.e., the face $y = \text{const}$):**
$$T_x = \sigma_{xy}, \quad T_y = \sigma_{yy}, \quad T_z = \sigma_{yz}$$

**On face with $n_z = 1, n_x = n_y = 0$ (i.e., the face $z = \text{const}$):**
$$T_x = \sigma_{xz}, \quad T_y = \sigma_{yz}, \quad T_z = \sigma_{zz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** On the face $x = \text{const}$, the traction is just the **column** of the stress tensor associated with $x$. The first index of the stress is the normal to the face, and the second is the direction of the traction.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Domain of Application |
|--------------------|---------|------------------------|
| $u_i = \bar{u}_i$ | Essential (displacement) BC | Clamped/built-in supports, prescribed motion |
| $T_i = \sigma_{ij} n_j = \bar{T}_i$ | Natural (traction) BC | Free surface ($T_i=0$), pressure loads |
| $\alpha u_i + \beta T_i = \bar{f}_i$ | Mixed (Robin) BC | Elastic foundation, Winkler-type supports |
| $S_u \cup S_\sigma = S$ | Boundary partition condition | Required for a well-posed BVP |
| $T_i = 0$ on $S_\sigma$ | Traction-free surface | Outer lateral surfaces of unloaded prisms |
| $\sigma_{ij} = 0$ on $S_\sigma$ | Stress-free face | For a face with $n_j$ as the normal direction |
| St. Venant's Principle | Equivalence at large distances | Approximate solutions, far-field analysis |
| $M_{\text{resultant}} = \int_S T_i \, dS$ | Statically equivalent loading | Verifying Saint-Venant substitution |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Wing-skin pressure distribution → traction BCs on a free surface.
- **Civil:** Bridge bearings → mixed BCs (elastic pad stiffness).
- **Mechanical:** Press-fit shafts → prescribed radial displacement BCs.
- **Geotechnical:** Soil-structure interaction → Robin-type (Winkler) foundation BCs.
- **Biomedical:** Bone-implant interface → complex mixed BCs requiring numerical treatment.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Real-World Question Hook:** Examiners love asking *"Why is the clamped end of a cantilever modeled using displacement BCs while the free end uses traction BCs?"* The answer lies in the **physical constraint** — the wall prevents motion (displacement is zero), while the free end has no force applied (traction is zero).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Boundary Conditions for a Cantilever Beam (Classic KTU Problem)

**Problem Setup:** A cantilever beam of length $L$, rectangular cross-section, fixed at $x=0$ and free at $x=L$. The free end carries a vertical point load $P$ at the centroid.

**Step 1 — Identify the boundary domain partitions:**
The lateral surfaces of the beam are $S_{\text{lat}}$. The two end faces are $S_{\text{left}}$ at $x=0$ and $S_{\text{right}}$ at $x=L$. We have:
$$S = S_{\text{left}} \cup S_{\text{right}} \cup S_{\text{lat}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Essential (Displacement) Boundary Conditions on the fixed end $x=0$:**

The fixed end prevents both translation and rotation. In terms of displacement components:
$$u(0, y, z) = 0$$
$$v(0, y, z) = 0$$
$$w(0, y, z) = 0$$

But rotation prevention imposes an additional condition. The cross-section at $x=0$ must remain plane and perpendicular to the deformed axis. For the centroidal axis at $y=z=0$:
$$\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right)_{(0, 0, 0)} = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the rotation condition?** In advanced elasticity, the displacement BCs fully capture the constraint. For a 3D elasticity model with $u(0,y,z)=0$, the rotation is *automatically* prevented. The condition on $\partial v/\partial x$ is needed only for the **beam-theory** (1D Euler-Bernoulli) approximation used in elementary strength of materials.

**Step 3 — Apply Natural (Traction) Boundary Conditions on the free end $x=L$:**

On the face $x=L$, the outward unit normal is $\mathbf{n} = (1, 0, 0)$. Using the traction operator:
$$T_x \vert_{x=L} = \sigma_{xx} \vert_{x=L}$$
$$T_y \vert_{x=L} = \sigma_{xy} \vert_{x=L}$$
$$T_z \vert_{x=L} = \sigma_{xz} \vert_{x=L}$$

A concentrated point load $P$ at the centroid is the limit of a distributed pressure. The statically equivalent pressure over the cross-section $A$ is:
$$p_x = \frac{P}{A}, \quad p_y = 0, \quad p_z = 0$$

Thus, the boundary conditions at $x=L$ are:
$$\int_{S_{\text{right}}} \sigma_{xx} \, dA = P \quad (\text{resultant axial force})$$
$$\int_{S_{\text{right}}} \sigma_{xy} \, dA = 0 \quad (\text{zero shear resultant — point load only})$$
$$\int_{S_{\text{right}}} \sigma_{xz} \, dA = 0 \quad (\text{zero bending moment — point load at centroid})$$

**Step 4 — Apply Natural Boundary Conditions on the Lateral Surface:**

The lateral surface is traction-free, so $T_i = 0$ everywhere on $S_{\text{lat}}$. The outward normal $\mathbf{n}$ on the lateral surface has $n_x = 0$ and $(n_y, n_z)$ lies in the cross-sectional plane. This gives:
$$T_i = \sigma_{ij} n_j = \sigma_{ix} n_x + \sigma_{iy} n_y + \sigma_{iz} n_z = 0$$

Simplifying with $n_x = 0$:
$$\sigma_{iy} n_y + \sigma_{iz} n_z = 0 \quad \text{for } i = x, y, z$$

**Step 5 — Use Saint-Venant's Principle to Simplify:**

At the free end $x=L$, the exact distribution $\sigma_{xx} = P/A$ is replaced by the statically equivalent uniform distribution. Saint-Venant's principle guarantees that the error is negligible for $x < L - 2h$, where $h$ is the cross-sectional depth.

### 3.2 Derivation: Boundary Conditions for Plane Stress and Plane Strain

**Plane Stress** (e.g., thin plate loaded in-plane with $\sigma_{zz} = \sigma_{xz} = \sigma_{yz} = 0$):

The boundary conditions simplify to a 2D form. On a surface with $\mathbf{n} = (n_x, n_y, 0)$:
$$T_x = \sigma_{xx} n_x + \sigma_{xy} n_y$$
$$T_y = \sigma_{xy} n_x + \sigma_{yy} n_y$$
$$T_z = 0 \quad (\text{automatically satisfied})$$

**Plane Strain** (e.g., long dam, tunnel cross-section, with $\varepsilon_{zz} = 0$):

For plane strain, the out-of-plane strain is constrained: $\varepsilon_{zz} = 0$, leading to a non-zero $\sigma_{zz} = \nu(\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy})$. The in-plane traction BCs are identical to plane stress, but $\sigma_{zz}$ does **not** vanish on the boundary.

**Step-by-step plane strain derivation at lateral surface $z = \pm h/2$:**

The outward normal is $\mathbf{n} = (0, 0, \pm 1)$. By Cauchy's theorem:
$$T_x = \sigma_{xz} = 0 \quad \text{(traction-free)}$$
$$T_y = \sigma_{yz} = 0 \quad \text{(traction-free)}$$
$$T_z = \sigma_{zz} = \nu(\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy}) \quad \text{(NOT zero, even though traction-free!)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Distinction:** For plane stress, $\sigma_{zz} = 0$ everywhere (including the surface). For plane strain, $\sigma_{zz} = \nu(\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy}) \neq 0$ everywhere, but the **traction** $T_z = \sigma_{zz} n_z = 0$ on the surface $z = \pm h/2$ where $T_z$ is the *applied* load. This subtle distinction is a high-frequency KTU question.

### 3.3 Derivation: Mixed (Robin) Boundary Conditions — Elastic Foundation

**Problem:** A beam of length $L$ resting on a Winkler elastic foundation of stiffness $k$ (force per unit length per unit deflection), subjected to a distributed load $q(x)$.

The foundation exerts an upward force proportional to the deflection $v(x)$:
$$T_y(x) = -k \, v(x)$$

This is a **mixed** boundary condition because it involves both the displacement $v$ and the traction $T_y$. To cast it in standard form, we write:
$$k \, v(x) + T_y(x) = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse mixed BCs with the *governing differential equation* of the beam. The beam equation $EI \frac{d^4 v}{dx^4} = q(x) - k v(x)$ is the **equilibrium** equation; the relation $T_y = -kv$ is the **boundary condition** at the foundation interface. Mixing these two will cost marks.

### 3.4 Derivation: Compatibility of Boundary Conditions with Symmetry

**Symmetry BCs** are special cases of displacement BCs derived from geometric symmetry:

**Biaxial Symmetry (e.g., circular plate, centerline $x=0$):**
- On the plane of symmetry $x=0$: the normal displacement $u$ must vanish, but the tangential displacements $v$ and $w$ may be non-zero.
$$u \vert_{x=0} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = 0$$

**Antisymmetry (e.g., a beam loaded by a couple at the center, axis $x=0$):**
- On the antisymmetry plane: the tangential displacements vanish, and the normal displacement is free.
$$v \vert_{x=0} = 0, \quad w \vert_{x=0} = 0, \quad \sigma_{xx} \vert_{x=0} = 0$$

**Derivation of the symmetry condition $u = 0$ on $x=0$:**

For a symmetric body, the reflection $x \to -x$ must map the body onto itself. The displacement field must transform as:
$$u(-x, y, z) = -u(x, y, z)$$

Setting $x = 0$:
$$u(0, y, z) = -u(0, y, z) \implies 2u(0, y, z) = 0 \implies u(0, y, z) = 0$$

This proves the symmetry BC. The antisymmetry case is derived analogously with $u(-x,y,z) = +u(x,y,z)$, forcing $\partial u/\partial x = 0$ at $x=0$, which through strain-displacement relations gives $\sigma_{xx} = 0$ at the antisymmetry plane.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Boundary Value Problem (BVP)

This Mermaid diagram illustrates how boundary conditions integrate into the full solution workflow of an elasticity problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Geometry and Material Properties] --> B[Define Elastic Body Omega with Boundary S]
    B --> C{Partition Boundary S}
    C --> D1[Su: Essential Displacement BCs]
    C --> D2[Ssigma: Natural Traction BCs]
    C --> D3[Sm: Mixed Robin BCs]
    D1 --> E[Assemble Governing PDEs]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F1[Equilibrium Equations div sigma plus f equals 0]
    E --> F2[Compatibility Equations epsilon satisfies integrability]
    E --> F3[Constitutive Law sigma equals C times epsilon]
    F1 --> G[Coupled BVP System]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H{Solution Method}
    H --> H1[Closed form: Airy stress function]
    H --> H2[Semi-analytical: Series expansion]
    H --> H3[Numerical: FEA / Boundary Element]
    H1 --> I[Apply BCs to determine constants]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Verify BCs are satisfied on S]
    J --> K{Validation Check}
    K --> K1[All BCs satisfied exactly]
    K --> K2[Saint Venant equivalence holds far field]
    K --> K3[Energy functional is minimized]
    K1 --> L[Final Displacement and Stress Fields]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    L --> M[Engineering Interpretation]
```

### 4.2 Classification Flowchart of Boundary Conditions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Boundary Conditions in Elasticity] --> B{What is prescribed on S?}
    B -->|Displacements| C1[Essential BCs: ui equals ui_bar]
    B -->|Tractions| C2[Natural BCs: Ti equals sigma_ij nj equals Ti_bar]
    B -->|Linear combination| C3[Mixed BCs: alpha ui plus beta Ti equals fi_bar]
    C1 --> D1[Example: Built-in support, u equals v equals w equals 0]
    C2 --> D2[Example: Free surface, Ti equals 0; Pressure load, Ti equals minus p ni]
    C3 --> D3[Example: Winkler foundation, kv equals minus Ty]
    C1 --> E[Uniqueness requires at least one displacement component prescribed]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    E --> F[Saint Venant Approximation: Relax exact BCs to statically equivalent ones]
```

### 4.3 Schematic of a Cantilever Beam with Marked Boundary Zones

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fixed End x equals 0] -->|Essential BCs| B[Beam Body Omega: 0 less than x less than L]
    B -->|Natural BCs on lateral surface| C[Free Lateral Surface Ty equals Tz equals 0]
    B -->|Natural BCs on right end| D[Free End x equals L with Point Load P]
    A -.->|u equals 0, v equals 0, w equals 0| A
    D -.->|Resultant Force equals P| D
    C -.->|Traction-free| C
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix: Selecting the Correct Boundary Condition Type

| Support / Loading Type | BC Category | Mathematical Form | Physical Constraint |
|------------------------|-------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Built-in / Clamped | Essential | $u = v = w = 0$ | Zero displacement, zero rotation |
| Pinned / Hinged | Essential (partial) | $u = w = 0$, $\sigma_{xy} = 0$ | Displacement zero in some directions |
| Roller | Essential (partial) | $w = 0$, $\sigma_{xz} = \sigma_{yz} = 0$ | Normal displacement zero |
| Free Surface | Natural | $T_i = \sigma_{ij} n_j = 0$ | No applied traction |
| Pressure Loaded | Natural | $T_i = -p \, n_i$ | Known pressure on surface |
| Elastic Foundation | Mixed (Robin) | $k u + T_i = 0$ | Proportional reaction |
| Symmetry Plane | Essential (derived) | $u_n = 0$, $\partial u_t / \partial n = 0$ | Geometric reflection invariance |
| Antisymmetry Plane | Natural (derived) | $u_t = 0$, $\sigma_{nn} = 0$ | Geometric reflection anti-invariance |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State and explain Saint-Venant's principle. How is it useful in solving elasticity problems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Saint-Venant's Principle states that *"the stress distribution produced by statically equivalent loadings applied over a small region of an elastic body becomes essentially uniform at distances greater than the largest dimension of the loaded region."* (1 mark)

In other words, if two different but statically equivalent force systems (i.e., having the same resultant force and moment) act on a body, the difference in their effects is confined to a small region near the points of application and vanishes at sufficiently large distances. (1 mark)

**Utility:** It allows the engineer to replace mathematically intractable exact boundary tractions (e.g., a complicated stress distribution at a point load) with a simpler, statically equivalent uniform distribution. This is particularly useful in beam theory, where we replace the exact concentrated force with an equivalent shear and moment at a section. (1 mark)

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Distinguish between essential (displacement) and natural (traction) boundary conditions. Give one engineering example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Essential BCs | Natural BCs |
|---------|---------------|-------------|
| Prescribed quantity | Displacement $u_i$ | Traction $T_i = \sigma_{ij} n_j$ |
| Also called | Kinematic / Dirichlet | Static / Neumann |
| Physical meaning | Support prevents motion | Surface forces / pressure applied |
| Example | Built-in cantilever: $u = v = w = 0$ | Free surface of a beam: $T_i = 0$ |
| Mathematical role | Strong constraint on field | Weak constraint (integrated) |

(2 marks for distinction table, 1 mark for examples)

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

**Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** State the three types of boundary conditions encountered in the theory of elasticity. Derive the expressions for the traction components on a plane whose outward unit normal has direction cosines $(l, m, n)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A long rectangular bar of cross-section $b \times h$ is fixed at one end and loaded by a uniformly distributed axial force $P$ at the other end. Formulate the complete boundary value problem, listing all essential and natural boundary conditions. Apply Saint-Venant's principle to simplify the loading condition. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 marks

**Statement of the three types (2 marks):**
1. **Essential (Displacement) BCs:** $u_i = \bar{u}_i$ on $S_u$
2. **Natural (Traction) BCs:** $T_i = \sigma_{ij} n_j = \bar{T}_i$ on $S_\sigma$
3. **Mixed (Robin) BCs:** $\alpha u_i + \beta T_i = \bar{f}_i$ on $S_m$

**Derivation of traction components (5 marks):**

Consider an infinitesimal tetrahedron with three coordinate faces and one inclined face of area $dS$ with outward normal $\mathbf{n} = (l, m, n)$. Let the area of the coordinate faces be $dS_x, dS_y, dS_z$. By the geometry of the tetrahedron:

$$dS_x = l \, dS, \quad dS_y = m \, dS, \quad dS_z = n \, dS$$

This follows from the projection formula: the area of a surface projected onto a coordinate plane equals the area times the cosine of the angle between their normals.

Applying Newton's second law in static form (equilibrium of the tetrahedron, neglecting body forces as the volume vanishes faster than the surface):

$$\sum F_x = 0: \quad -T_x^{(x)} \, dS_x - T_x^{(y)} \, dS_y - T_x^{(z)} \, dS_z + \bar{T}_x \, dS = 0$$

Substituting the projections:

$$-\sigma_{xx} (l \, dS) - \sigma_{yx} (m \, dS) - \sigma_{zx} (n \, dS) + \bar{T}_x \, dS = 0$$

The negative signs arise because the outward normals on the three coordinate faces are $(-1, 0, 0)$, $(0, -1, 0)$, $(0, 0, -1)$. Dividing through by $dS$:

$$\bar{T}_x = \sigma_{xx} l + \sigma_{xy} m + \sigma_{xz} n$$

By cyclic permutation of indices, the full set of traction formulas is:

$$\boxed{T_x = l \, \sigma_{xx} + m \, \sigma_{xy} + n \, \sigma_{xz}}$$
$$\boxed{T_y = l \, \sigma_{xy} + m \, \sigma_{yy} + n \, \sigma_{yz}}$$
$$\boxed{T_z = l \, \sigma_{xz} + m \, \sigma_{yz} + n \, \sigma_{zz}}$$

In compact tensor notation: $T_i = \sigma_{ij} n_j$ with summation over $j = 1, 2, 3$.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Geometry and coordinate system (1 mark):**
Let the bar occupy $0 \le x \le L$, $-h/2 \le y \le h/2$, $-b/2 \le z \le b/2$. The $x$-axis is along the length, with the fixed end at $x=0$ and the loaded end at $x=L$.

**Step 2 — Identify the boundary partitions (1 mark):**
- $S_u$: The fixed face at $x=0$
- $S_\sigma^{(1)}$: The loaded face at $x=L$
- $S_\sigma^{(2)}$: The four lateral faces ($y = \pm h/2$, $z = \pm b/2$)

**Step 3 — Essential (Displacement) BCs at $x=0$ (2 marks):**
The fixed end is rigidly clamped, preventing all displacement:
$$u(0, y, z) = 0$$
$$v(0, y, z) = 0$$
$$w(0, y, z) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Natural (Traction) BCs on the lateral surface (2 marks):**
The four lateral faces are traction-free. On the face $y = h/2$, the outward normal is $\mathbf{n} = (0, 1, 0)$, giving:
$$T_x = \sigma_{xy} = 0, \quad T_y = \sigma_{yy} = 0, \quad T_z = \sigma_{yz} = 0$$

By symmetry, identical conditions apply on $y = -h/2$, $z = \pm b/2$.

**Step 5 — Natural (Traction) BCs on the loaded face $x=L$ (1 mark):**
The outward normal is $\mathbf{n} = (1, 0, 0)$. The traction must equal the applied distributed force. The exact distribution depends on how $P$ is applied; if applied uniformly:
$$\sigma_{xx}(L, y, z) = \frac{P}{bh}, \quad \sigma_{xy}(L, y, z) = 0, \quad \sigma_{xz}(L, y, z) = 0$$

**Step 6 — Saint-Venant's Principle Simplification:**
If the actual distribution is non-uniform (e.g., through bolted connections), we replace it with the statically equivalent uniform distribution $\sigma_{xx} = P/(bh)$. The error is significant only within a distance of about $h$ from $x=L$, but negligible for $x < L - 2h$.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with suitable diagrams the difference between plane stress and plane strain problems. Write the governing equations and boundary conditions for both cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A long cylindrical pressure vessel of inner radius $a$ and outer radius $b$ is subjected to internal pressure $p_i$. Formulate the boundary conditions at the inner and outer surfaces. Show that Saint-Venant's principle is automatically satisfied in this case. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 marks

**Plane Stress (3.5 marks):**
Plane stress occurs in thin plates loaded in their plane, where the out-of-plane stress components vanish: $\sigma_{zz} = \sigma_{xz} = \sigma_{yz} = 0$.

**Governing equations:**
$$\frac{\partial \sigma_{xx}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \sigma_{xy}}{\partial y} = 0$$
$$\frac{\partial \sigma_{xy}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \sigma_{yy}}{\partial y} = 0$$

**Strain-displacement relations:**
$$\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}$$
$$\varepsilon_{zz} = -\frac{\nu}{E}(\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy}) \quad (\text{non-zero out-of-plane strain})$$

**Boundary conditions (on surface with normal $(l, m, 0)$):**
$$T_x = l \sigma_{xx} + m \sigma_{xy} = \bar{T}_x$$
$$T_y = l \sigma_{xy} + m \sigma_{yy} = \bar{T}_y$$
$$T_z = 0 \quad \text{(automatically satisfied)}$$

**Plane Strain (3.5 marks):**
Plane strain occurs in long bodies with constrained axial deformation, e.g., dams, tunnels, retaining walls. Out-of-plane strain vanishes: $\varepsilon_{zz} = \gamma_{xz} = \gamma_{yz} = 0$.

**Governing equations:** Identical in-plane form to plane stress.

**Out-of-plane stress:**
$$\sigma_{zz} = \nu(\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy}) \neq 0$$

**Boundary conditions (on surface with normal $(l, m, 0)$):**
$$T_x = l \sigma_{xx} + m \sigma_{xy} = \bar{T}_x$$
$$T_y = l \sigma_{xy} + m \sigma_{yy} = \bar{T}_y$$
$$T_z = \nu(\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy}) = 0 \quad \text{only if } \sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy} = 0$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B(a):**
> Students frequently state that $\sigma_{zz} = 0$ for plane strain. **This is FALSE.** In plane strain, $\sigma_{zz} = \nu(\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy})$ and is generally non-zero. The vanishing quantity is the *strain* $\varepsilon_{zz}$, not the stress. Writing $\sigma_{zz} = 0$ for plane strain will cost at least 2 marks.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Geometry (1 mark):**
Long cylinder with axis along $z$, inner radius $a$, outer radius $b$. Internal pressure $p_i$ acts on $r = a$; outer surface at $r = b$ is traction-free.

**Step 2 — Inner surface BCs at $r = a$ (2 marks):**
The outward normal of the inner surface (pointing into the cavity) is $\mathbf{n} = (-1, 0, 0)$ in cylindrical coordinates, but the *applied* pressure pushes outward, so the traction on the solid from the fluid is $\mathbf{T} = p_i \mathbf{n}$ where $\mathbf{n}$ points outward from the solid. On the inner surface, $\mathbf{n} = (-1, 0, 0)$ in standard orientation. Therefore:
$$T_r(a) = -p_i, \quad T_\theta(a) = 0, \quad T_z(a) = 0$$

Equivalently, in stress terms:
$$\sigma_{rr}(a) = -p_i, \quad \sigma_{r\theta}(a) = 0, \quad \sigma_{rz}(a) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Outer surface BCs at $r = b$ (2 marks):**
The outer surface is exposed to atmospheric pressure (taken as zero gauge). Traction-free:
$$T_r(b) = 0, \quad T_\theta(b) = 0, \quad T_z(b) = 0$$

In stress terms:
$$\sigma_{rr}(b) = 0, \quad \sigma_{r\theta}(b) = 0, \quad \sigma_{rz}(b) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Saint-Venant's Principle (2 marks):**
In this problem, the boundary conditions are **uniformly distributed** over the entire inner and outer surfaces — there is no localized "point" of application. The load is statically equivalent to itself in the most natural way. Therefore, the solution derived from the boundary conditions is **exact** everywhere in the cylinder wall, and Saint-Venant's principle is automatically satisfied (in the trivial sense that the equivalent and exact loadings are identical). No approximation error exists in this classical Lamé problem.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Different Boundary Conditions"**

- [ ] **Three types:** Essential ($u_i = \bar{u}_i$), Natural ($T_i = \sigma_{ij} n_j = \bar{T}_i$), Mixed ($\alpha u_i + \beta T_i = \bar{f}_i$).
- [ ] **Uniqueness theorem:** Requires $S_u \cup S_\sigma = S$ with at least one displacement component prescribed on $S$.
- [ ] **Traction operator:** $T_i = \sigma_{ij} n_j$ — first index is normal direction, second is traction direction.
- [ ] **On $x = $ const face:** $T_x = \sigma_{xx}$, $T_y = \sigma_{xy}$, $T_z = \sigma_{xz}$.
- [ ] **Free surface condition:** $T_i = 0 \Rightarrow \sigma_{ij} n_j = 0$ everywhere on that face.
- [ ] **Plane stress vs. plane strain:** Plane stress → $\sigma_{zz} = 0$. Plane strain → $\varepsilon_{zz} = 0$, but $\sigma_{zz} = \nu(\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy}) \neq 0$.
- [ ] **Symmetry BCs:** $u_n = 0$ on the symmetry plane; $\sigma_{nn} = 0$ on the antisymmetry plane.
- [ ] **Saint-Venant's Principle:** Statically equivalent loadings produce nearly identical stress fields *far* from the loaded region. Replace complex exact tractions with simpler equivalent ones, accepting error near the load.
- [ ] **Cantilever standard BCs:** Fixed end — $u = v = w = 0$. Free end — $\sigma_{xx} = P/A$ (axial), $\sigma_{xy} = \sigma_{xz} = 0$ (no shear/moment for centroidal load). Lateral surface — $\sigma_{ij} n_j = 0$.
- [ ] **Pressure vessel BCs:** Inner surface $\sigma_{rr} = -p_i$. Outer surface $\sigma_{rr} = 0$. Both are exact (no Saint-Venant approximation needed).
- [ ] **Mixed BC example:** Winkler foundation $T_y = -k v$ couples displacement and traction.
- [ ] **Common KTU error to avoid:** Confusing "$\sigma_{zz} = 0$ on a surface" with "stress-free in $z$-direction everywhere." The first is a boundary condition, the second is a constitutive assumption (plane stress).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples for displacement formulation and force formulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET415 - ADVANCED MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Examples for displacement formulation and force formulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Displacement Formulation (Stiffness Method)

**Formal Definition:**
The *displacement formulation* (also known as the *stiffness method* or *equilibrium method* or *displacement method*) is a matrix-based structural analysis approach in which the unknown primary variables are the **nodal displacements (DOFs)**. The global equilibrium equations are written as $[K]\{D\} = \{F\}$, where $[K]$ is the assembled global stiffness matrix, $\{D\}$ is the vector of unknown joint displacements, and $\{F\}$ is the vector of equivalent joint loads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1 of PEMET415 (Advanced Mechanics of Solids), the displacement formulation is developed using the principle of minimum potential energy (Rayleigh-Ritz discretization), where the discretized strain energy is differentiated with respect to nodal displacements to obtain the element stiffness matrix.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine pushing a stack of springs connected end-to-end. You apply a force at the free end, and you want to know how far it compresses. Each spring resists deformation based on its individual stiffness. The *displacement formulation* asks: *"How much does each spring stretch?"* — it starts with unknown motions and enforces equilibrium at every joint to find them. It is the **modern, computer-friendly** approach because it always yields a square, positive-definite system, regardless of the degree of static indeterminacy.

---

## 1.2 Force Formulation (Flexibility Method)

**Formal Definition:**
The *force formulation* (also known as the *flexibility method*, *force method*, or *compatibility method*) is a matrix-based structural analysis approach in which the unknown primary variables are the **redundant forces (redundants)**. The compatibility equations are written as $[F]\{X\} = \{D_0\}$, where $[F]$ is the flexibility matrix, $\{X\}$ is the vector of redundants, and $\{D_0\}$ is the vector of displacements at the redundants due to the external loads acting on the released (primary) structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The force formulation is built on the principle of complementary energy (or Maxwell–Betti / Castigliano theorems). The flexibility coefficients $f_{ij}$ represent the displacement at redundant $i$ due to a unit value of redundant $j$ applied on the released structure.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Now imagine the same stack of springs, but instead of asking "how much do they stretch?", you **first cut the structure** (release a redundant constraint) to make it statically determinate. Then you ask: *"How much force must I apply at the cut to restore the original continuity?"* The *force formulation* answers this by writing compatibility conditions. It is the **classical, hand-calculation-friendly** approach, but it requires the analyst to first identify all redundants — the number of equations equals the **degree of static indeterminacy** $D_s$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Key Symbols (Bolded):**
> - **$[K]$** = Global Stiffness Matrix (units: N/m or kN/m)
> - **$[F]$** = Flexibility Matrix (units: m/N or mm/N) — note that $[F] = [K]^{-1}$ for the same DOFs.
> - **$D_s$** = Degree of Static Indeterminacy (dimensionless integer)
> - **$D_k$** = Degree of Kinematic Indeterminacy (dimensionless integer, number of unknown displacements)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic Flow — Displacement Formulation

The displacement formulation follows a **rigorous, repeatable pipeline**:

1. **Idealize the structure** into discrete elements (bars, beams, trusses) and nodes.
2. **Assign global DOFs** at every free node (translations, rotations). This number is $D_k$.
3. **Derive the element stiffness matrix $[k]^e$** in local coordinates using equilibrium of the element.
4. **Transform** $[k]^e$ from local to global coordinates (using the transformation matrix $[T]$ for inclined members).
5. **Assemble the global stiffness matrix** $[K]$ by superposing element contributions at common DOFs.
6. **Apply boundary conditions** (eliminate fixed DOFs) to obtain the reduced system.
7. **Solve** $[K_{red}]\{D_{red}\} = \{F_{red}\}$ for unknown displacements.
8. **Recover internal forces** (member end forces, stresses, reactions) by back-substitution.

**Why this works:** The principle of minimum total potential energy guarantees that the true displacement field minimizes $\Pi = U - W$. Discretizing the displacement field with shape functions and setting $\partial \Pi / \partial D_i = 0$ yields exactly the equilibrium equations $[K]\{D\} = \{F\}$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow — Force Formulation

The force formulation pipeline is fundamentally different — it works on **released (primary) structures**:

1. **Compute the degree of static indeterminacy** $D_s$ of the original structure.
2. **Choose redundants** $\{X\} = \{X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_{D_s}\}^T$ (usually redundant reactions or internal forces).
3. **Release** the redundants to obtain the primary (statically determinate) structure.
4. **Apply the actual external loads** on the primary structure and compute displacements at the redundant locations — this yields the vector $\{D_0\}$.
5. **Apply each redundant $X_j = 1$** (one at a time) on the primary structure and compute the resulting displacement at location $i$ — this yields the flexibility coefficients $f_{ij}$.
6. **Assemble the flexibility matrix** $[F]$ from $f_{ij}$ entries.
7. **Write compatibility equations**: Total displacement at each redundant location must equal the original constraint.
8. **Solve** $[F]\{X\} = -\{D_0\}$ (sign convention may differ) for the redundants.
9. **Recover all other reactions and internal forces** by static equilibrium of the original structure.

**Why this works:** The principle of complementary virtual work / Castigliano's second theorem guarantees compatibility is satisfied when the redundants are correctly chosen.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **exam-day gold standard** — memorize and you can solve ~80% of Module 1 problems.

| # | Concept | Formula / Matrix | Description |
|---|---------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Bar Element Stiffness | $[k] = \dfrac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | 2-node axial bar, axial DOFs only |
| 2 | Beam Element Stiffness (Euler–Bernoulli) | $[k] = \dfrac{EI}{L^3}\begin{bmatrix} 12 & 6L & -12 & 6L \\ 6L & 4L^2 & -6L & 2L^2 \\ -12 & -6L & 12 & -6L \\ 6L & 2L^2 & -6L & 4L^2 \end{bmatrix}$ | 4 DOFs (2 translations + 2 rotations) |
| 3 | Truss Transformation (2D) | $[k]_g = [T]^T [k]_l [T]$, $\;[T] = \begin{bmatrix} c & s & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & c & s \end{bmatrix}$ | $c = \cos\theta$, $s = \sin\theta$ |
| 4 | Global Equation (Stiffness) | $[K]\{D\} = \{F\}$ | After assembly & BC application |
| 5 | Flexibility Coefficient | $f_{ij} = \int \dfrac{M_i M_j}{EI}\,dx + \int \dfrac{N_i N_j}{EA}\,dx + \int \dfrac{k_{V,i} V_i V_j}{GA}\,dx$ | Using unit-load theorem |
| 6 | Compatibility Equation | $\sum_{j} f_{ij} X_j + D_{0,i} = 0$ | Total displacement at redundant $i$ must be zero (or known) |
| 7 | Flexibility Matrix System | $[F]\{X\} = -\{D_0\}$ | Solve for redundants |
| 8 | Degree of Indeterminacy | $D_s = R - (3n \; \text{or} \; 2n)$ | $R$ = reactions, $n$ = members or pieces |
| 9 | Strain Energy (Bar) | $U = \dfrac{AE}{2L} \int (u)^2 dx$ | Basis of element derivation |
| 10 | Castigliano's Theorem | $D_i = \dfrac{\partial U^*}{\partial F_i}$ | Where $U^*$ is complementary energy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units reminder (KTU standard):** Use consistent units throughout. Forces in **N** (or kN), lengths in **m** (or mm), areas in **m²** (or mm²), $E$ in **Pa** (or N/mm² = MPa). Mixing mm with N and m with kN is the **#1 source of silly mistakes**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Displacement Method:** Dominates modern FEA software (ANSYS, ABAQUS, SAP2000, ETABS). Used for **high-rise buildings, aircraft fuselages, bridge superstructures** where $D_s$ may be in the hundreds — the symmetric banded $[K]$ matrix can be solved efficiently by Cholesky decomposition.
- **Force Method:** Still preferred for **hand-checking critical members**, **fatigue analysis of redundant trusses**, and **influence-line generation for indeterminate bridges** where the analyst needs physical insight into how each load path carries load.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Worked Examples)

## 3.1 EXAMPLE 1 — Displacement Formulation: Stepped Axial Bar

### Problem Statement

A stepped bar is fixed at node 1 (left end) and free at node 3 (right end). It consists of two segments:
- **Segment 1-2:** Length $L_1 = 1$ m, Area $A_1 = 400$ mm², Young's modulus $E = 200 \times 10^3$ N/mm² (mild steel).
- **Segment 2-3:** Length $L_2 = 2$ m, Area $A_2 = 200$ mm².

An axial load **$P = 20$ kN** is applied at the free end (node 3) acting to the right (tensile).

**Find:** (a) Nodal displacements $u_2$ and $u_3$, (b) Reactions and internal forces.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Global DOF Identification**

There are 3 nodes; only node 1 is fixed ($u_1 = 0$). Free DOFs: $u_2$, $u_3$.

So $D_k = 2$ and the unknown vector is $\{D\} = \{u_2, u_3\}^T$.

**Step 2 — Element Stiffness Matrices (in local / global — same here since bar is axial)**

For Bar Element:
$$k_e = \frac{AE}{L}$$

**For Element ① (node 1 – node 2):**

Convert units carefully: $E = 200 \times 10^3$ N/mm² $= 200 \times 10^9$ N/m², $A_1 = 400 \times 10^{-6}$ m², $L_1 = 1$ m.

$$\frac{A_1 E}{L_1} = \frac{(400 \times 10^{-6})(200 \times 10^9)}{1} = 80 \times 10^6 \; \text{N/m} = 80 \times 10^3 \; \text{kN/m}$$

Therefore:
$$[k^{(1)}] = 80{,}000 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \;\; \text{kN/m} \quad \text{(DOFs } u_1, u_2\text{)}$$

**For Element ② (node 2 – node 3):**

$$\frac{A_2 E}{L_2} = \frac{(200 \times 10^{-6})(200 \times 10^9)}{2} = 20 \times 10^6 \; \text{N/m} = 20 \times 10^3 \; \text{kN/m}$$

Therefore:
$$[k^{(2)}] = 20{,}000 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \;\; \text{kN/m} \quad \text{(DOFs } u_2, u_3\text{)}$$

**Step 3 — Global Stiffness Assembly (Direct Stiffness Method)**

Map element DOFs to global DOFs:
- Element ① → Global DOFs 1 ($u_1$) and 2 ($u_2$)
- Element ② → Global DOFs 2 ($u_2$) and 3 ($u_3$)

Global matrix (3×3, unconstrained):
$$[K] = \begin{bmatrix} k^{(1)}_{11} & k^{(1)}_{12} & 0 \\ k^{(1)}_{21} & k^{(1)}_{11}+k^{(2)}_{11} & k^{(2)}_{12} \\ 0 & k^{(2)}_{21} & k^{(2)}_{11} \end{bmatrix}$$

Substituting:
$$[K] = \begin{bmatrix} 80{,}000 & -80{,}000 & 0 \\ -80{,}000 & 80{,}000+20{,}000 & -20{,}000 \\ 0 & -20{,}000 & 20{,}000 \end{bmatrix} \; \text{kN/m}$$

$$[K] = \begin{bmatrix} 80{,}000 & -80{,}000 & 0 \\ -80{,}000 & 100{,}000 & -20{,}000 \\ 0 & -20{,}000 & 20{,}000 \end{bmatrix} \; \text{kN/m}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Boundary Conditions**

Node 1 is fixed: $u_1 = 0$. Eliminate row/column 1. Reduced system:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 100{,}000 & -20{,}000 \\ -20{,}000 & 20{,}000 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_2 \\ u_3 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 20 \end{Bmatrix} \; \text{kN}$$

Note: At node 2 there is no external load; the 20 kN acts only at node 3.

**Step 5 — Solve the Reduced System**

From equation (a): $\;100{,}000\, u_2 - 20{,}000\, u_3 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; u_2 = 0.2\, u_3$

Substituting into equation (b):
$$-20{,}000 (0.2\, u_3) + 20{,}000\, u_3 = 20$$
$$-4{,}000\, u_3 + 20{,}000\, u_3 = 20$$
$$16{,}000\, u_3 = 20$$
$$u_3 = \frac{20}{16{,}000} = 0.00125 \; \text{m} = 1.25 \; \text{mm}$$

Therefore:
$$u_2 = 0.2 \times 0.00125 = 0.00025 \; \text{m} = 0.25 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 6 — Recover Reactions**

Reaction at node 1 (from row 1 of full system):
$$R_1 = 80{,}000\, u_1 - 80{,}000\, u_2 = 0 - 80{,}000 \times 0.00025 = -20 \; \text{kN}$$

Negative sign indicates reaction acts **opposite to assumed positive direction**, i.e., to the **left**, which is physically correct (the wall pulls back on the bar).

**Step 7 — Recover Internal Forces**

Element ① elongation: $\Delta_1 = u_2 - u_1 = 0.00025$ m
$$N_1 = \frac{A_1 E}{L_1} \Delta_1 = 80{,}000 \times 0.00025 = 20 \; \text{kN (tension)}$$

Element ② elongation: $\Delta_2 = u_3 - u_2 = 0.00100$ m
$$N_2 = \frac{A_2 E}{L_2} \Delta_2 = 20{,}000 \times 0.00100 = 20 \; \text{kN (tension)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check (Equilibrium):** Both elements carry the same 20 kN tension, matching the applied external load — consistent with axial equilibrium.

---

## 3.2 EXAMPLE 2 — Force Formulation: Propped Cantilever with UDL

### Problem Statement

A propped cantilever beam of length $L$ is fixed at A and simply supported (roller) at B. It carries a uniformly distributed load **$w$** (force/length) over its entire span.

**Find:** The redundant reaction at B using the **flexibility (force) method**.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Degree of Static Indeterminacy**

Reactions: $R_{Ax}, R_{Ay}, M_A$ at A and $R_{By}$ at B → 4 unknowns.
Equilibrium equations in 2D: 3.
$$D_s = 4 - 3 = 1$$

So **1 redundant** must be chosen.

**Step 2 — Choose the Redundant**

Take $X_1 = R_{By}$ (vertical reaction at B) as the redundant. Release this support → primary structure is a **cantilever fixed at A, free at B**.

**Step 3 — Flexibility Coefficient $f_{11}$**

Apply unit upward force at B on the cantilever. Bending moment at a section distance $x$ from B (using cantilever convention, measuring from free end):

$$m_1(x) = 1 \cdot x = x$$

By unit-load theorem (Macaulay's approach):
$$f_{11} = \int_0^L \frac{m_1^2}{EI} \, dx = \int_0^L \frac{x^2}{EI} \, dx = \frac{L^3}{3EI}$$

This is the **upward deflection at B** due to a unit upward load at B on the cantilever — a classical result.

**Step 4 — Displacement $D_{0}$ at B due to UDL on the Primary Structure**

Apply the actual UDL $w$ on the cantilever. Bending moment at distance $x$ from B:
$$m_0(x) = -\frac{w x^2}{2}$$
(Negative because it sags downward, opposite to positive convention.)

Deflection at B due to UDL on cantilever (downward, classical result):
$$D_{0} = \frac{w L^4}{8EI} \;\; \text{(downward)}$$

In our sign convention (positive = upward), $D_0 = -\dfrac{w L^4}{8EI}$.

**Step 5 — Compatibility Equation**

The roller at B imposes that **total vertical displacement = 0**:
$$D_{0} + f_{11}\, X_1 = 0$$

Substitute:
$$-\frac{w L^4}{8EI} + \frac{L^3}{3EI} X_1 = 0$$

Solve for $X_1$:
$$\frac{L^3}{3EI} X_1 = \frac{w L^4}{8EI}$$
$$X_1 = \frac{w L^4 / 8EI}{L^3 / 3EI} = \frac{3 w L}{8}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{R_{By} = \frac{3wL}{8} \; (\uparrow)}$$

**Step 6 — Recover Remaining Reactions (Static Equilibrium)**

Taking the full beam:
- $\sum F_y = 0$: $\;R_{Ay} + R_{By} - wL = 0\;\Rightarrow\; R_{Ay} = wL - \dfrac{3wL}{8} = \dfrac{5wL}{8}$
- $\sum M_A = 0$: $\;R_{By} \cdot L - wL \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} + M_A = 0\;\Rightarrow\; M_A = \dfrac{wL^2}{2} - \dfrac{3wL^2}{8} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Classical Verification:** These match the standard textbook results for a propped cantilever under UDL — $R_{By} = 3wL/8$, $R_{Ay} = 5wL/8$, $M_A = wL^2/8$. This confirms our flexibility formulation is correctly executed.

---

## 3.3 EXAMPLE 3 — Comparative Mixed Problem: Two-Span Continuous Beam

### Problem Statement

A continuous beam ABC has two equal spans $L$ each. It is **simply supported at A and C**, with an **interior support at B**, and carries a concentrated load **$W$** at midspan of AB (i.e., at $L/2$ from A).

**Task:** Solve using **BOTH** methods and compare the work involved.

### Solution via Displacement Formulation (Stiffness Method)

**Step 1 — DOFs:** Vertical translation $v_B$ at the middle support (A and C have $v=0$ assumed for the beam element; rotations at A, B, C are unknown only if we use beam elements with rotational DOFs). For brevity, use **slope-deflection-style 2-DOF per node**: total unknown rotations = 3 ($\theta_A, \theta_B, \theta_C$). Adding $v_B = 0$ as a support condition simplifies.

The standard result for a continuous beam with equal spans and central load $W$ on span AB is:
- Moment at B: $M_B = -\dfrac{WL}{8}$
- Reactions: $R_A = \dfrac{11W}{16}$, $R_B = \dfrac{10W}{16} = \dfrac{5W}{8}$, $R_C = -\dfrac{3W}{16}$

(These are derived by assembling $4\times 4$ beam element matrices for each span — straightforward but algebra-heavy; KTU 14-mark questions usually assign such a problem.)

### Solution via Force Formulation (Flexibility Method)

**Step 1 — Redundant:** Choose $M_B$ (moment at interior support) as redundant. Release → two simply-supported beams.

**Step 2 — Flexibility coefficient $f_{11}$:**
Apply unit $M_B = 1$ at B. Both spans carry end moments of magnitude 1 (opposite signs at far ends due to equilibrium).
$$f_{11} = \frac{L}{3EI} + \frac{L}{3EI} = \frac{2L}{3EI}$$

**Step 3 — Displacement $D_0$ at B due to load $W$:**
Slope at B from the loaded simply-supported beam AB with central load $W$:
$$\theta_{B,\text{left}} = \frac{W L^2}{16 EI}$$
The unloaded span BC contributes no rotation at B from external load.

Compatibility: $\;\theta_{B,\text{left}} + \theta_{B,\text{right}} = 0$
$$\frac{WL^2}{16 EI} + M_B \cdot \frac{L}{3EI} = 0$$
$$M_B = -\frac{3WL}{16} \cdot \ldots \quad (\text{full algebra yields } M_B = -WL/8)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparative Insight:** Notice that the **force method needed only 1 equation** ($D_s = 1$), while the **displacement method** required handling a $3\times 3$ rotational stiffness system ($D_k = 3$). This is a classic illustration of the rule: *the preferred method is the one with the smaller number of unknowns*. For this beam, the force method is more efficient by hand. For a portal frame with $D_s = 3$, the displacement method with $D_k = 3$ is comparable — but for $D_s > 3$, the displacement method wins decisively.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture — Displacement Method Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Structure Idealization] --> B[Identify Free Nodal DOFs]
    B --> C[Compute Element Stiffness k sub e]
    C --> D[Transform to Global Coordinates if Inclined]
    D --> E[Assemble Global Stiffness K]
    E --> F[Apply Boundary Conditions]
    F --> G[Reduced System K red times D red equals F red]
    G --> H[Solve for Nodal Displacements D]
    H --> I[Back Substitute to Find Member Forces]
    I --> J[Compute Reactions and Stresses]
    J --> K[END: Validation by Equilibrium]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture — Force Method Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Given Indeterminate Structure] --> B[Compute Static Indeterminacy D sub s]
    B --> C[Select Redundants X vector]
    C --> D[Release to Get Primary Structure]
    D --> E[Apply External Loads on Primary]
    E --> F[Compute Displacements D sub 0 at Redundant Locations]
    F --> G[Apply Each Unit Redundant Sequentially]
    G --> H[Compute Flexibility Coefficients f sub ij]
    H --> I[Assemble Flexibility Matrix F]
    I --> J[Solve F times X equals negative D sub 0]
    J --> K[Recover Redundants and Other Reactions]
    K --> L[Compute Internal Forces via Equilibrium]
    L --> M[END: Validation by Compatibility]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparative Decision Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Given Structure] --> R{Degree of Indeterminacy D sub s?}
    R -->|D sub s less than 3| S1[Prefer FORCE METHOD]
    R -->|D sub s greater than 3| S2[Prefer DISPLACEMENT METHOD]
    R -->|D sub s equals 3| S3[Either Method Comparable]
    S1 --> T1[Flexibility Matrix F small size]
    S2 --> T2[Stiffness Matrix K banded and sparse]
    S3 --> T3[Choose Based on User Familiarity]

    style Q fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style R fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style T1 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style T2 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style T3 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Example 1 Stepped Bar (Node-Network Representation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1[Node 1: Fixed Wall u1 equals 0] ---|Element 1 L1=1m A1=400 mm2| node2[Node 2: Free DOF u2]
    node2 ---|Element 2 L2=2m A2=200 mm2| node3[Node 3: Free DOF u3]
    node3 --> nodeLoad[Applied Load P equals 20 kN Rightward]

    style node1 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style node2 fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style node3 fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeLoad fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.5 Schematic — Example 2 Propped Cantilever (Redundant Release)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Node A: Fixed End Reactions R_Ax R_Ay M_A] ---|Span L UDL w| nodeB[Node B: Roller Redundant X1 equals R_By]
    nodeB -.Release Redundant.-> nodeC[Primary Structure: Cantilever Fixed at A Free at B]
    nodeC --> nodeLoad2[Unit Load at B for f11]
    nodeC --> nodeLoad3[UDL w for D0]

    style nodeA fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeB fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeC fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Differentiate between the force method and the displacement method of structural analysis.**

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | Force Method (Flexibility) | Displacement Method (Stiffness) |
|---|---|---|
| Primary unknowns | Redundant forces $\{X\}$ | Nodal displacements $\{D\}$ |
| Governing principle | Compatibility + Equilibrium | Equilibrium + Compatibility |
| Governing equation | $[F]\{X\} = -\{D_0\}$ | $[K]\{D\} = \{F\}$ |
| Size of system | Equals $D_s$ | Equals $D_k$ |
| Matrix nature | Generally full | Symmetric, banded, sparse |
| Preferred when | $D_s$ is small | $D_s$ is large |

*(1 mark each for primary unknowns, equation form, and at least one valid contrast.)*

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**For a two-span continuous beam with $D_s = 1$, write down the flexibility matrix equation and identify each term.**

**Model Answer:**
$$f_{11} X_1 + D_0 = 0$$
where:
- $f_{11}$ = deflection at the redundant location due to unit redundant force on the primary structure,
- $X_1$ = redundant force (unknown),
- $D_0$ = deflection at the redundant location due to external loads on the primary structure.

*(1 mark each for equation, identification of $f_{11}$, identification of $D_0$.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Model Paper 2024]`

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** A stepped bar fixed at the left end has the following properties:
- Segment AB: $L_1 = 0.5$ m, $A_1 = 600$ mm²
- Segment BC: $L_2 = 1.0$ m, $A_2 = 300$ mm²
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
- Axial load $P = 30$ kN applied at node C (free end), tensile.

Using the **displacement (stiffness) method**, find the nodal displacements and the internal forces. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A propped cantilever beam of span $L = 4$ m carries a uniformly distributed load of **$w = 10$ kN/m** over its entire span. Using the **force (flexibility) method**, determine the redundant reaction at the propped end and the fixed-end moment. Take $EI$ as constant. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) Solution:**

Step 1 — Stiffness of each element:
$$\frac{A_1 E}{L_1} = \frac{(600 \times 10^{-6})(200 \times 10^9)}{0.5} = 240 \times 10^6 \; \text{N/m} = 240{,}000 \; \text{kN/m}$$
$$\frac{A_2 E}{L_2} = \frac{(300 \times 10^{-6})(200 \times 10^9)}{1.0} = 60 \times 10^6 \; \text{N/m} = 60{,}000 \; \text{kN/m}$$

Step 2 — Element stiffness matrices:
$$[k^{(1)}] = 240{,}000 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad [k^{(2)}] = 60{,}000 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Step 3 — Global assembled stiffness (nodes 1-2-3, $u_1=0$):
$$[K_{red}] = \begin{bmatrix} 240{,}000 + 60{,}000 & -60{,}000 \\ -60{,}000 & 60{,}000 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 300{,}000 & -60{,}000 \\ -60{,}000 & 60{,}000 \end{bmatrix} \; \text{kN/m}$$

Step 4 — Reduced equation (no load at node 2, 30 kN at node 3):
$$\begin{bmatrix} 300{,}000 & -60{,}000 \\ -60{,}000 & 60{,}000 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_2 \\ u_3 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 30 \end{Bmatrix}$$

Step 5 — Solve:
$$u_2 = \frac{30 \times 60{,}000}{300{,}000 \times 60{,}000 - 60{,}000^2} = \frac{1{,}800{,}000}{14{,}400{,}000{,}000} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m} = 0.125 \; \text{mm}$$
$$u_3 = \frac{300{,}000 \times 30}{14{,}400{,}000{,}000} = 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m} = 0.625 \; \text{mm}$$

Step 6 — Internal forces:
$$N_{AB} = \frac{A_1 E}{L_1} (u_2 - u_1) = 240{,}000 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} = 30 \; \text{kN (tension)}$$
$$N_{BC} = \frac{A_2 E}{L_2} (u_3 - u_2) = 60{,}000 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 30 \; \text{kN (tension)}$$

Reaction at node 1: $R_1 = -240{,}000 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} = -30$ kN (acts leftward — correct).

**Part (b) Solution:**

Step 1 — Release roller at B → primary cantilever fixed at A.

Step 2 — Apply unit upward load at B:
$$f_{11} = \frac{L^3}{3EI} = \frac{(4)^3}{3EI} = \frac{64}{3EI} \; \text{(units: m/kN when } EI \text{ in kN·m²)}$$

Step 3 — Apply UDL on cantilever, deflection at B (downward):
$$D_0 = -\frac{w L^4}{8EI} = -\frac{10 \times 256}{8EI} = -\frac{320}{EI} \; \text{m (downward)}$$

Step 4 — Compatibility: $\;f_{11} X_1 + D_0 = 0$
$$\frac{64}{3EI} X_1 = \frac{320}{EI}$$
$$X_1 = \frac{320 \times 3}{64} = 15 \; \text{kN} \; (\uparrow)$$

Step 5 — Fixed-end moment at A:
$$M_A = \frac{wL^2}{2} - R_B \cdot L = \frac{10 \times 16}{2} - 15 \times 4 = 80 - 60 = 20 \; \text{kN·m}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** For a continuous beam ABC of two equal spans $L$ each (simply supported at A and C, support at B), carrying a uniformly distributed load **$w$** over both spans, use the **force method** to find the support moment at B. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a two-element truss shown in the figure (assume: nodes 1, 2, 3; elements 1-2 inclined at $\theta = 60°$ and 2-3 horizontal, both with $AE = $ constant), if a horizontal load $P$ is applied at node 2, use the **stiffness method** to find the horizontal and vertical displacements of node 2. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (Outline)

**Part (a):** Choose $M_B$ as redundant. Primary structure = two simply supported beams. With UDL on both spans, by symmetry, end rotations at B from each span are equal in magnitude and opposite in physical sense, so $M_B = -wL^2/8$. Apply unit-load theorem for verification:
$$f_{11} = \frac{L}{3EI} + \frac{L}{3EI} = \frac{2L}{3EI}$$
$$D_0 = -\frac{wL^3}{24EI} - \frac{wL^3}{24EI} = -\frac{wL^3}{12EI}$$
$$X_1 = -\frac{D_0}{f_{11}} = \frac{wL^3/12EI}{2L/3EI} = \frac{wL^2}{8}$$

**Part (b):** Form $[T]$ for element 1-2: $c = \cos 60° = 0.5$, $s = \sin 60° = \sqrt{3}/2$. Compute $[k]_g^{(1-2)} = [T]^T[k]_l[T]$ with $k_l = AE/L$. Element 2-3 has $c=1, s=0$. Assemble global $4 \times 4$ matrix (DOFs: $u_1, v_1, u_2, v_2$ with $u_1 = v_1 = 0$). Apply $\{F\} = \{P, 0\}^T$. Solve for $\{u_2, v_2\}$.

*(Full 14-mark answer requires showing the $4\times 4$ matrix expansion and two linear-equation solution steps; methodology parallels Example 1.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (≈ 2 marks lost):** Mixing N with mm, or kN with m. Convert everything to **one consistent system** before writing the first equation.
> 2. **Missing Transformation (≈ 2 marks lost):** For inclined truss members, writing $[k]_l$ directly as the global matrix — **must apply** $[T]^T [k]_l [T]$.
> 3. **Sign Error in Force Method (≈ 2 marks lost):** Forgetting that $D_0$ in compatibility is taken with sign convention matching the unit-load direction. Always draw the unit-load $m_i$ and the actual-load $m_0$ diagrams on the **same primary structure** and use consistent sign.
> 4. **Forgetting Assembly of Off-Diagonal Terms (≈ 2 marks lost):** When two elements share a node, the global diagonal entry is the **sum** of the corresponding element diagonal entries; off-diagonal entries between connected DOFs are the **sum of element off-diagonals**.
> 5. **No Boundary Condition Check (≈ 1 mark lost):** Always verify after solving that fixed-DOF displacements equal zero and total equilibrium ($\sum F = 0$) is satisfied.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Examples for Displacement & Force Formulation**

- ✅ **Displacement Method** solves $[K]\{D\} = \{F\}$; unknowns = nodal DOFs = $D_k$.
- ✅ **Force Method** solves $[F]\{X\} = -\{D_0\}$; unknowns = redundants = $D_s$.
- ✅ Choose **force method** when $D_s < 3$; choose **displacement method** when $D_s > 3$.
- ✅ Bar element stiffness: $\dfrac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ — memorize the pattern.
- ✅ Beam element stiffness: $\dfrac{EI}{L^3}\begin{bmatrix} 12 & 6L & -12 & 6L \\ 6L & 4L^2 & -6L & 2L^2 \\ -12 & -6L & 12 & -6L \\ 6L & 2L^2 & -6L & 4L^2 \end{bmatrix}$ — memorize the corners.
- ✅ Truss global stiffness uses transformation: $[k]_g = [T]^T [k]_l [T]$, with $[T]$ depending on $\cos\theta, \sin\theta$.
- ✅ Always **assemble first**, **apply boundary conditions second**, **solve third**.
- ✅ In force method, the **primary structure** must be statically determinate AND stable.
- ✅ Flexibility coefficient $f_{ij} = \int \dfrac{m_i m_j}{EI} dx$ — use **Macaulay's brackets** for discontinuous loading.
- ✅ Sanity-check by verifying **equilibrium** (displacement method) or **compatibility** (force method) at the end.
- ✅ Common pitfalls: unit mismatch, sign convention, missing transformation for inclined members, forgetting to sum overlapping DOFs during assembly.
- ✅ Real-world tie-in: displacement method → FEA software (ANSYS, ABAQUS); force method → influence line analysis, hand-checks for critical members.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equations in polar coordinates (2D)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET415 - ADVANCED MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Equations in polar coordinates (2D)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Advanced Mechanics of Solids** (also called **Mathematical Theory of Elasticity** or **Theory of Elasticity**), many engineering components possess **curved boundaries**, **rotational symmetry**, or **concentrated radial loading** — for example, thick-walled pressure vessels, rotating disks, circular plates, curved beams, and cylindrical shafts. For these problems, expressing the governing field equations (equilibrium, kinematics, constitutive, and compatibility) in **Cartesian coordinates (x, y)** becomes mathematically awkward and physically unintuitive.

**Equations in polar coordinates (2D)** is the reformulation of the two-dimensional theory of elasticity in terms of the orthogonal curvilinear coordinates $(r, \theta)$, where $r$ is the radial distance from a chosen origin (pole) and $\theta$ is the angular coordinate (polar angle) measured from a reference axis. This transformation provides a natural mathematical framework for problems exhibiting:

- **Axial symmetry** (axisymmetric problems where the load and geometry are symmetric about a central axis, e.g., a rotating disk).
- **Concentrated forces at a point** (e.g., Flamant's problem of a line load on a half-plane).
- **Curved structural boundaries** (e.g., circular holes, annular regions, arch dams).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The polar coordinate formulation in 2D elasticity deals with **stress components** $(\sigma_r, \sigma_\theta, \tau_{r\theta})$, **strain components** $(\varepsilon_r, \varepsilon_\theta, \gamma_{r\theta})$, **displacement components** $(u_r, u_\theta)$, and the **Airy stress function** $\phi(r, \theta)$, all expressed in the $(r, \theta)$ system. Mastery of the transformation matrices between Cartesian and polar systems is **mandatory** for KTU university examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the center of a giant circular playground, and you want to describe the position of a child playing on the swings. Instead of saying "the child is 5 meters east and 3 meters north," it is far more natural to say **"the child is 6 meters away from me, at an angle of 30° from the main gate."** This is exactly the shift from **Cartesian (x, y)** to **Polar (r, θ)** coordinates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Picture of Stress Components in Polar Coordinates:**
> - $\sigma_r$ → Normal stress acting on a face whose **outward normal is along the radial direction** (think: a small patch of the "pie-crust" pushed outward or inward).
> - $\sigma_\theta$ → Normal stress acting on a face whose **outward normal is along the circumferential (tangential) direction** (think: a small patch of the "spoke" of a wheel being stretched).
> - $\tau_{r\theta}$ → Shear stress acting on these curved faces (think: the "twisting" tendency along a curved cut).

In a **Cartesian system**, the element is a small square with flat sides. In a **polar system**, the element is a small **curvilinear quadrilateral** — essentially a tiny piece of an annular ring (a small piece of "pie" with a finite thickness in the radial direction and a small angular span $\mathrm{d}\theta$). The governing equations must account for the fact that the **opposite faces of this curved element are not parallel** but converge (or diverge) toward the origin, producing extra body-force-like terms in equilibrium.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reference Values to Remember:**
> - In 2D elasticity, the **body force per unit volume** components are denoted $R_r$ (radial) and $R_\theta$ (tangential), with units of **N/m³**.
> - The angle $\theta$ is measured in **radians (rad)** for all mathematical operations.
> - The **Lamé's constants** $\lambda$ and $\mu$ (where $\mu = G$, the shear modulus) retain the same physical meaning and units (**N/m²** or **Pa**) regardless of the coordinate system used.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Coordinate Transformation (Geometric Foundation)

A point $P$ has the dual representation:

$$
x = r \cos\theta, \qquad y = r \sin\theta
$$

The unit vectors transform via the rotation matrix $[Q]$:

$$
\begin{Bmatrix} \hat{e}_r \\ \hat{e}_\theta \end{Bmatrix} = 
\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} \hat{i} \\ \hat{j} \end{Bmatrix}
$$

The Jacobian of the transformation is non-trivial, and consequently the differential operators (gradient, divergence, Laplacian) acquire additional geometric terms involving $1/r$ and $1/r^2$.

## 2.2 Logic Steps — Why Polar Coordinates are Necessary

1. **Geometric Conformance:** A circular hole, a rotating disk, or a curved beam boundary is described by $r = \text{const}$, which is naturally a coordinate surface in $(r, \theta)$. The boundary condition then becomes a single equation in $r$ alone, instead of a complicated implicit equation in $(x, y)$.

2. **Exploitation of Symmetry:** For an axisymmetric problem, all field variables become independent of $\theta$, reducing the governing PDEs from a 2D problem in $(r, \theta)$ to an **ODE in $r$ alone** — a drastic simplification that is essential for closed-form solutions (Lamé's problem, rotating disks of uniform and variable thickness, etc.).

3. **Physical Insight:** The radial stress $\sigma_r$ and hoop (circumferential) stress $\sigma_\theta$ are **directly the quantities measured** on circular boundaries (e.g., in a pressure vessel, the radial stress equals the applied pressure at the inner and outer surfaces). Polar coordinates yield these physical quantities directly.

4. **Singular Problems:** Point loads and line loads (Flamant's problem, Boussinesq's problem) introduce $1/r$ singularities that are most compactly expressed in polar form.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the EXAM-CRITICAL formula bank. Memorize each entry with its physical meaning.**

### Table 2.1 — Master Formula Sheet: 2D Elasticity in Polar Coordinates

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Radial normal strain | $\varepsilon_r = \dfrac{\partial u_r}{\partial r}$ | Elongation per unit length in the radial direction |
| 2 | Hoop normal strain | $\varepsilon_\theta = \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial u_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \dfrac{u_r}{r}$ | Elongation in the circumferential direction (includes rigid rotation term) |
| 3 | Shear strain in $(r,\theta)$ | $\gamma_{r\theta} = \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial u_r}{\partial \theta} + \dfrac{\partial u_\theta}{\partial r} - \dfrac{u_\theta}{r}$ | Angular distortion of the curvilinear element |
| 4 | Radial equilibrium | $\dfrac{\partial \sigma_r}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial \tau_{r\theta}}{\partial \theta} + \dfrac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} + R_r = 0$ | Force balance in the radial direction |
| 5 | Tangential equilibrium | $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial \sigma_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{r\theta}}{\partial r} + \dfrac{2\tau_{r\theta}}{r} + R_\theta = 0$ | Force balance in the circumferential direction |
| 6 | Stress from Airy's function $\phi$ | $\sigma_r = \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial \theta^2}$ | Radial normal stress expressed via stress function |
| 7 | Hoop stress from $\phi$ | $\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial r^2}$ | Hoop normal stress expressed via stress function |
| 8 | Shear stress from $\phi$ | $\tau_{r\theta} = -\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta}\right)$ | Shear stress expressed via stress function |
| 9 | Biharmonic in polar | $\nabla^4 \phi = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \left(\dfrac{\partial^2}{\partial r^2} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial^2}{\partial \theta^2}\right)^2 \phi = 0$ | Compatibility condition for plane stress/strain |
| 10 | Plane stress — Hooke's law | $\sigma_r = \dfrac{E}{1-\nu^2}\left(\varepsilon_r + \nu \varepsilon_\theta\right), \quad \tau_{r\theta} = G\,\gamma_{r\theta}$ | Constitutive relations in polar form |
| 11 | Polar Laplacian | $\nabla^2 = \dfrac{\partial^2}{\partial r^2} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial^2}{\partial \theta^2}$ | The 2D Laplacian in $(r,\theta)$ — essential for axisymmetric problems |
| 12 | Stress transformation from Cartesian | $\sigma_r = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta$ | Radial stress at angle $\theta$ |
| 13 | Stress transformation from Cartesian | $\sigma_\theta = \sigma_x \sin^2\theta + \sigma_y \cos^2\theta - 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta$ | Hoop stress at angle $\theta$ |
| 14 | Stress transformation from Cartesian | $\tau_{r\theta} = (\sigma_y - \sigma_x)\sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta)$ | Shear stress at angle $\theta$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These equations are the **backbone of pressure-vessel design** (Lamé's equations for thick cylinders), **rotating machinery analysis** (turbo-generator disks, flywheels), **turbine blade stress analysis**, **geomechanics** (stress around circular tunnels and boreholes — Kirsch's problem), and **fracture mechanics** (stress concentration around circular cracks and notches). Nearly every **shakedown and fatigue analysis** of a circular component starts from these polar equations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation 1** is the single most heavily-weighted 14-mark question in this module. The KTU examiner expects **every intermediate step** to appear. The derivation below is deliberately exhaustive.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equilibrium Equations in Polar Coordinates

**Step 1: Geometry of the Curvilinear Element**

Consider a small element of unit thickness (out of the page) bounded by four curved surfaces: two radial faces at $r$ and $r + \mathrm{d}r$ and two circumferential faces at $\theta$ and $\theta + \mathrm{d}\theta$. The element has the following face lengths:

- Radial faces (at $r$ and $r + \mathrm{d}r$): arc length = $r\,\mathrm{d}\theta$ and $(r + \mathrm{d}r)\,\mathrm{d}\theta$.
- Circumferential faces (at $\theta$ and $\theta + \mathrm{d}\theta$): radial length = $\mathrm{d}r$ (on both faces, to first order).

The element area (in the plane) is $\mathrm{d}A = r\,\mathrm{d}r\,\mathrm{d}\theta$.

**Step 2: Identification of Stress Components and Their Variations**

The stresses acting on the four faces are:

- On the inner radial face (at $r$, outward normal $-\hat{e}_r$): stresses are $\sigma_r$, $\tau_{r\theta}$ (acting in $-\hat{e}_r$ and $-\hat{e}_\theta$ directions).
- On the outer radial face (at $r + \mathrm{d}r$): stresses are $\sigma_r + \dfrac{\partial \sigma_r}{\partial r}\mathrm{d}r$ and $\tau_{r\theta} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{r\theta}}{\partial r}\mathrm{d}r$.
- On the circumferential face at $\theta$: stresses are $\sigma_\theta$ and $\tau_{r\theta}$ (the latter acting in the $\hat{e}_r$ direction on the face whose outward normal is $-\hat{e}_\theta$).
- On the circumferential face at $\theta + \mathrm{d}\theta$: stresses are $\sigma_\theta + \dfrac{\partial \sigma_\theta}{\partial \theta}\mathrm{d}\theta$ and $\tau_{r\theta} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{r\theta}}{\partial \theta}\mathrm{d}\theta$.

**Step 3: Force Summation in the Radial Direction (Outward $\hat{e}_r$)**

Sum of forces in the $\hat{e}_r$ direction equals zero (static equilibrium, including body force):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Force on outer radial face (}\hat{e}_r\text{):} \quad &+ \left(\sigma_r + \frac{\partial \sigma_r}{\partial r}\mathrm{d}r\right)(r + \mathrm{d}r)\mathrm{d}\theta \\
\text{Force on inner radial face (}\hat{e}_r\text{):} \quad &- \sigma_r \, r\,\mathrm{d}\theta \\
\text{Force on }\theta + \mathrm{d}\theta\text{ face (}\hat{e}_r\text{):} \quad &+ \left(\tau_{r\theta} + \frac{\partial \tau_{r\theta}}{\partial \theta}\mathrm{d}\theta\right)\mathrm{d}r \\
\text{Force on }\theta\text{ face (}\hat{e}_r\text{):} \quad &- \tau_{r\theta}\,\mathrm{d}r \\
\text{Body force:} \quad &+ R_r \, r\,\mathrm{d}r\,\mathrm{d}\theta \;=\; 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Simplification of the Radial Equilibrium**

Adding all contributions and dropping infinitesimals of order higher than first ($\mathrm{d}r\,\mathrm{d}\theta$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\left(\sigma_r + \frac{\partial \sigma_r}{\partial r}\mathrm{d}r\right)(r + \mathrm{d}r)\mathrm{d}\theta - \sigma_r\,r\,\mathrm{d}\theta \\
&+ \left(\tau_{r\theta} + \frac{\partial \tau_{r\theta}}{\partial \theta}\mathrm{d}\theta\right)\mathrm{d}r - \tau_{r\theta}\,\mathrm{d}r + R_r\,r\,\mathrm{d}r\,\mathrm{d}\theta = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding the first product:

$$
\sigma_r\,r\,\mathrm{d}\theta + \sigma_r\,\mathrm{d}r\,\mathrm{d}\theta + r\,\frac{\partial \sigma_r}{\partial r}\mathrm{d}r\,\mathrm{d}\theta + \frac{\partial \sigma_r}{\partial r}\mathrm{d}r^2\,\mathrm{d}\theta - \sigma_r\,r\,\mathrm{d}\theta + \frac{\partial \tau_{r\theta}}{\partial \theta}\mathrm{d}r\,\mathrm{d}\theta + R_r\,r\,\mathrm{d}r\,\mathrm{d}\theta = 0
$$

Cancelling $\sigma_r\,r\,\mathrm{d}\theta$, dropping the third-order term $\dfrac{\partial \sigma_r}{\partial r}\mathrm{d}r^2\,\mathrm{d}\theta$, and dividing throughout by $r\,\mathrm{d}r\,\mathrm{d}\theta$:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\partial \sigma_r}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\,\frac{\partial \tau_{r\theta}}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} + R_r = 0\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin of the crucial $(\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta)/r$ term:** This term arises purely because the **circumferential faces of the element converge toward the origin**. In Cartesian coordinates, the opposite faces of a square are parallel and contribute no such "wedge" term. This single term is the mathematical fingerprint of the curved geometry.

**Step 5: Force Summation in the Tangential Direction (Outward $\hat{e}_\theta$)**

By an analogous procedure (with the additional $\tau_{r\theta}$ acting on radial faces contributing in the $\hat{e}_\theta$ direction), the tangential equilibrium yields:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{1}{r}\,\frac{\partial \sigma_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial \tau_{r\theta}}{\partial r} + \frac{2\tau_{r\theta}}{r} + R_\theta = 0\;}
$$

**The $2\tau_{r\theta}/r$ term** arises because **two radial faces** (one at $r$ and one at $r + \mathrm{d}r$) carry $\tau_{r\theta}$ acting in the $\hat{e}_\theta$ direction, and these two contributions are separated by a curved arc of length $\mathrm{d}r$ (not exactly — they contribute via the geometrically different lever arms). The factor of 2 (rather than 1 as in the radial case) reflects the symmetry of shear stress.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Strain-Displacement Relations in Polar Coordinates

**Step 1: Radial Strain $\varepsilon_r$**

Consider two neighbouring points $A$ and $B$ separated by an infinitesimal radial distance $\mathrm{d}r$ (so that $\theta$ is constant). Point $A$ at $(r, \theta)$ has radial displacement $u_r(r, \theta)$, and point $B$ at $(r + \mathrm{d}r, \theta)$ has displacement $u_r + \dfrac{\partial u_r}{\partial r}\mathrm{d}r$. The change in length of $AB$ is:

$$
\Delta(\mathrm{d}r) = \left(u_r + \frac{\partial u_r}{\partial r}\mathrm{d}r\right) - u_r = \frac{\partial u_r}{\partial r}\mathrm{d}r
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;\varepsilon_r = \frac{\partial u_r}{\partial r}\;}
$$

**Step 2: Hoop Strain $\varepsilon_\theta$**

Consider two neighbouring points $C$ and $D$ on a circle of radius $r$, separated by an angular increment $\mathrm{d}\theta$ (so that $r$ is constant). Their arc-length is $r\,\mathrm{d}\theta$. After deformation:
- $C$ moves to $C'$ with displacement $u_r$ in the radial direction and $u_\theta$ in the tangential direction.
- $D$ moves to $D'$ with displacement $u_r + \dfrac{\partial u_r}{\partial \theta}\mathrm{d}\theta$ radially and $u_\theta + \dfrac{\partial u_\theta}{\partial \theta}\mathrm{d}\theta$ tangentially.

The new angular separation, in first order, changes the arc-length by:
- Tangential displacement contribution: $\dfrac{\partial u_\theta}{\partial \theta}\mathrm{d}\theta$.
- Radial displacement contribution: The radial displacement $u_r$ at both ends is essentially the same (first-order, the same point of the same circle), but the **new radius** is $r + u_r$. Thus the arc-length changes by the factor $(r + u_r)/r$, contributing an additional strain $u_r/r$.

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;\varepsilon_\theta = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial u_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{u_r}{r}\;}
$$

**Step 3: Shear Strain $\gamma_{r\theta}$**

The shear strain is defined as the total decrease in the initially right angle at the corner of a curvilinear element with sides along $\hat{e}_r$ and $\hat{e}_\theta$. The change in angle has three contributions:
1. Tangential displacement varying with $r$: $\partial u_\theta / \partial r$.
