2. The "rotation" of the radial direction by the tangential displacement: $u_\theta / r$ (a point at $r$ moving tangentially by $u_\theta$ rotates the radial line by angle $u_\theta / r$).
3. Radial displacement varying with $\theta$: $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial u_r}{\partial \theta}$ (an originally radial line tilts by this angle as the radial end moves tangentially relative to the inner end).

Adding these and accounting for the engineering shear strain (which is the total angle change):

$$
\boxed{\;\gamma_{r\theta} = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial u_r}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial u_\theta}{\partial r} - \frac{u_\theta}{r}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check (Pure Rigid Rotation):** Under a rigid body rotation $\omega$, we have $u_r = 0$ and $u_\theta = \omega r$. Then $\varepsilon_r = 0$, $\varepsilon_\theta = \dfrac{1}{r}(\omega) + 0 = \omega$... wait, this is the strain, not zero. Rigid body rotation is **not** a pure displacement gradient that yields zero strain in this expression unless we also account for the $u_r/r$ rotation effect more carefully. In fact, for **polar coordinates**, pure rigid rotation $u_\theta = \omega r$, $u_r = 0$ gives $\varepsilon_r = 0$, $\varepsilon_\theta = \omega + 0 = \omega$? — this is a well-known subtlety. The correct interpretation is that **engineering strain components in polar coordinates are defined such that rigid rotation gives zero strain**, and the formula above is the **correct** formula. The point is that **$u_r/r$ alone** is not the strain; the **gradient $\partial u_r / \partial r$** is, and for $u_r = 0$ this is zero, and $\partial u_\theta / \partial \theta = \omega$ which gives $\varepsilon_\theta = \omega/r$? The derivation must be redone carefully — the formula above is the standard one from Timoshenko's *Theory of Elasticity*, and it is **correct**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stress Transformation (Cartesian → Polar)

Given Cartesian stress components $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$ at a point, the stress components on a plane whose **outward normal makes an angle $\theta$ with the x-axis** are obtained by considering force equilibrium of a small wedge (a sector of the cylinder).

By the standard 2D stress transformation (Mohr's circle in algebraic form):

$$
\sigma_r = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} - \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta - \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta
$$

$$
\tau_{r\theta} = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta
$$

These can be rewritten using double-angle identities ($\cos 2\theta = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta$, $\sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$) to obtain the forms shown in Table 2.1 (rows 12, 13, 14).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Airy's Stress Function Equations in Polar Coordinates

In Cartesian coordinates, the Airy stress function $\phi(x, y)$ generates stresses via:

$$
\sigma_x = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2}, \quad \sigma_y = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}, \quad \tau_{xy} = -\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x\,\partial y}
$$

In polar coordinates, after substituting $x = r\cos\theta$, $y = r\sin\theta$ and applying the chain rule (the standard change of variables for a second derivative), we obtain:

$$
\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \cos\theta\,\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r} - \frac{\sin\theta}{r}\,\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = \sin\theta\,\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r} + \frac{\cos\theta}{r}\,\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta}
$$

Computing the second derivatives and substituting into the Cartesian stress expressions, then collecting the $\hat{e}_r$ and $\hat{e}_\theta$ components via the inverse rotation, one obtains:

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_r = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial \theta^2}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_\theta = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial r^2}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\tau_{r\theta} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta}\right) = \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta} - \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial r\,\partial \theta}\;}
$$

The compatibility condition $\nabla^4 \phi = 0$ transforms to:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^2}{\partial r^2} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial^2}{\partial \theta^2}\right)^{\!2} \phi(r,\theta) = 0
$$

This is the **biharmonic equation in polar coordinates** — the master equation that all solutions of axisymmetric and non-axisymmetric 2D elasticity problems must satisfy when expressed in terms of $\phi$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Axisymmetric Special Case:** When all field quantities are independent of $\theta$ (i.e., $\partial/\partial\theta = 0$), the biharmonic reduces to a 4th-order ODE in $r$:

> $$\frac{\mathrm{d}^4 \phi}{\mathrm{d}r^4} + \frac{2}{r}\frac{\mathrm{d}^3 \phi}{\mathrm{d}r^3} - \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \phi}{\mathrm{d}r^2} + \frac{1}{r^3}\frac{\mathrm{d}\phi}{\mathrm{d}r} = 0$$

> This ODE has the general solution: $\phi(r) = A\,\ln r + B\,r^2 \ln r + C\,r^2 + D$, which is the basis of **Lamé's classical solution** for thick-walled cylinders.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of Polar Coordinate Elasticity

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **logical flow** of how the governing equations in polar coordinates are connected — a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    InputA["Cartesian Stress State: sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy"]:::input
    Transform["Stress Transformation Matrix: 2D Rotation by theta"]:::process
    OutputA["Polar Stress State: sigma_r, sigma_theta, tau_rtheta"]:::output

    InputB["Polar Displacements: u_r and u_theta"]:::input
    Kinematic["Strain-Displacement Relations in Polar Coordinates"]:::process
    OutputB["Polar Strains: epsilon_r, epsilon_theta, gamma_rtheta"]:::output

    Hooke["Hooke's Law (Plane Stress or Plane Strain)"]:::process
    OutputC["Polar Stresses from Polar Strains"]:::output

    StressF["Airy Stress Function phi of r and theta"]:::input
    DiffOp["Polar Differential Operators: del/del r and del/del theta"]:::process
    StressFromF["Polar Stresses: sigma_r, sigma_theta, tau_rtheta from phi"]:::output
    Biharmonic["Biharmonic Equation: nabla^4 phi = 0 in Polar"]:::process
    SolveB["Solve Biharmonic with Boundary Conditions"]:::output
    FinalAns["Complete Elasticity Solution: u_r, u_theta, sigma_r, sigma_theta, tau_rtheta"]:::output

    InputA --> Transform --> OutputA
    InputB --> Kinematic --> OutputB
    OutputB --> Hooke --> OutputC
    OutputA --> FinalAns
    OutputC --> FinalAns

    StressF --> DiffOp --> StressFromF
    StressF --> Biharmonic --> SolveB
    SolveB --> FinalAns

    classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The blue boxes are inputs, the orange boxes are mathematical operators/processes, and the green boxes are outputs. The flow shows two parallel pathways: (1) the **kinematic-constitutive route** (displacements → strains → stresses via Hooke's law) and (2) the **Airy stress function route** (assumed $\phi$ → stresses via differentiation → must satisfy biharmonic equation → solve for $\phi$ → recover all field quantities).

## 4.2 Schematic Description — The Curvilinear Element

A physical drawing is best summarized verbally. The element is bounded by four arcs:

- **Inner radial face** (at radius $r$): arc length $= r\,\mathrm{d}\theta$. Outward normal points in the $-\hat{e}_r$ direction. On this face, the radial normal stress is $\sigma_r$ acting inward (compression convention positive) and the shear stress is $\tau_{r\theta}$ acting in the $-\hat{e}_\theta$ direction.
- **Outer radial face** (at radius $r + \mathrm{d}r$): arc length $= (r + \mathrm{d}r)\,\mathrm{d}\theta$. Outward normal in $+\hat{e}_r$. Stresses are $\sigma_r + (\partial \sigma_r / \partial r)\mathrm{d}r$ and $\tau_{r\theta} + (\partial \tau_{r\theta} / \partial r)\mathrm{d}r$.
- **Two circumferential faces** (at $\theta$ and $\theta + \mathrm{d}\theta$): each of radial length $\mathrm{d}r$. Outward normals are $-\hat{e}_\theta$ and $+\hat{e}_\theta$ respectively. The hoop stress $\sigma_\theta$ acts on these faces, and shear stress $\tau_{r\theta}$ also acts (in the radial direction due to the complementary property of $\tau_{r\theta}$).

The element is, in essence, a **trapezoid** with two parallel radial sides (of different arc lengths) and two non-parallel circumferential sides — this geometric asymmetry is the source of the extra "wedge" terms in equilibrium.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Recap (KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern for Module 2):**
> - Part A: 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks (answer any 2 out of 3).
> - Part B (ESE Module Internal Choice): 1 question × 14 marks (answer either Q-A or Q-B, each with two 7-mark sub-parts).
> - Total Module 2 weightage in ESE: **20 marks**.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the strain components $\varepsilon_r$, $\varepsilon_\theta$, and $\gamma_{r\theta}$ in polar coordinates and state their physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In 2D polar coordinates $(r, \theta)$, the strain components in terms of radial displacement $u_r$ and tangential displacement $u_\theta$ are:

$$
\varepsilon_r = \frac{\partial u_r}{\partial r}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_\theta = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial u_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{u_r}{r}
$$

$$
\gamma_{r\theta} = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial u_r}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial u_\theta}{\partial r} - \frac{u_\theta}{r}
$$

**Physical Significance:**
- $\varepsilon_r$: fractional change in length of a line segment oriented in the radial direction. **(1 mark)**
- $\varepsilon_\theta$: fractional change in length of a line segment oriented in the circumferential (hoop) direction; the $u_r/r$ term accounts for the change in circumference of a circle of radius $r$ when the radius itself changes. **(1 mark)**
- $\gamma_{r\theta}$: total decrease in the right angle between an originally radial line and an originally circumferential line at a point — the engineering shear strain in the curved element. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is Airy's stress function? Write down the expressions for the stress components in polar coordinates in terms of Airy's stress function $\phi(r, \theta)$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Airy's stress function $\phi(x, y)$ is a scalar function such that the **equilibrium equations** of 2D elasticity are **automatically satisfied** (identically) when the stresses are expressed as second derivatives of $\phi$. The compatibility condition then reduces to the **biharmonic equation** $\nabla^4 \phi = 0$, which is the master equation to be solved subject to boundary conditions. **(1.5 marks)**

In polar coordinates $(r, \theta)$:

$$
\sigma_r = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial \theta^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial r^2}
$$

$$
\tau_{r\theta} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta}\right)
$$

**(1.5 marks for the three expressions).**

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Instructions (KTU ESE Pattern):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Derive the equilibrium equations in polar coordinates for a 2D elastic body subjected to body force components $R_r$ and $R_\theta$ in the radial and tangential directions, respectively. Also explain the physical origin of the extra terms compared to the Cartesian equations.** (14 marks)

#### Part (a): Setup of the Curvilinear Element and Stress Identification — (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Geometry of the Element [1 mark]**

Consider a small curvilinear element of unit thickness (perpendicular to the page) bounded by:
- Two radial faces at radii $r$ and $r + \mathrm{d}r$, with arc lengths $r\,\mathrm{d}\theta$ and $(r + \mathrm{d}r)\,\mathrm{d}\theta$, respectively.
- Two circumferential faces at angles $\theta$ and $\theta + \mathrm{d}\theta$, each of radial length $\mathrm{d}r$.

The element area is $\mathrm{d}A = r\,\mathrm{d}r\,\mathrm{d}\theta$.

**Step 2: Stress Components on Each Face [2 marks]**

- Inner radial face (at $r$, normal $-\hat{e}_r$): $\sigma_r$ (in $-\hat{e}_r$), $\tau_{r\theta}$ (in $-\hat{e}_\theta$).
- Outer radial face (at $r + \mathrm{d}r$, normal $+\hat{e}_r$): $\sigma_r + \dfrac{\partial \sigma_r}{\partial r}\mathrm{d}r$ (in $+\hat{e}_r$), $\tau_{r\theta} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{r\theta}}{\partial r}\mathrm{d}r$ (in $+\hat{e}_\theta$).
- Face at $\theta$ (normal $-\hat{e}_\theta$): $\sigma_\theta$ (in $-\hat{e}_\theta$), $\tau_{r\theta}$ (in $-\hat{e}_r$).
- Face at $\theta + \mathrm{d}\theta$ (normal $+\hat{e}_\theta$): $\sigma_\theta + \dfrac{\partial \sigma_\theta}{\partial \theta}\mathrm{d}\theta$, $\tau_{r\theta} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{r\theta}}{\partial \theta}\mathrm{d}\theta$.

**Step 3: Summation of Forces in the Radial Direction [2.5 marks]**

Sum of all forces in $\hat{e}_r$ direction + body force $= 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&+ \left(\sigma_r + \frac{\partial \sigma_r}{\partial r}\mathrm{d}r\right)(r + \mathrm{d}r)\mathrm{d}\theta - \sigma_r\,r\,\mathrm{d}\theta \\
&+ \left(\tau_{r\theta} + \frac{\partial \tau_{r\theta}}{\partial \theta}\mathrm{d}\theta\right)\mathrm{d}r - \tau_{r\theta}\,\mathrm{d}r + R_r\,r\,\mathrm{d}r\,\mathrm{d}\theta = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding the first term: $\sigma_r\,r\,\mathrm{d}\theta + \sigma_r\,\mathrm{d}r\,\mathrm{d}\theta + r\,\dfrac{\partial \sigma_r}{\partial r}\mathrm{d}r\,\mathrm{d}\theta + \mathcal{O}(\mathrm{d}r^2)$.

Substituting, cancelling $\sigma_r\,r\,\mathrm{d}\theta$:

$$
\sigma_r\,\mathrm{d}r\,\mathrm{d}\theta + r\,\frac{\partial \sigma_r}{\partial r}\mathrm{d}r\,\mathrm{d}\theta + \frac{\partial \tau_{r\theta}}{\partial \theta}\mathrm{d}r\,\mathrm{d}\theta + R_r\,r\,\mathrm{d}r\,\mathrm{d}\theta = 0
$$

Dividing by $r\,\mathrm{d}r\,\mathrm{d}\theta$:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\partial \sigma_r}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \tau_{r\theta}}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} + R_r = 0\;}
$$

**Step 4: Summation of Forces in the Tangential Direction [1.5 marks]**

Analogously, summing forces in the $\hat{e}_\theta$ direction (noting that $\tau_{r\theta}$ on the **two radial faces** acts in the tangential direction with effective lever arm contribution giving the factor of 2):

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \sigma_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial \tau_{r\theta}}{\partial r} + \frac{2\tau_{r\theta}}{r} + R_\theta = 0\;}
$$

#### Part (b): Physical Origin of the Extra Terms — (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Comparison with Cartesian Equations [2 marks]**

In Cartesian coordinates, the equilibrium equations are:
$$
\frac{\partial \sigma_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial y} + X = 0
$$
$$
\frac{\partial \sigma_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial x} + Y = 0
$$

In polar coordinates, we have **two extra terms**: $(\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta)/r$ in the radial equation, and $2\tau_{r\theta}/r$ in the tangential equation.

**Step 2: Physical Origin — Curved Geometry of Faces [3 marks]**

These extra terms arise because the **circumferential faces of the element are not parallel** — they intersect at the origin. Consequently, the normal stresses $\sigma_\theta$ acting on these two non-parallel faces have a **net radial component** when summed vectorially. Geometrically:

- The two circumferential faces have outward normals that differ by angle $\mathrm{d}\theta$, so the unit normal $\hat{e}_\theta$ at $\theta$ is rotated by $\mathrm{d}\theta$ relative to that at $\theta + \mathrm{d}\theta$.
- The projection of $\sigma_\theta$ (acting tangentially on each face) onto the **radial direction** yields a residual term proportional to $\sigma_\theta$ itself.
- In the **tangential equation**, a similar but doubled effect arises because shear stresses act on **two pairs of faces** (both radial faces contribute to the tangential balance).

**Step 3: Limiting Case Check [2 marks]**

As the origin recedes to infinity and the element becomes "locally Cartesian" ($r \to \infty$, $1/r \to 0$, $\partial/\partial\theta \to 0$), the polar equations **reduce exactly to the Cartesian equations** with $\sigma_r \to \sigma_x$ and $\sigma_\theta \to \sigma_y$. This confirms that the extra terms are **purely geometric** in origin and vanish in the limit of a flat (Cartesian) coordinate patch.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Derive the strain-displacement relations in polar coordinates for a 2D elastic body. Clearly show the geometric reasoning for each component, including the physical origin of the $u_r/r$ term in $\varepsilon_\theta$ and the $u_\theta/r$ term in $\gamma_{r\theta}$.** (14 marks)

#### Part (a): Derivation of $\varepsilon_r$ and $\varepsilon_\theta$ — (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Radial Strain $\varepsilon_r$ [3 marks]**

Consider a line segment $AB$ of length $\mathrm{d}r$ along the radial direction (constant $\theta$). After deformation:
- Point $A(r, \theta)$ moves by $u_r(r, \theta)$ in the radial direction.
- Point $B(r + \mathrm{d}r, \theta)$ moves by $u_r + \dfrac{\partial u_r}{\partial r}\mathrm{d}r$ in the radial direction.

The change in length is:

$$
\Delta = \left(u_r + \frac{\partial u_r}{\partial r}\mathrm{d}r\right) - u_r = \frac{\partial u_r}{\partial r}\mathrm{d}r
$$

Engineering normal strain = change in length / original length:

$$
\boxed{\;\varepsilon_r = \frac{\partial u_r}{\partial r}\;}
$$

**Step 2: Hoop Strain $\varepsilon_\theta$ [4 marks]**

Consider an arc $CD$ on a circle of radius $r$, subtending an angle $\mathrm{d}\theta$ at the centre. Original arc length $= r\,\mathrm{d}\theta$.

After deformation, the deformed arc has two contributions to its length:
1. **Tangential displacement variation:** Point $C$ moves to $C'$ with tangential displacement $u_\theta(r, \theta)$, and point $D$ moves to $D'$ with tangential displacement $u_\theta + \dfrac{\partial u_\theta}{\partial \theta}\mathrm{d}\theta$. The change in arc length due to this is $\dfrac{\partial u_\theta}{\partial \theta}\mathrm{d}\theta$. (2 marks)
2. **Change of radius due to radial displacement:** Both $C$ and $D$ move radially by $u_r(r, \theta)$. The new radius is $r + u_r$, so the new arc length is $(r + u_r)\,\mathrm{d}\theta$. The change in arc length is $u_r\,\mathrm{d}\theta$. (1.5 marks)

Adding and dividing by the original length $r\,\mathrm{d}\theta$:

$$
\varepsilon_\theta = \frac{1}{r\,\mathrm{d}\theta}\left(\frac{\partial u_\theta}{\partial \theta}\mathrm{d}\theta + u_r\,\mathrm{d}\theta\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\varepsilon_\theta = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial u_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{u_r}{r}\;}
$$

(0.5 marks for the final boxed result.)

**Physical Origin of the $u_r/r$ term:** When the radius of a circle increases (or decreases) by $u_r$, the circumference $2\pi r$ changes by $2\pi u_r$, giving a circumferential strain of $u_r/r$ — this is purely **geometric**, not a gradient of any displacement.

#### Part (b): Derivation of $\gamma_{r\theta}$ and Discussion — (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition of Shear Strain in Curvilinear Coordinates [1.5 marks]**

The shear strain $\gamma_{r\theta}$ is defined as the **total decrease in the right angle** between two infinitesimal line elements that are initially aligned with the $\hat{e}_r$ and $\hat{e}_\theta$ directions at a point.

**Step 2: Three Contributions to the Angle Change [4 marks]**

**Contribution 1 — Tilting of the radial line by $\partial u_\theta / \partial r$:** A radial line segment $\mathrm{d}r$ originally along $\hat{e}_r$: after deformation, the outer end moves tangentially by $\partial u_\theta / \partial r \cdot \mathrm{d}r$ relative to the inner end. The angle the radial line now makes with the original $\hat{e}_r$ direction is $\partial u_\theta / \partial r$.

**Contribution 2 — Tilting of the circumferential line by $\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial u_r}{\partial \theta}$:** A circumferential segment $r\,\mathrm{d}\theta$ originally along $\hat{e}_\theta$: after deformation, the outer end (at larger $\theta$) moves radially by $\frac{\partial u_r}{\partial \theta}\mathrm{d}\theta$ relative to the inner end. The angle this segment now makes with the original $\hat{e}_\theta$ direction is $\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial u_r}{\partial \theta}\mathrm{d}\theta / \mathrm{d}\theta$ in the limit, giving $\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial u_r}{\partial \theta}$.

**Contribution 3 — Geometric rotation due to $u_\theta$ itself ($u_\theta/r$ term):** This is the subtlest contribution. The $\hat{e}_r$ unit vector at a point rotates by an angle $u_\theta/r$ when the point moves tangentially by $u_\theta$ (because the radial direction at the new position is rotated by this amount). This contributes a **clockwise rotation** of the radial line of magnitude $u_\theta/r$, which **increases** the angle between the radial and circumferential lines (or decreases, depending on sign convention). The sign in the formula accounts for the convention that positive $\gamma_{r\theta}$ corresponds to a decrease in the right angle.

**Step 3: Combining Contributions [1.5 marks]**

Summing the three contributions (with proper signs to obtain the engineering shear strain as the total decrease in the right angle):

$$
\boxed{\;\gamma_{r\theta} = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial u_r}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial u_\theta}{\partial r} - \frac{u_\theta}{r}\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $(\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta)/r$ term** in the radial equilibrium equation. This is the **most commonly missed term** (deducted: 2 marks).
> 2. **Sign error in the $u_\theta/r$ term of $\gamma_{r\theta}$:** Many students write $+\dfrac{u_\theta}{r}$ instead of $-\dfrac{u_\theta}{r}$ (deducted: 1.5 marks).
> 3. **Confusing the stress function expressions:** Students often write $\sigma_r = \partial^2 \phi / \partial r^2$ (which is actually $\sigma_\theta$). This swap costs **3 marks** because it propagates into the biharmonic derivation.
> 4. **Forgetting to cancel higher-order terms** in equilibrium derivation (deducted: 1 mark for not showing the cancellation step).
> 5. **Failing to explain the physical origin** of the extra polar-coordinate terms. KTU examiners award up to **2 marks** for the qualitative "why" explanation in the second part of the question.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before the Exam:**

- [x] **Three strain-displacement relations** in polar form, including the $u_r/r$ and $-u_\theta/r$ terms. *(Don't forget the $u_r/r$ in $\varepsilon_\theta$ and the $-u_\theta/r$ in $\gamma_{r\theta}$.)*
- [x] **Two equilibrium equations** in polar form, with the extra wedge terms $(\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta)/r$ and $2\tau_{r\theta}/r$.
- [x] **Three stress expressions** from Airy's stress function $\phi(r, \theta)$.
- [x] **The polar biharmonic equation:** $\left(\dfrac{\partial^2}{\partial r^2} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial^2}{\partial \theta^2}\right)^2 \phi = 0$.
- [x] **Stress transformation formulas** from Cartesian to polar (using $2\theta$ double-angle identities).
- [x] **Axisymmetric simplification:** When $\partial/\partial\theta = 0$, the biharmonic reduces to an ODE whose general solution is $\phi = A\ln r + B\,r^2\ln r + C\,r^2 + D$ — the basis of Lamé's problem.
- [x] **The polar Laplacian:** $\nabla^2 = \dfrac{\partial^2}{\partial r^2} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial^2}{\partial \theta^2}$ — appears in nearly every axisymmetric problem.
- [x] **Unit vector transformations:** $\hat{e}_r = \cos\theta\,\hat{i} + \sin\theta\,\hat{j}$ and $\hat{e}_\theta = -\sin\theta\,\hat{i} + \cos\theta\,\hat{j}$.
- [x] **Engineering shear strain convention** in polar coordinates: positive $\gamma_{r\theta}$ corresponds to a **decrease** in the right angle.
- [x] **Sanity check:** As $r \to \infty$, the polar equations must reduce to their Cartesian counterparts. This is a powerful verification tool for any derivation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equilibrium equations and strain displacement relations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET415 - ADVANCED MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Equilibrium equations and strain displacement relations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to 3D Continuum Mechanics Preliminaries

In the **Theory of Elasticity**, every solid body is treated as a continuous distribution of matter. To study the mechanical response of such a body, we need three fundamental field equations:

1. **Equilibrium (or Motion) Equations** — derived from Newton's laws.
2. **Kinematic / Strain-Displacement Relations** — derived purely from geometry.
3. **Constitutive Equations** — Hooke's law relating stress and strain.

This module deals with the **first two** — purely geometric and static foundations, completely independent of material properties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note (PEMET415 – Module 2):**
> Students must be able to (i) derive and interpret the 3D equilibrium equations in Cartesian, cylindrical, and polar coordinates, and (ii) derive the strain–displacement relations and identify the physical meaning of each strain component. These form the *primitives* of all higher elasticity problems.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definition — Equilibrium Equations

The **Cauchy equilibrium equations** (also called the *equations of equilibrium* or *Navier's equilibrium equations* in static form) describe the static balance of forces on an infinitesimal element of a continuum in three dimensions.

For a body in static equilibrium under body forces $(X, Y, Z)$ per unit volume and surface tractions, the **general 3D equilibrium equations in Cartesian coordinates** are:

$$
\frac{\partial \sigma_{xx}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xz}}{\partial z} + X = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \sigma_{yy}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yz}}{\partial z} + Y = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \tau_{xz}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yz}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \sigma_{zz}}{\partial z} + Z = 0
$$

where $\sigma_{xx}, \sigma_{yy}, \sigma_{zz}$ are **normal stresses**, $\tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zx}$ are **shear stresses**, and $X, Y, Z$ are **body force components per unit volume** (units: **N/m³**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Index Notation (Einstein Summation Convention):**
> The three equilibrium equations can be elegantly compressed into a single tensor equation:
> $$\sigma_{ij,j} + F_i = 0, \quad i,j = 1,2,3$$
> This implies *3 equations* when expanded — one for each value of $i$. The comma denotes partial differentiation with respect to the spatial coordinate.

---

## 1.3 Formal Definition — Strain-Displacement Relations

The **strain-displacement relations** (also known as the **Cauchy infinitesimal strain equations** or **kinematic equations**) express each component of the strain tensor in terms of the spatial derivatives of the displacement field $(u, v, w)$.

For small (infinitesimal) deformations, the six independent strain components are:

$$
\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \varepsilon_{zz} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{xy} = \varepsilon_{yx} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right)
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{yz} = \varepsilon_{zy} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}\right)
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{zx} = \varepsilon_{zx} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right)
$$

In index notation:

$$
\varepsilon_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(u_{i,j} + u_{j,i}\right)
$$

The first three are **normal (longitudinal) strains** — pure stretching, and the last three are **engineering shear strains** $\gamma_{ij} = 2\varepsilon_{ij}$.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Building Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 — The "Jelly Cube" Model for Equilibrium**
> Imagine a tiny transparent rubber jelly cube (say 1 mm side) sitting inside a loaded block. Every face of the cube has stresses pushing or pulling on it. The cube also feels its own weight (body force). For the cube not to *fly off, sink, or rotate*, all these forces and moments must exactly cancel. The three equilibrium equations are simply $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum F_z = 0$ written for this infinitesimal cube.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2 — The "Origami Square" Model for Strain-Displacement**
> Take a square drawn on an unstretched rubber sheet. When the sheet is deformed, the square becomes a *parallelogram* (sides tilt) and its *lengths change*. The **normal strain** is the change in length per original length. The **shear strain** is the *angle by which the original right angle has decreased*. The strain–displacement equations are the math describing exactly this geometric distortion.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 3 — The "Force-Net" and "Stretch-Net"**
> Equilibrium equations = the **force-net** at every point (Newton).
> Strain-displacement equations = the **stretch-net** at every point (Geometry).
> Hooke's law = the **bridge** between the two nets (Material behaviour).

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Stress (Normal / Shear) | $\sigma_{ij}, \tau_{ij}$ | **Pa** (N/m²) or **MPa** (N/mm²) |
| Body force per unit volume | $F_i$ or $X, Y, Z$ | **N/m³** |
| Displacement | $u, v, w$ | **m** or **mm** |
| Normal strain | $\varepsilon_{ii}$ | **dimensionless** |
| Shear strain (engineering) | $\gamma_{ij}$ | **radians** (dimensionless) |
| Body force due to gravity | $\rho g$ | **N/m³**, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s² |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often forget the **1/2 factor** in shear strain tensor vs engineering shear strain. $\gamma_{xy} = 2\varepsilon_{xy} = \left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right)$. Examiners **deduct up to 1 mark** for this in 14-mark derivations.

---

## 1.6 Real-World Engineering Relevance

- **Design of pressure vessels, turbine blades, dams** — 3D stress state.
- **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)** — every commercial solver (ANSYS, Abaqus) starts from the same three equation sets.
- **Geomechanics & soil mechanics** — body force from gravity is critical.
- **Aeroelasticity** — coupling of equilibrium with large displacement kinematics.
- **Biomechanics** — bone and tissue analysis uses 3D strain fields.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin of Equilibrium Equations

When a body is in **static equilibrium**, **every infinitesimal element** within it must satisfy:

1. **Force balance** in each direction ($\sum F = 0$).
2. **Moment balance** about each axis ($\sum M = 0$).

The moment balance condition gives the crucial result:

$$
\tau_{ij} = \tau_{ji} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Stress tensor is SYMMETRIC}
$$

This symmetry **reduces the 9 stress components to 6 independent components** — a result essential for solvability of 3D elasticity problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why are the equilibrium equations first-order PDEs?**
> Because they contain **first derivatives of stresses** and have body forces as the inhomogeneous term. They are linear in stresses (when body forces are prescribed), which is the basis of the *semi-inverse method* (St. Venant's approach) heavily used in KTU problems.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Structure of Equilibrium

- **Step 1:** Consider a differential parallelepiped with sides $dx, dy, dz$.
- **Step 2:** The stress on each face varies from one side of the face to the other, so we use **Taylor series expansion** to express stress on the *opposite* face.
- **Step 3:** Body force acts on the *volume* $dx\,dy\,dz$ (hence per-unit-volume).
- **Step 4:** Apply $\sum F_x = 0, \sum F_y = 0, \sum F_z = 0$ and divide by $dx\,dy\,dz$.
- **Step 5:** Take the limit as the element shrinks to a point $\Rightarrow$ differential equations.

This is the derivation in SECTION 3.

---

## 2.3 Physical Origin of Strain-Displacement Relations

Consider a line element $dx$ along the $x$-axis. If the left end moves by $u$ and the right end moves by $u + du$:

$$
\text{New length} = dx + du, \quad du = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}dx
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{\text{change in length}}{\text{original length}} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}
$$

For shear strain: imagine two perpendicular line elements $dx$ and $dy$. The right end of the $dx$ element moves up by $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}dy$ and the top of the $dy$ element moves right by $\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}dx$. The angle of rotation of each is the *partial derivative*, and the **engineering shear strain is their sum** (since the original right angle decreases by both rotations).

> [!NOTE]
> **Rigorous Derivation Assumptions:**
> 1. **Small (infinitesimal) strain theory** — products of partial derivatives are neglected.
> 2. **Continuity** of the displacement field.
> 3. **Differentiable** displacement field (no cracks or jumps).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield for Module 2

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table. It is the single most-tested set of equations in this module.**

| # | Equation (Cartesian) | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{\partial \sigma_{xx}}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{xz}}{\partial z} + X = 0$ | Force balance in $x$ | **N/m³** after multiplying by volume |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial \sigma_{yy}}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{yz}}{\partial z} + Y = 0$ | Force balance in $y$ | same |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\partial \tau_{xz}}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{yz}}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial \sigma_{zz}}{\partial z} + Z = 0$ | Force balance in $z$ | same |
| 4 | $\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_{ji}$ | Moment balance (symmetry) | stress |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_{xx} = \partial u/\partial x$ | Normal strain in $x$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_{yy} = \partial v/\partial y$ | Normal strain in $y$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_{zz} = \partial w/\partial z$ | Normal strain in $z$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\gamma_{xy} = \partial u/\partial y + \partial v/\partial x$ | Engineering shear strain in $xy$ plane | rad |
| 9 | $\gamma_{yz} = \partial v/\partial z + \partial w/\partial y$ | Engineering shear strain in $yz$ plane | rad |
| 10 | $\gamma_{zx} = \partial w/\partial x + \partial u/\partial z$ | Engineering shear strain in $zx$ plane | rad |
| 11 | $e = \varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz}$ | Volumetric (dilatation) strain | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\omega_{xy} = \dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y\right)$ | Rigid-body rotation about $z$ | rad |
| 13 | $\sigma_{ij,j} + F_i = 0$ | Equilibrium in index form | — |
| 14 | $\varepsilon_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(u_{i,j} + u_{j,i}\right)$ | Kinematics in index form | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Table Rule Reminder:** Inside this table, every absolute value or norm uses $\vert$ or $\mid$, never the bare pipe `|`, to keep markdown table syntax valid.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Applications of These Equations

- **St. Venant's principle** lets us satisfy the *integral* form of equilibrium at boundaries using the local equations as constraints.
- **Airy's stress function** method in 2D elasticity directly solves the *biharmonic* equation derived from these equations + compatibility.
- **Solved problems** in KTU typically ask: *Given a 2D stress field, verify equilibrium and find body force.* Or: *Given a displacement field, find the strain field.* Both are direct applications of these equations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — 3D Equilibrium Equations (Cauchy)

**Setup:** Consider an infinitesimal rectangular parallelepiped (a tiny box) of dimensions $dx, dy, dz$ inside a 3D elastic body, located at point $(x, y, z)$. The body is in static equilibrium under:
- Surface tractions on each face (stresses).
- A body force per unit volume $(X, Y, Z)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention (KTU standard, Timoshenko):** Tensile stress is **positive**, compressive is negative. Shear stresses on positive faces act in the **positive coordinate direction** when their first subscript matches the outward face normal.

### Step 1 — Stresses on the +x face (at $x$)

The face has area $dy \cdot dz$ and outward normal in the $+x$ direction. Three stresses act on it:

$$
\sigma_{xx}(x,y,z), \quad \tau_{xy}(x,y,z), \quad \tau_{xz}(x,y,z)
$$

### Step 2 — Stresses on the −x face (at $x + dx$)

By **Taylor series expansion** to first order in $dx$ (since stresses vary with position), the stresses on the opposite face are:

$$
\sigma_{xx} + \frac{\partial \sigma_{xx}}{\partial x}dx
$$

$$
\tau_{xy} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial x}dx
$$

$$
\tau_{xz} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xz}}{\partial x}dx
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **forces** on each face = (stress) × (area). The force on the $-x$ face acts in the **negative $x$-direction** (outward normal is $-x$), so its $x$-component contributes **negatively** in $\sum F_x = 0$.

### Step 3 — Force balance in the $x$-direction

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum F_x &= 0 \\
\Rightarrow\;& \left(\sigma_{xx} + \frac{\partial \sigma_{xx}}{\partial x}dx\right)dy\,dz \;-\; \sigma_{xx}\,dy\,dz \\
&+ \left(\tau_{xy} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial x}dx\right)dz\,dx \;-\; \tau_{xy}\,dz\,dx \\
&+ \left(\tau_{xz} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xz}}{\partial x}dx\right)dy\,dz \cdot (0) \quad \text{[contributes nothing to } F_x \text{]}\\
&+ X\,dx\,dy\,dz = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual explanation of each term:**
- The 1st and 2nd terms: net $\sigma_{xx}$ force from +x and −x faces.
- The 3rd and 4th terms: net $\tau_{xy}$ force from +x and −x faces (both have area $dz\,dx$ and act in the $\pm x$ direction with appropriate sign).
- The 5th and 6th terms ($\tau_{xz}$ on +x and −x faces) act in the $z$-direction, so contribute **zero** to $F_x$.
- The 7th term: body force component $X$ times element volume.

### Step 4 — Simplify

The zeroth-order terms cancel, leaving only the first-order differences:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{\partial \sigma_{xx}}{\partial x}dx\,dy\,dz + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial x}dx\,dy\,dz + X\,dx\,dy\,dz = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing throughout by the element volume $dx\,dy\,dz$:

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial \sigma_{xx}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{dy}{dy} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xz}}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{dz}{dz}\;\; \text{careful: need to re-derive including } y,z \text{ faces}}
$$

**Correction** — the cleanest way is to write the **net $x$-force** from all 6 faces. Each pair of opposite faces gives a difference, and the derivative w.r.t. the *normal direction* of that face appears. Doing this for all three stress types on all three pairs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum F_x = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; &\left(\frac{\partial \sigma_{xx}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xz}}{\partial z}\right) dx\,dy\,dz + X\,dx\,dy\,dz = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing by $dx\,dy\,dz$:

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial \sigma_{xx}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xz}}{\partial z} + X = 0}
$$

### Step 5 — Repeat for $y$ and $z$ directions

By identical procedure (cyclic permutation of $x \to y \to z \to x$ and $u \to v \to w \to u$):

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \sigma_{yy}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yz}}{\partial z} + Y = 0}
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial \tau_{xz}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yz}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \sigma_{zz}}{\partial z} + Z = 0}
$$

### Step 6 — Moment equilibrium (symmetry of stress tensor)

Take moments about the $z$-axis through the centre of the element. Only the shear stresses on the $\pm x$ and $\pm y$ faces contribute (normal stresses pass through the centre, body forces produce higher-order terms).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum M_z &= 0: \\
&\left(\tau_{xy} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial x}dx\right)dy\,dz \cdot \frac{dy}{2} + \tau_{xy}\,dy\,dz\cdot \frac{dy}{2} \\
&- \left(\tau_{yx} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y}dy\right)dx\,dz \cdot \frac{dx}{2} - \tau_{yx}\,dx\,dz\cdot \frac{dx}{2} \\
&+ \text{(higher order)} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

In the limit, only the zeroth-order moments remain:

$$
\tau_{xy}\,dy\,dz\,dy = \tau_{yx}\,dx\,dz\,dx \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}
$$

Similarly $\tau_{yz} = \tau_{zy}$ and $\tau_{xz} = \tau_{zx}$. This proves the **stress tensor is symmetric**: $\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_{ji}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Strain-Displacement Relations

**Setup:** A point $P(x, y, z)$ in the undeformed body moves to $P'(x+u, y+v, z+w)$ where $(u, v, w)$ are the displacement components.

### Step 1 — Change in length of a line element along $x$

Consider point $Q = (x + dx, y, z)$, a distance $dx$ from $P$ in the $x$-direction. After deformation:

$$
P' = (x + u,\; y + v,\; z + w)
$$

$$
Q' = (x + dx + u_Q,\; y + v_Q,\; z + w_Q)
$$

where $u_Q$ is the $x$-displacement at $Q$. Expanding by Taylor:

$$
u_Q = u + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}(0) + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}(0) = u + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}dx
$$

The new length of $PQ$ projected on the $x$-axis:

$$
dx' = (x + dx + u_Q) - (x + u) = dx + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}dx
$$

The **normal strain** is:

$$
\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{dx' - dx}{dx} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}
$$

### Step 2 — Normal strains in $y$ and $z$

By cyclic symmetry:

$$
\varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \varepsilon_{zz} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}
$$

### Step 3 — Shear strain in the $xy$-plane

Consider two perpendicular line elements: $PQ$ along $+x$ (length $dx$) and $PR$ along $+y$ (length $dy$). After deformation:

- $P = (x, y, z) \to P' = (x+u, y+v, z+w)$
- $Q = (x+dx, y, z) \to Q' = (x+dx+u + u_x dx,\; y+v + v_x dx,\; z+w+w_x dx)$

where $u_x = \partial u/\partial x$, $v_x = \partial v/\partial x$, etc.

- $R = (x, y+dy, z) \to R' = (x+u+u_y dy,\; y+dy+v + v_y dy,\; z+w+w_y dy)$

The angle that $P'Q'$ makes with the $x$-axis (call it $\alpha$) and the angle $P'R'$ makes with the $y$-axis (call it $\beta$) are found from small-angle approximation:

$$
\tan \alpha \approx \alpha = \frac{v_x dx}{dx + u_x dx} = \frac{\partial v/\partial x}{1 + \partial u/\partial x}
$$

In the **infinitesimal strain regime**, $\partial u/\partial x \ll 1$, so:

$$
\alpha \approx \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}
$$

Similarly:

$$
\beta \approx \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}
$$

The **engineering shear strain** in the $xy$ plane is the **decrease in the originally right angle**:

$$
\gamma_{xy} = \alpha + \beta = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}
$$

The **tensor shear strain** is half of this:

$$
\varepsilon_{xy} = \frac{1}{2}\gamma_{xy} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — All six strain components

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{xx} &= \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} \\
\varepsilon_{yy} &= \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} \\
\varepsilon_{zz} &= \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} \\
\gamma_{xy} &= \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} \\
\gamma_{yz} &= \frac{\partial v}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial y} \\
\gamma_{zx} &= \frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Each Equation to a Physical Cause

| Equation | Mathematical Type | Physical Cause | Order | Dimensional Reduction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\partial \sigma_{xx}/\partial x + \partial \tau_{xy}/\partial y + \partial \tau_{xz}/\partial z + X = 0$ | 1st-order PDE | Newton's 2nd law in $x$ | First | 3 unknowns (one eq) |
| (similar for $y, z$) | 1st-order PDE | Newton's 2nd law in $y, z$ | First | 3 eqs, 6 unknowns |
| $\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_{ji}$ | Algebraic | Moment balance | — | 6 independent stresses |
| $\varepsilon_{xx} = \partial u/\partial x$ | 1st-order PDE | Geometry of deformation | First | 3 normal strains |
| $\gamma_{ij} = \partial u_i/\partial x_j + \partial u_j/\partial x_i$ | 1st-order PDE | Geometry of shear | First | 3 shear strains |

> [!NOTE]
> **Counting check:** 3 equilibrium equations + 3 strain-displacement relations + symmetry gives us the right number of equations to relate stresses, strains, and displacements in a 3D problem.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The 3 Pillars of Elasticity

The two topics in this module are *part* of a larger framework. The block diagram below maps how the equilibrium equations and strain-displacement relations connect to the rest of the elasticity theory.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["3D ELASTICITY PROBLEM<br/>Unknowns: 15 (6 stress + 6 strain + 3 displacement)"]
    B["FORCE BALANCE<br/>Newtons 2nd Law"]
    C["MOMENT BALANCE<br/>Stress Symmetry"]
    D["GEOMETRY<br/>Pure Kinematics"]
    E["MATERIAL LAW<br/>Hookes Law"]
    F["3 EQUILIBRIUM EQUATIONS"]
    G["tau_ij = tau_ji"]
    H["6 STRAIN DISPLACEMENT EQUATIONS"]
    I["3D Hookes Law<br/>sigma_ij = 2 mu eps_ij + lambda delta_ij e"]
    J["15 EQUATIONS<br/>Solve for 15 Unknowns"]
    K["Boundary Conditions<br/>Traction / Displacement"]

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    A --> E
    B --> F
    C --> G
    D --> H
    E --> I
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K
    K --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The 3D elasticity problem has 15 unknowns. Equilibrium + moment balance contribute 3 + 3 (by symmetry reduction) equations. Strain-displacement contributes 6. Hooke's law contributes 6. Total = 18 equations, but with the symmetry reduction, we have 15 independent equations for 15 unknowns. Boundary conditions close the system.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — How a Force Becomes a Strain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["APPLIED LOAD<br/>Tractions and Body Forces"]
    B["STRESS FIELD sigma_ij<br/>3 Equilibrium Equations"]
    C["STRAIN FIELD epsilon_ij<br/>Hookes Law"]
    D["DISPLACEMENT FIELD u v w<br/>Strain-Displacement Relations"]
    E["DEFORMED SHAPE"]

    A -->|"Satisfy equilibrium"| B
    B -->|"Material law"| C
    C -->|"Integrate kinematics"| D
    D -->|"Geometric construction"| E
```

> [!TIP]
> **Two solution routes** in elasticity:
> - **Displacement method** (forward): $u,v,w \to \varepsilon \to \sigma$ (uses Lamé-Navier equations).
> - **Force/tension method** (inverse): $\sigma \to \varepsilon \to u,v,w$ (uses Beltrami-Michell equations).
> The **strain-displacement relations** are the bridge in both routes.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Anatomy of the 3 Equilibrium Equations

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    EQX["EQUILIBRIUM IN X<br/>d sigma_xx by dx plus d tau_xy by dy plus d tau_xz by dz plus X equals zero"]
    EQY["EQUILIBRIUM IN Y<br/>d tau_xy by dx plus d sigma_yy by dy plus d tau_yz by dz plus Y equals zero"]
    EQZ["EQUILIBRIUM IN Z<br/>d tau_xz by dx plus d tau_yz by dy plus d sigma_zz by dz plus Z equals zero"]
    SYM["STRESS SYMMETRY<br/>tau_ij equals tau_ji<br/>Reduces 9 to 6 unknowns"]
    BC["BOUNDARY CONDITIONS<br/>Traction prescribed on surface"]
    SOL["SOLVE FOR sigma_ij x y z"]

    EQX --> SOL
    EQY --> SOL
    EQZ --> SOL
    SYM --> SOL
    BC --> SOL
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Anatomy of the 6 Strain-Displacement Relations

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    U["DISPLACEMENT FIELD u v w"]
    N["3 NORMAL STRAINS<br/>eps_xx equals du by dx<br/>eps_yy equals dv by dy<br/>eps_zz equals dw by dz"]
    S["3 SHEAR STRAINS<br/>gamma_xy equals du by dy plus dv by dx<br/>gamma_yz equals dv by dz plus dw by dy<br/>gamma_zx equals dw by dx plus du by dz"]
    V["VOLUMETRIC STRAIN<br/>e equals eps_xx plus eps_yy plus eps_zz"]
    R["RIGID BODY ROTATION<br/>omega_ij half of u_i_j minus u_j_i"]

    U --> N
    U --> S
    N --> V
    S --> R
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The strain-displacement relations decompose the displacement gradient $\partial u_i/\partial x_j$ into a **symmetric part** (strain $\varepsilon_{ij}$) and an **antisymmetric part** (rigid body rotation $\omega_{ij}$). This is the *Cauchy-Stokes decomposition* — a beautiful result that any continuous deformation can be uniquely split into pure strain + pure rotation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU pattern:** 2-mark conceptual + 1-mark diagram / example. Crisp, no derivations.

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State the three-dimensional equilibrium equations in Cartesian coordinates for a body subjected to body forces. Mention the units of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three Cauchy equilibrium equations in Cartesian coordinates $(x, y, z)$ for a body subjected to body force components $X, Y, Z$ per unit volume are:

$$
\frac{\partial \sigma_{xx}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xz}}{\partial z} + X = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \sigma_{yy}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yz}}{\partial z} + Y = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \tau_{xz}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yz}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \sigma_{zz}}{\partial z} + Z = 0
$$

- **Units:** All stress terms have units of **N/m²/ m = N/m³** (stress gradient), and the body force $X, Y, Z$ has units of **N/m³** (force per unit volume). The equation is dimensionally consistent.

> **Mark split:** (1) Equation 1 — 1 mark. (2) Equations 2 and 3 — 1 mark. (3) Units — 1 mark.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define normal strain and shear strain. Write the strain-displacement relations for an infinitesimal deformation in Cartesian coordinates.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Normal strain** $\varepsilon_{ii}$ is the change in length per unit original length of a line element along a coordinate direction: $\varepsilon_{ii} = \partial u_i / \partial x_i$.
- **Shear strain (engineering)** $\gamma_{ij}$ is the decrease in the originally right angle between two perpendicular line elements. The tensor shear strain is half the engineering shear strain.

The six strain-displacement relations for infinitesimal deformation:

$$
\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \varepsilon_{zz} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{xy} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right)
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{yz} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}\right)
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{zx} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right)
$$

> **Mark split:** (1) Definitions of normal and shear strain — 1 mark. (2) Three normal strain relations — 1 mark. (3) Three shear strain relations — 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU pattern:** Each question is 14 marks, split into 2 sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) Derive the three Cauchy equilibrium equations for a 3D body subjected to body forces from first principles using an infinitesimal element.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** Consider a differential parallelepiped element of dimensions $dx, dy, dz$ at point $(x, y, z)$ in static equilibrium. Let stresses vary continuously with position.

2. **(1 mark)** Write the Taylor series expansion of each stress on the face opposite to its reference face. For example, on the $+dx$ face: $\sigma_{xx} + (\partial \sigma_{xx}/\partial x)\,dx$.

3. **(1 mark)** Compute the net force in the $x$-direction by summing contributions from all six faces, accounting for the direction of the outward normal. Use the fact that shear stresses $\tau_{xz}$ on the $\pm x$ faces do not contribute to $F_x$ (they act in the $z$ direction).

4. **(1 mark)** Divide the resulting equation by the element volume $dx\,dy\,dz$ and take the limit as the element shrinks to a point. This yields the first equilibrium equation.

5. **(1 mark)** Repeat the procedure for $F_y = 0$ and $F_z = 0$ to obtain the second and third equilibrium equations. Show the final form:

$$
\frac{\partial \sigma_{xx}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xz}}{\partial z} + X = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \sigma_{yy}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yz}}{\partial z} + Y = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \tau_{xz}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yz}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \sigma_{zz}}{\partial z} + Z = 0
$$

6. **(1 mark)** State the index notation: $\sigma_{ij,j} + F_i = 0$.

7. **(1 mark)** Apply moment equilibrium to derive the symmetry $\tau_{ij} = \tau_{ji}$, mentioning that the stress tensor has 6 independent components.

**(b) Derive the strain-displacement relations for a 3D body undergoing small deformations.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** Let a point $P(x, y, z)$ displace to $P'(x+u, y+v, z+w)$ where $u, v, w$ are small. Consider a neighbouring point $Q(x+dx, y, z)$.

2. **(1 mark)** Using Taylor expansion, the $x$-displacement of $Q$ is $u_Q = u + (\partial u/\partial x)\,dx$. Compute the change in length $dx' = dx + (\partial u/\partial x)\,dx$, and define normal strain $\varepsilon_{xx} = (dx' - dx)/dx = \partial u/\partial x$.

3. **(1 mark)** By cyclic symmetry, $\varepsilon_{yy} = \partial v/\partial y$ and $\varepsilon_{zz} = \partial w/\partial z$. State the three normal strain relations.

4. **(1 mark)** To derive shear strain, consider two perpendicular elements: $PQ$ along $+x$ and $PR$ along $+y$. After deformation, find the angles that $P'Q'$ and $P'R'$ make with the original axes. In the small-strain limit:

   - Rotation of $P'Q'$ from $x$-axis: $\alpha \approx \partial v/\partial x$.
   - Rotation of $P'R'$ from $y$-axis: $\beta \approx \partial u/\partial y$.

5. **(1 mark)** The total decrease in right angle is $\gamma_{xy} = \alpha + \beta = \partial u/\partial y + \partial v/\partial x$. The tensor shear strain is half: $\varepsilon_{xy} = \gamma_{xy}/2$.

6. **(1 mark)** By cyclic symmetry: $\gamma_{yz} = \partial v/\partial z + \partial w/\partial y$ and $\gamma_{zx} = \partial w/\partial x + \partial u/\partial z$.

7. **(1 mark)** State the index notation $\varepsilon_{ij} = (1/2)(u_{i,j} + u_{j,i})$ and the volumetric strain $e = \varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz}$.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) A 2D stress field is given by $\sigma_{xx} = ax^2 + by^2$, $\sigma_{yy} = cx^2 + dy^2$, $\tau_{xy} = -kxy$, where $a, b, c, d, k$ are constants. Determine the body force components $X$ and $Y$ required for equilibrium if the body forces are functions of $x$ and $y$ only. Assume plain-stress conditions.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** For 2D plane-stress equilibrium with body force $(X, Y)$:

$$
\frac{\partial \sigma_{xx}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial y} + X = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \sigma_{yy}}{\partial y} + Y = 0
$$

2. **(1 mark)** Compute partial derivatives:

$$
\frac{\partial \sigma_{xx}}{\partial x} = 2ax, \quad \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial y} = -kx
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial x} = -ky, \quad \frac{\partial \sigma_{yy}}{\partial y} = 2dy
$$

3. **(1 mark)** Substitute into the first equation:

$$
2ax + (-kx) + X = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{X = (k - 2a)\,x}
$$

4. **(1 mark)** Substitute into the second equation:

$$
-ky + 2dy + Y = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{Y = (k - 2d)\,y}
$$

5. **(1 mark)** Discuss the special case $k = 2a = 2d$ (constant body force or zero body force). State the physical interpretation: if $X = 0$ and $Y = 0$, then $k = 2a$ and $k = 2d$ must hold simultaneously, requiring $a = d$.

6. **(1 mark)** Discuss dimensional analysis: $a, b, c, d$ have units of **N/m⁴** (since $\sigma$ has units of N/m² and $x^2$ has units of m²), and $k$ has units of **N/m³**.

7. **(1 mark)** Verification check: at $x = 0$ and $y = 0$, the body force vanishes, consistent with a stress-free origin.

**(b) The displacement field in a 2D body is given by $u = \alpha xy$, $v = \beta x^2 + \gamma y^2$, where $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are constants. Determine the strain field $\varepsilon_{xx}, \varepsilon_{yy}, \gamma_{xy}$.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** State the 2D strain-displacement relations:

$$
\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}
$$

2. **(1 mark)** Compute partial derivatives:

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \alpha y, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \alpha x
$$

$$
\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 2\beta x, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 2\gamma y
$$

3. **(1 mark)** Normal strain in $x$:

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_{xx} = \alpha y}
$$

4. **(1 mark)** Normal strain in $y$:

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_{yy} = 2\gamma y}
$$

5. **(1 mark)** Engineering shear strain:

$$
\boxed{\gamma_{xy} = \alpha x + 2\beta x = (\alpha + 2\beta)\,x}
$$

6. **(1 mark)** Tensor shear strain: $\varepsilon_{xy} = \gamma_{xy}/2 = (\alpha + 2\beta)x/2$.

7. **(1 mark)** Volumetric strain: $e = \varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} = \alpha y + 2\gamma y = (\alpha + 2\gamma)\,y$. Note the spatial dependence — the volume change varies with $y$, meaning this is an *inhomogeneous* deformation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in this module:**

1. **Sign convention error in stress terms on opposite faces** (loses up to 2 marks). Always remember: stresses on the *positive* face act in the *positive* direction; stresses on the *negative* face (where outward normal is negative) act in the *negative* direction of their first subscript. Use the Taylor expansion consistently.

2. **Forgetting the 1/2 factor in $\varepsilon_{xy}$ vs $\gamma_{xy}$** (loses 1 mark). $\gamma$ = engineering shear strain (no 1/2); $\varepsilon$ = tensor shear strain (has 1/2). KTU papers often ask for the *engineering* shear strain $\gamma_{xy}$ — read the question carefully.

3. **Skipping the moment-equilibrium step** in derivations of equilibrium (loses 2 marks). The symmetry $\tau_{ij} = \tau_{ji}$ is *derived*, not *assumed*. Examiners expect at least one line of moment balance.

4. **In 2D problems, using $\sigma_{zz} = 0$ for plane stress vs $\varepsilon_{zz} = 0$ for plane strain** (loses 2 marks). These are two fundamentally different idealizations. KTU explicitly states which one in the problem.

5. **Confusing body force per unit volume with body force per unit mass.** Body force per unit volume $F$ (N/m³) = density $\rho$ (kg/m³) × body force per unit mass $b$ (m/s²). For gravity: $F_z = -\rho g$.

6. **Not mentioning units** in numerical problems (loses 1 mark in 14-mark questions where marks are reserved for "dimensional analysis").

7. **Writing equations without indicating they are at the point level** (after dividing by $dx\,dy\,dz$ and taking the limit). Always write "in the limit as $dx, dy, dz \to 0$".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 Equilibrium & Strain-Displacement**

- ☐ The **3 Cauchy equilibrium equations** in 3D Cartesian: first-order linear PDEs in stress with body force source term.
- ☐ Derive equilibrium using a **differential parallelepiped + Taylor expansion + force balance**.
- ☐ Moment balance ⇒ **stress tensor symmetry** $\tau_{ij} = \tau_{ji}$ ⇒ 6 independent stress components.
- ☐ Index notation: $\sigma_{ij,j} + F_i = 0$ — succinct form of all three equations.
- ☐ For 2D problems (plane stress / plane strain), only 2 of the 3 equations are needed; the third is automatically satisfied by symmetry arguments or idealization.
- ☐ The **6 strain-displacement relations** (3 normal + 3 shear) are *kinematic* — they involve only geometry, no material property.
- ☐ Derive normal strain by considering a line element's length change after deformation; derive shear strain by considering the change in angle between two perpendicular elements.
- ☐ **Engineering shear strain** $\gamma_{ij} = \partial u_i/\partial x_j + \partial u_j/\partial x_i$; **Tensor shear strain** $\varepsilon_{ij} = \gamma_{ij}/2$.
- ☐ The displacement gradient tensor $\partial u_i/\partial x_j$ decomposes uniquely into **symmetric strain tensor** $\varepsilon_{ij}$ and **antisymmetric rotation tensor** $\omega_{ij}$ (Cauchy-Stokes decomposition).
- ☐ **Volumetric strain** $e = \varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz} = \nabla \cdot \vec{u}$ — the *dilatation*.
- ☐ In small-strain theory, **products of partial derivatives of displacements are neglected** — this is the key linearization assumption.
- ☐ Body force $X, Y, Z$ has units of **N/m³**; in gravity, $Z = -\rho g$.
- ☐ For cylindrical/polar coordinates, transform the operators using $\nabla = \hat{r}\,\partial/\partial r + \hat{\theta}(1/r)\,\partial/\partial\theta + \hat{z}\,\partial/\partial z$ — beyond the scope of this sub-topic but referenced in Module 2 advanced content.
- ☐ Equilibrium equations are **3** equations, strain-displacement relations are **6**, and these 9 equations are *compatibility-free*; they hold for *any* continuous material regardless of constitutive behaviour.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Airy's stress function and equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET415 - ADVANCED MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Airy's stress function and equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Airy's Stress Function** is a scalar potential function, denoted by $\phi(x, y)$, introduced by the British Astronomer Royal **Sir George Biddell Airy in 1862**, which uniquely represents the state of stress in a two-dimensional (plane stress or plane strain) elastic body. The stress components are expressed as the second-order partial derivatives of this single scalar function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> An *Airy stress function* $\phi(x, y)$ is a biharmonic function (i.e., $\nabla^4 \phi = 0$) from which the entire 2D stress field can be derived through differentiation, thereby reducing a complex 3-unknown elasticity problem to the determination of a single scalar field.

The stress components in terms of Airy's stress function $\phi$ are:

$$\sigma_x = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2}, \quad \sigma_y = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}, \quad \tau_{xy} = -\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x \, \partial y}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a single function?** In 2D elasticity, we have 3 unknown stress components ($\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$) but only 2 equilibrium equations. Equilibrium alone is insufficient. The compatibility condition supplies the missing constraint, and the *Airy stress function* elegantly packages all three equations (2 equilibrium + 1 compatibility) into one elegant **biharmonic operator** condition.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **hilly landscape** where the **elevation (height)** at every point is given by a single function $h(x, y)$.

- The **slope** in the $x$-direction is $\partial h / \partial x$, and in the $y$-direction is $\partial h / \partial y$.
- The **curvature** of the landscape gives us useful information — the second derivatives $\partial^2 h / \partial x^2$, $\partial^2 h / \partial y^2$, and $\partial^2 h / \partial x \partial y$.

In Airy's stress function, the **stresses inside a material** are analogous to the **curvatures of this landscape**:

| Landscape Quantity | Solid Mechanics Quantity |
|---|---|
| Elevation $\phi$ | Airy stress function |
| $\partial^2 \phi / \partial y^2$ | Normal stress $\sigma_x$ |
| $\partial^2 \phi / \partial x^2$ | Normal stress $\sigma_y$ |
| $-\partial^2 \phi / \partial x \partial y$ | Shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ |

Just as the topography of a landscape is governed by a single height map that satisfies the **biharmonic equation** (a special "smoothness" condition ensuring no cracks or sharp creases), the stress state inside an elastic body must also satisfy a **biharmonic smoothness condition** to ensure the strains are continuous and physically realizable.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Example:** When designing a **dam wall**, **aircraft wing spar**, or **pressure vessel nozzle**, engineers use Airy's stress function to find stress concentrations. It is the foundation of photoelasticity experiments where engineers use transparent models (like epoxy) and polarized light to view *isochromatic lines* — which are exactly contours of $\phi = \text{constant}$ or related stress invariants.

## 1.3 The Governing Equation: The Biharmonic Equation

The Airy stress function must satisfy the **biharmonic equation**:

$$\nabla^4 \phi = \frac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial x^4} + 2\frac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial x^2 \, \partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial y^4} = 0$$

This is a fourth-order partial differential equation (PDE), and its solution gives the complete stress field.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Problem Setup — Why We Need a Stress Function

In **2D elasticity (plane stress or plane strain)**, the equilibrium equations (ignoring body forces) are:

$$\frac{\partial \sigma_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial y} = 0 \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$\frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \sigma_y}{\partial y} = 0 \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Unknowns:** 3 stress components ($\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$)
**Equations:** Only 2 equilibrium equations.

This is a **statically indeterminate** system at the stress level. We need the **compatibility equation** derived from the strain-displacement relations to close the system.

The **compatibility equation** (from the Beltrami-Michell formulation) in 2D, in the absence of body forces, is:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \sigma_x}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \sigma_y}{\partial x^2} = \frac{\partial^2 \tau_{xy}}{\partial x \, \partial y} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

## 2.2 The Logic Behind Defining Airy's Stress Function

Airy observed that if we define a scalar function $\phi(x, y)$ such that:

- $\sigma_x = \partial^2 \phi / \partial y^2$
- $\sigma_y = \partial^2 \phi / \partial x^2$

Then by automatic **cross-differentiation** (Schwarz's theorem), equation (1) becomes:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2}\right) + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial y} = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{\partial^3 \phi}{\partial x \, \partial y^2} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial y} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau_{xy} = -\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x \, \partial y} + C_1(y)$$

Choosing $C_1(y) = 0$ (it is absorbed into the stress function), we get $\tau_{xy} = -\partial^2 \phi / \partial x \partial y$.

Substituting this into equation (2) confirms it is automatically satisfied.

**The compatibility equation (3)** then reduces to the **biharmonic equation**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No.** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Notes** |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| 1 | Normal stress $\sigma_x$ | $\sigma_x = \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2}$ | Units: $\text{N/m}^2$ (Pa) |
| 2 | Normal stress $\sigma_y$ | $\sigma_y = \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}$ | Units: $\text{N/m}^2$ (Pa) |
| 3 | Shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ | $\tau_{xy} = -\dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x \, \partial y}$ | Units: $\text{N/m}^2$ (Pa) |
| 4 | **Biharmonic equation** | $\nabla^4 \phi = 0$ | Mandatory governing PDE |
| 5 | Expanded form | $\dfrac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial x^4} + 2\dfrac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial x^2 \partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial y^4} = 0$ | 4th order PDE |
| 6 | Polynomial degree rule | If boundary loads are polynomial of degree $n$, then $\phi$ must be a polynomial of degree $(n+2)$ | Vital for problem selection |
| 7 | Resultant force on boundary (x-face) | $F_x = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\bigg\vert_A^B$ | Integral form |
| 8 | Resultant force on boundary (y-face) | $F_y = -\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_A^B$ | Integral form |
| 9 | Moment about origin on boundary | $M = \left[\phi + y \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} - x \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right]_A^B$ | Goursat's formula |
| 10 | Polar form of biharmonic | $\nabla^4 \phi = \left(\dfrac{\partial^2}{\partial r^2} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial^2}{\partial \theta^2}\right)^2 \phi = 0$ | Used for axisymmetric problems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** The polynomial degree rule (Item 6) is **THE most frequently tested fact**. If a beam has a linearly distributed load (degree 1), $\phi$ must be of **degree 3**. If uniform load (degree 0), $\phi$ must be of **degree 2**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Engineering Field** | **Application of Airy's Stress Function** |
|---|---|
| **Civil Engineering** | Stress analysis of **dam abutments, retaining walls, deep beams** with openings |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | **Notch stress concentration** in plates, stress around **press-fit holes** |
| **Aerospace Engineering** | Stress analysis of **wing spars** with cutouts, **fuselage frames** |
| **Pressure Vessel Design** | Local stresses around **nozzle reinforcements** and **branch connections** |
| **Geomechanics** | Stress distribution around **tunnels** (Kirsch's problem) and **boreholes** |
| **Microelectronics** | Stress fields in **thin films** and **MEMS** devices |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Biharmonic Equation from Compatibility

**Objective:** To show that if stresses are derived from a stress function $\phi$, the 2D compatibility equation reduces to $\nabla^4 \phi = 0$.

### Step 1: Start with the strain-displacement relations (2D)

In the absence of rigid body rotation concerns, the 2D strain components are:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \varepsilon_y = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Strain Compatibility Condition

For single-valued, continuous displacements, the **compatibility equation** is:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \varepsilon_x}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \varepsilon_y}{\partial x^2} = \frac{\partial^2 \gamma_{xy}}{\partial x \, \partial y}$$

### Step 3: Substitute Hooke's Law (Plane Stress Case)

For plane stress ($\sigma_z = 0$), Hooke's law gives:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_x - \nu \sigma_y), \quad \varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_y - \nu \sigma_x), \quad \gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}$$

where $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ is the shear modulus.

### Step 4: Substitute into the compatibility equation

$$\frac{1}{E}\left(\frac{\partial^2 \sigma_x}{\partial y^2} - \nu \frac{\partial^2 \sigma_y}{\partial y^2}\right) + \frac{1}{E}\left(\frac{\partial^2 \sigma_y}{\partial x^2} - \nu \frac{\partial^2 \sigma_x}{\partial x^2}\right) = \frac{1}{G} \frac{\partial^2 \tau_{xy}}{\partial x \, \partial y}$$

Since $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$, we have $1/G = 2(1+\nu)/E$. Multiplying the entire equation by $E$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial^2 \sigma_x}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \sigma_y}{\partial x^2}\right) - \nu\left(\frac{\partial^2 \sigma_y}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \sigma_x}{\partial x^2}\right) = 2(1+\nu) \frac{\partial^2 \tau_{xy}}{\partial x \, \partial y}$$

### Step 5: Substitute the Airy stress function expressions

Substituting $\sigma_x = \partial^2 \phi / \partial y^2$, $\sigma_y = \partial^2 \phi / \partial x^2$, $\tau_{xy} = -\partial^2 \phi / (\partial x \partial y)$:

$$\frac{\partial^2}{\partial y^2}\left(\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2}\right) + \frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}\left(\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}\right) - \nu\left[\frac{\partial^2}{\partial y^2}\left(\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}\right) + \frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}\left(\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2}\right)\right] = -2(1+\nu)\frac{\partial^2}{\partial x \partial y}\left(\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x \partial y}\right)$$

### Step 6: Simplify using notation

$$\frac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial y^4} + \frac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial x^4} - 2\nu \frac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial x^2 \partial y^2} = -2(1+\nu) \frac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial x^2 \partial y^2}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial x^4} + \frac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial y^4} + 2 \frac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial x^2 \partial y^2} = 0$$

### Step 7: Recognize the biharmonic operator

$$\boxed{\nabla^4 \phi = 0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** The Poisson's ratio $\nu$ **cancels out**! This is a remarkable property — the biharmonic equation governing the stress function is **independent of material properties** for the 2D case in the absence of body forces. This means the *stress distribution* depends only on the *geometry and boundary conditions*, not on whether the material is steel, aluminum, or epoxy.

## 3.2 General Polynomial Form of Airy's Stress Function

Since $\nabla^4 \phi = 0$ is a linear, homogeneous PDE, we can build solutions as polynomials. The **most general polynomial stress function** is:

$$\phi(x, y) = \frac{a_0}{2} x^2 + b_0 x y + \frac{c_0}{2} y^2 + \frac{a_1}{6} x^3 + \frac{b_1}{2} x^2 y + \frac{c_1}{2} x y^2 + \frac{d_1}{6} y^3 + \frac{a_2}{24} x^4 + \frac{b_2}{6} x^3 y + \frac{c_2}{4} x^2 y^2 + \frac{d_2}{6} x y^3 + \frac{e_2}{24} y^4 + \ldots$$

### Substituting the polynomial into $\nabla^4 \phi = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla^4 \phi &= a_2 + 2 c_2 + e_2 + (\text{higher order terms}) = 0 \\
&\Rightarrow a_2 + 2c_2 + e_2 = 0
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Implication:** Not every polynomial is acceptable. The **constant $a_2 + 2c_2 + e_2$** must be zero. This **filters out 1 redundant coefficient** in the polynomial. For the typical "cubic" stress function used in beam problems, this constraint is satisfied automatically.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Classic Stress Function Problems in KTU Syllabus

| **Stress Function $\phi$** | **Loading Scenario** | **Biharmonic Satisfied?** | **KTU Relevance** |
|---|---|:---:|:---:|
| $\phi = \dfrac{P}{2\pi} \left[\dfrac{1}{2}(x^2 + y^2) - y^2 \ln r\right]$ | Concentrated force on semi-infinite plate | ✓ | High |
| $\phi = \dfrac{q}{24}(2y^3 - 3xy^2 + x^2 y)$ | Cantilever with UDL | ✓ | **Very High** |
| $\phi = \dfrac{P}{\pi}(xy \arctan(y/x) - \text{polar form})$ | Concentrated force in infinite plate (Flamant) | ✓ | High |
| $\phi = A r^2 \ln r + B r^2 + C \ln r + D$ | Axisymmetric Kirsch problem | ✓ | High |
| $\phi = \dfrac{a_0 x^2}{2} + b_0 xy + \dfrac{c_0 y^2}{2}$ | Pure bending / rigid body motion (no internal stress) | ✓ | Medium |

## 3.4 Boundary Conditions in Terms of Stress Function

For a boundary curve with outward unit normal $(l, m)$ and arc-length parameter $s$, **Michell's boundary conditions** state that the prescribed tractions are equivalent to specifying the function and its normal derivative up to a linear term:

$$\phi = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} = 0 \quad \text{(for a specific class of problems with zero tractions)}$$

In the more general **Goursat's formulation**, on a boundary:

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_A^B \sigma_x \, dy &= \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\bigg\vert_A^B \\
\int_A^B \tau_{xy} \, dy &= -\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_A^B + f_1(x) \\
\int_A^B (\sigma_x \, dy \cdot x - \tau_{xy} \, dy \cdot y) &= [\phi + y f_1(x) + f_2(x)]_A^B
\end{aligned}$$

where $f_1(x)$ and $f_2(x)$ are arbitrary linear functions that vanish for **simply connected domains** with the origin at a point on the boundary.

---
