# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**State the biharmonic equation in Cartesian coordinates. Explain why Airy's stress function must satisfy it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The biharmonic equation in Cartesian coordinates for Airy's stress function $\varphi(x, y)$ is:

$$\nabla^4 \varphi = \frac{\partial^4 \varphi}{\partial x^4} + 2\frac{\partial^4 \varphi}{\partial x^2 \partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^4 \varphi}{\partial y^4} = 0$$

**Why it must be satisfied (2 marks):** Any $\varphi$ twice differentiable gives a stress field that **automatically satisfies equilibrium**. To also satisfy **strain compatibility** (which is required for a unique, continuous displacement field), the stress components must satisfy the Beltrami–Michell compatibility equations. For 2D problems with no body forces, these reduce exactly to $\nabla^4 \varphi = 0$. Thus, satisfying the biharmonic equation guarantees both equilibrium and compatibility.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**For a circular hole of radius $a$ in a large plate under uniaxial tension $\sigma$, state the maximum hoop stress and the stress concentration factor. Why is this result independent of the hole size?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

From the Kirsch solution, the hoop stress at the hole boundary is:

$$\sigma_{\theta\theta}\big\vert_{r=a} = \sigma(1 - 2 \cos 2\theta)$$

The maximum value occurs at $\theta = \pi/2$ (top/bottom of hole, perpendicular to load):

$$\sigma_{\theta\theta}^{\max} = \sigma(1 - 2 \cos \pi) = \sigma(1 + 2) = 3\sigma$$

The **stress concentration factor** is:

$$K_t = \frac{\sigma_{\theta\theta}^{\max}}{\sigma} = \frac{3\sigma}{\sigma} = 3$$

**Independence of hole size (1 mark):** The Kirsch solution is for an *infinite* plate, where the only length scale is the hole radius $a$. After non-dimensionalization by $a$, all stress ratios are functions of $r/a$ alone — hence $K_t = 3$ regardless of the absolute size of the hole.

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Using Airy's stress function approach, derive the stress distribution in a cantilever beam of length $l$ and uniform depth $2h$ loaded by a concentrated force $P$ at the free end. Show that the result reduces to the elementary beam theory formula. (7 marks)

**(b)** A thick-walled steel cylinder has inner radius $a = 50$ mm, outer radius $b = 75$ mm, and is subjected to an internal pressure $p_i = 60$ MPa (no external pressure). Using Lame's equations, calculate the maximum hoop stress and the radial stress at the inner surface. Take $\nu = 0.3$. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a)

**Step 1: Stress function ansatz** (1 mark)

$$\varphi(x, y) = a x y^3 + b x^3 y + c x y + d y^3 + e x^3 + f y^2 + g x^2 + h x + i y + j$$

**Step 2: Apply $\sigma_y = 0$ on $y = \pm h$** (2 marks)

Yields: $b = 0$, $e = 0$, $g = 0$.

**Step 3: Apply $\tau_{xy} = 0$ on $y = \pm h$** (1 mark)

Yields: $c = -3 a h^2$, $b = 0$ (already).

**Step 4: Resultant moment and shear conditions** (2 marks)

$$M(x) = \int_{-h}^{h} \sigma_x \, y \, dy = 4 h^3 (a x + d) = -P(l - x)$$

$$\Rightarrow a = \frac{P}{4 h^3}, \quad d = -\frac{P l}{4 h^3}$$

**Step 5: Final stresses with $I = 2h^3/3$** (1 mark)

$$\sigma_x = -\frac{P y (l - x)}{I}, \quad \sigma_y = 0, \quad \tau_{xy} = \frac{P (h^2 - y^2)}{2 I}$$

**Reconciliation with elementary theory:** These match the **Euler–Bernoulli** bending formula $\sigma_x = M y / I$ exactly, and the parabolic shear distribution $\tau_{xy} = V(h^2 - y^2)/(2I)$. The elasticity-based derivation is **more rigorous** because it does not assume plane sections remain plane. **(end of 7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b)

**Given:** $a = 0.050$ m, $b = 0.075$ m, $p_i = 60$ MPa, $p_o = 0$, $\nu = 0.3$.

**Step 1: Apply Lame's formula** (2 marks)

For $p_o = 0$:

$$\sigma_r = \frac{p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\!\left(1 - \frac{b^2}{r^2}\right), \quad \sigma_\theta = \frac{p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\!\left(1 + \frac{b^2}{r^2}\right)$$

**Step 2: Compute geometric constants** (1 mark)

$$b^2 - a^2 = (0.075)^2 - (0.050)^2 = 5.625 \times 10^{-3} - 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 3.125 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$a^2 b^2 = (2.5 \times 10^{-3})(5.625 \times 10^{-3}) = 1.40625 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4$$

$$\frac{p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{60 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{3.125 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.150}{3.125 \times 10^{-3}} = 48 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Maximum hoop stress at $r = a$** (2 marks)

$$\sigma_\theta^{\max} = 48 \times \left(1 + \frac{5.625 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}\right) = 48 \times (1 + 2.25) = 48 \times 3.25$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_\theta^{\max} = 156 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 4: Radial stress at inner surface** (1 mark)

$$\sigma_r\big\vert_{r=a} = 48 \times (1 - 2.25) = 48 \times (-1.25) = -60 \text{ MPa} = -p_i \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Axial stress (closed ends, plane strain)** (1 mark)

$$\sigma_z = 2 \nu \cdot \frac{p_i a^2 - p_o b^2}{b^2 - a^2} = 2 \times 0.3 \times 48 = 28.8 \text{ MPa}$$

**Engineering check:** Max hoop stress = 156 MPa, well below typical steel yield (~250 MPa). The cylinder is safe. **(end of 7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Using the stress function method in polar coordinates, derive the stress distribution around a small circular hole of radius $a$ in an infinite plate subjected to uniform uniaxial tension $\sigma$ along the $x$-direction. State all boundary conditions explicitly and verify the boundary check at $r = a$ and at $r \to \infty$. (10 marks)

**(b)** For an aluminium plate (yield stress 240 MPa) under uniaxial tension of 80 MPa containing a small hole, determine the factor of safety. Justify whether the factor of safety changes if the hole diameter is doubled (assume "large plate" condition still holds). (4 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a)

**Step 1: Problem statement and BCs** (2 marks)

Two boundary conditions:
- At $r = a$: $\sigma_{rr} = 0$, $\tau_{r\theta} = 0$ (traction-free hole)
- At $r \to \infty$: $\sigma_{xx} = \sigma$, $\sigma_{yy} = 0$, $\tau_{xy} = 0$

**Step 2: Choice of stress function** (2 marks)

By symmetry, choose:

$$\varphi(r,\theta) = \frac{\sigma}{4}\!\left(2 r^2 + \frac{a^4}{r^2} - a^2\right) + \frac{\sigma}{4}\!\left(2 r^2 + 2 a^2 - \frac{a^4}{r^2}\right)\!\cos 2\theta - \frac{\sigma a^2}{2} \ln r$$

This form is constructed from general solutions of $\nabla^4 \varphi = 0$ in polar coordinates.

**Step 3: Stress derivation** (4 marks)

After carrying out the polar-coordinate differentiations (as done in Section 3.3.4):

$$\sigma_{rr} = \frac{\sigma}{2}\!\left(1 - \frac{a^2}{r^2}\right) + \frac{\sigma}{2}\!\left(1 - \frac{4a^2}{r^2} + \frac{3 a^4}{r^4}\right)\!\cos 2\theta$$

$$\sigma_{\theta\theta} = \frac{\sigma}{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{a^2}{r^2}\right) - \frac{\sigma}{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{3 a^4}{r^4}\right)\!\cos 2\theta$$

$$\tau_{r\theta} = -\frac{\sigma}{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{2 a^2}{r^2} - \frac{3 a^4}{r^4}\right)\!\sin 2\theta$$

**Step 4: Boundary check at $r = a$** (1 mark)

$$\sigma_{rr}\big\vert_{r=a} = \frac{\sigma}{2}(0) + \frac{\sigma}{2}(1 - 4 + 3)\cos 2\theta = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\tau_{r\theta}\big\vert_{r=a} = -\frac{\sigma}{2}(1 + 2 - 3)\sin 2\theta = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\sigma_{\theta\theta}\big\vert_{r=a} = \sigma(1 - 2 \cos 2\theta)$$

Maximum at $\theta = \pi/2$: $\sigma_{\theta\theta}^{\max} = 3\sigma$

**Step 5: Boundary check at $r \to \infty$** (1 mark)

All $a/r$ terms vanish:

$$\sigma_{rr} \to \frac{\sigma}{2}(1 + \cos 2\theta) = \sigma \cos^2\theta = \sigma_{xx} = \sigma \quad \checkmark$$

**(end of 10 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b)

**Step 1: Maximum stress at the hole boundary** (1 mark)

$$\sigma_{\max} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{\text{applied}} = 3 \times 80 = 240 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Factor of Safety** (1 mark)

$$\text{FoS} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{yield}}}{\sigma_{\max}} = \frac{240}{240} = 1.0$$

The component is at the **threshold of yielding**.

**Step 3: Effect of doubling hole diameter** (2 marks)

The stress concentration factor $K_t = 3$ depends only on the **ratio** $r/a$, not on the absolute hole diameter. As long as the plate remains "large" (i.e., no interaction with other holes or free edges), doubling the hole diameter **does not change** $K_t = 3$. Therefore:

- $\sigma_{\max} = 3 \times 80 = 240$ MPa (unchanged)
- FoS = 1.0 (unchanged)

**However:** In practice, doubling the hole diameter reduces the net cross-sectional area of the plate, increasing the *nominal* stress in the reduced section. This is a *separate* effect from stress concentration. The FoS based on ultimate tensile strength in the net section would indeed be lower — but the **elastic SCF remains 3** by Kirsch's theory.

**(end of 4 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign convention errors in moment equation for cantilever beam:** Many students write $M(x) = P(l - x)$ but then forget the sign when substituting back. Always reconcile your sign convention with the chosen coordinate axes at the start.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $\sigma_z$ term in Lame's problem:** If the cylinder has **closed ends** (realistic case), the longitudinal stress is $\sigma_z = 2\nu A$ — it is **not** zero. Many students lose 1–2 marks by omitting this.
>
> 3. **Mistaking stress-concentration factor location:** The maximum stress in Kirsch's problem is at the hole boundary **perpendicular to the load direction** ($\theta = 90°$), not parallel to it. The point on the hole parallel to the load is in **compression**, not tension.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to verify the shear force condition** in the cantilever derivation: After finding $a$ and $d$ from the moment equation, always **plug back** to verify $V(x) = -P$. KTU examiners award partial credit for this check.
>
> 5. **Units consistency in Lame's numerical problems:** Keep all radii in **metres** (or all in mm), and pressures in **Pa** (or all in MPa). Mixed units are the #1 cause of arithmetic errors in 14-mark numerical questions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 Stress-Function Problems**

- [x] **Airy stress function:** Defined as $\varphi$ such that $\sigma_x = \partial^2\varphi/\partial y^2$, etc. Must satisfy $\nabla^4\varphi = 0$.

- [x] **Biharmonic equation:** $\nabla^4 \varphi = 0$ in 2D Cartesian; $\nabla^4 \varphi = 0$ in 2D polar with $\nabla^2 = \partial^2/\partial r^2 + (1/r)\partial/\partial r + (1/r^2)\partial^2/\partial \theta^2$.

- [x] **Cantilever beam (Cartesian polynomial):**
  - Use $\varphi$ of degree 4 (terms $xy^3$ and $x^3y$).
  - Boundary conditions: $\sigma_y = 0$ and $\tau_{xy} = 0$ on $y = \pm h$.
  - Resultant conditions: Match bending moment $M(x)$ and shear force $V(x)$ on any cross-section.
  - **Final:** $\sigma_x = -Py(l-x)/I$, $\sigma_y = 0$, $\tau_{xy} = P(h^2 - y^2)/(2I)$.

- [x] **Lame's thick cylinder (Polar, axisymmetric):**
  - Stress function: $\varphi = (A r^2/2) + B \ln r$.
  - Boundary conditions: $\sigma_r = -p_i$ at $r = a$, $\sigma_r = -p_o$ at $r = b$.
  - **Final:** $\sigma_r = A - B/r^2$, $\sigma_\theta = A + B/r^2$ with $A = (p_i a^2 - p_o b^2)/(b^2 - a^2)$ and $B = (p_i - p_o) a^2 b^2/(b^2 - a^2)$.
  - Max hoop stress at **inner surface** $r = a$.
  - For plane strain (closed ends): $\sigma_z = 2\nu A$.

- [x] **Kirsch problem (Polar, 2θ-symmetric):**
  - Stress function must contain $\cos 2\theta$ terms.
  - Boundary conditions: $\sigma_{rr} = 0$, $\tau_{r\theta} = 0$ at $r = a$; far-field uniaxial $\sigma$.
  - **Final hoop stress at hole boundary:** $\sigma_{\theta\theta}\big\vert_{r=a} = \sigma(1 - 2\cos 2\theta)$.
  - **Maximum tensile stress:** $3\sigma$ at $\theta = 90°$ (top/bottom of hole).
  - **Maximum compressive stress:** $-\sigma$ at $\theta = 0°$ (sides of hole).
  - **Stress concentration factor:** $K_t = 3$ (independent of hole size for "large plate").

- [x] **Engineering Memory Aids:**
  - *Cantilever beam:* "Bending is linear in $y$; shear is parabolic in $y$."
  - *Lame's cylinder:* "$1/r^2$ terms vanish at infinity; maximum stress is at the inside."
  - *Kirsch hole:* "Factor of 3 at the equator of the hole; negative on the meridian."

- [x] **Exam Strategy:**
  - Always draw the geometry and label axes/coordinate system.
  - State all boundary conditions **explicitly**.
  - For polynomial stress functions, list all coefficients at the start and **strike through** those eliminated by BCs.
  - Always **verify** boundary conditions on the final stress field (full marks await!).
  - Mention **Saint-Venant's principle** wherever end conditions are matched only in resultant.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Axisymmetric problems: thin cylinders pressurized from inside, and thick cylinders pressurized from inside and outside
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET415 - ADVANCED MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Axisymmetric problems: thin cylinders pressurized from inside, and thick cylinders pressurized from inside and outside

---

# AXISYMMETRIC CYLINDER PROBLEMS — KTU PREMIER NOTES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Axisymmetric Loading**: A loading condition where the geometry, material, and applied loads are all symmetric about a single central axis (the longitudinal axis of the cylinder). Every cross-section perpendicular to this axis experiences identical stress and strain states.

### Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Thin Cylinder**: A cylindrical pressure vessel in which the wall thickness **t** is small compared to the internal diameter **D**, specifically when **t ≤ D/20** (some texts use D/10). Under this condition, the radial stress is negligible compared to hoop and longitudinal stresses, and stresses are assumed **uniformly distributed** across the wall thickness.

**Thick Cylinder**: A cylindrical pressure vessel in which the wall thickness is comparable to or greater than the radius (typically **t > D/20**). Here, stress is **NOT uniform** across the thickness — it varies hyperbolically with radial distance, and radial stress is significant.

**Lamé's Equations**: The governing stress equations for thick cylinders under axisymmetric internal/external pressure, derived using the equilibrium of an elemental ring combined with radial displacement compatibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition Rule (Always Tested in KTU)**:
> - Internal pressure only: $\sigma_r = -p_i$ at $r=a$ and $\sigma_r = 0$ at $r=b$
> - Combined pressure: $\sigma_r = -p_i$ at $r=a$ and $\sigma_r = -p_o$ at $r=b$
> - Sign convention: **Tensile (Hoop) = POSITIVE**, **Compressive (Radial) = NEGATIVE**

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Balloon vs. Coconut" Intuition 🥥

Imagine pressing a rubber balloon (thin shell) versus cracking open a coconut (thick shell):

- **Thin Balloon**: When you inflate it, the rubber stretches **uniformly** all around. You can safely assume every fiber in the wall feels the same pull — this is the *membrane assumption*.
- **Thick Coconut Shell**: The inner fibers are crushed (compressed radially), but the outer fibers are stretched (hoop tension). The stress is NOT uniform — it **decays** from inside to outside.

> [!TIP]
> A simple rule: if you can press your thumb noticeably into the wall, it's **THIN**. If it feels like a cannon barrel, it's **THICK**. For KTU problems, always check the **t/D ratio** first before selecting your formula.

### Standard Engineering Metrics

| Metric | Thin Cylinder | Thick Cylinder |
|---|---|---|
| Ratio Check | $t \leq D/20$ | $t > D/20$ |
| Analysis Type | Membrane (uniform) | Lamé (varying) |
| Governing Theory | Simple equilibrium | Lamé + Compatibility |
| Typical Examples | LPG cylinders, water pipes | Hydraulic cylinders, gun barrels, pressure reactor shells |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Thin Cylinder Analysis (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Geometric Setup**
Consider a thin cylindrical shell of internal diameter **d**, wall thickness **t**, length **L**, subjected to internal pressure **p**.

**Step 2 — Hoop (Circumferential) Stress Derivation Logic**
- Cut the cylinder along a longitudinal plane.
- The internal pressure acts on the projected area **d × L**.
- The resisting force comes from **two** walls (one on each side), each of cross-sectional area **t × L**.
- Equilibrium gives the hoop stress.

**Step 3 — Longitudinal (Axial) Stress Derivation Logic**
- Cut the cylinder along a transverse plane.
- The internal pressure acts on the circular end cap area $\frac{\pi}{4}d^2$.
- The resisting force comes from the entire annular cross-section $\pi d t$.
- Equilibrium gives the longitudinal stress.

**Step 4 — Failure Theories Applied in KTU**
- **Maximum Principal Stress Theory**: $\sigma_1 = \sigma_h$
- **Maximum Shear Stress Theory**: $\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2} = \frac{pd}{8t}$
- **Maximum Strain Energy / Distortion Energy**: Use Von-Mises equivalent stress

### 2.2 Thick Cylinder Analysis (Lamé's Framework)

**Step 1 — Equilibrium of Elemental Ring**
Consider a small elemental ring of radius **r**, thickness **dr**, and unit length. The forces acting are:
- Radial stress $\sigma_r$ on inner face → outward force $= \sigma_r \cdot r \cdot d\theta$
- Radial stress $(\sigma_r + d\sigma_r)$ on outer face → inward force $= (\sigma_r + d\sigma_r)(r + dr)d\theta$
- Hoop stress $\sigma_h$ on side faces → inward radial component

**Step 2 — Simplified Equilibrium**
After expanding and neglecting second-order infinitesimals:

$$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_h}{r} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compatibility / Displacement Assumption**
The radial displacement **u** is given by:

$$u = \frac{1}{E}\left[(1-\nu)A r + (1+\nu)\frac{B}{r}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Strain-Displacement Relations**
$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{du}{dr}, \quad \varepsilon_h = \frac{u}{r}$$

**Step 5 — Hooke's Law for Plane Strain/Stress**
Apply generalized Hooke's law in cylindrical coordinates.

**Step 6 — Lamé's Stress Equations (Final Result)**

$$\boxed{\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = A + \frac{B}{r^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The constants **A** and **B** are determined from the **boundary conditions** at the inner and outer radii. They are the SINGLE most important numerical exercise in any KTU thick cylinder problem.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

#### A. Thin Cylinder Formulae (Membrane Theory)

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hoop Stress | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pd}{2t}$ | N/mm² or MPa | Burst resistance in circumferential direction |
| Longitudinal Stress | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{pd}{4t}$ | N/mm² or MPa | End-cap separation resistance |
| Hoop Strain | $\varepsilon_h = \dfrac{pd}{2tE}(1 - \nu/2)$ | — | Dimensional change |
| Volumetric Strain | $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{pd}{4tE}(5 - 4\nu)$ | — | Capacity change in tanks |
| Efficiency of Joint | $\eta = \dfrac{pd}{2t \cdot \sigma_t}$ | — | Welded/riveted joint design |

#### B. Thick Cylinder Formulae (Lamé's Theory)

| Quantity | Formula | Boundary Condition Source |
|---|---|---|
| Radial Stress | $\sigma_r = A - \dfrac{B}{r^2}$ | From equilibrium + compatibility |
| Hoop (Tangential) Stress | $\sigma_h = A + \dfrac{B}{r^2}$ | From equilibrium + compatibility |
| Radial Displacement | $u = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[(1-\nu)Ar + (1+\nu)\dfrac{B}{r}\right]$ | From Hooke's law |
| Max Hoop Stress (at $r=a$) | $\sigma_{h,max} = \dfrac{p_i a^2(b^2+a^2) - 2p_o b^2 a^2}{(b^2-a^2)a^2}$ | Inner radius |
| Max Shear Stress | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_h - \sigma_r}{2} = \dfrac{B}{r^2}$ | At inner surface |

#### C. Constants A and B for Various Loading Conditions

| Loading Case | Constant A | Constant B |
|---|---|---|
| Internal pressure only | $\dfrac{p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2}$ | $\dfrac{p_i a^2 b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$ |
| External pressure only | $\dfrac{-p_o b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$ | $\dfrac{-p_o a^2 b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$ |
| Combined (internal + external) | $\dfrac{p_i a^2 - p_o b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$ | $\dfrac{(p_i - p_o) a^2 b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick for A & B**: Think of **A** as the "average" stress at infinity and **B** as the "concentration" term. At $r \to \infty$, both stresses tend to **A**. At the bore, the hoop stress is always maximum (most positive).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Component | Theory Used |
|---|---|---|
| Oil & Gas | High-pressure pipelines, wellhead components | Thick cylinder |
| Hydraulic Systems | Cylinder barrels, accumulators | Thick cylinder |
| Defence | Gun barrels, missile casings | Thick (autofrettaged) |
| Domestic | LPG cylinders, water heaters | Thin cylinder |
| Power Plants | Boiler tubes, steam drums | Thin cylinder |
| Nuclear | Reactor pressure vessels | Thick cylinder |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION: Thin Cylinder Stresses

#### Derivation of Hoop Stress $\sigma_h$

**Starting Picture**: Take a thin cylindrical shell of internal diameter **d**, wall thickness **t**, and length **L**. Apply internal pressure **p**.

**Step 1**: Imagine cutting the cylinder along a diametrical longitudinal plane (i.e., slice it lengthwise into two halves). This exposes the cross-section of the wall — two rectangular strips, each of dimensions **L × t**.

**Step 2**: The internal pressure acts on the projected area of the cut, which is **d × L**. The total bursting force is:

$$F_{burst} = p \times d \times L$$

**Step 3**: This bursting force is resisted by the tensile force in the two halves of the cylindrical wall. Each half carries the hoop tension $\sigma_h$ over an area **t × L**, and there are two such halves:

$$F_{resist} = 2 \times \sigma_h \times t \times L$$

**Step 4**: Apply equilibrium $\sum F = 0$:

$$2 \sigma_h t L = p d L$$

**Step 5**: Cancel **L** from both sides:

$$2 \sigma_h t = p d$$

**Step 6**: Solve for $\sigma_h$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = \frac{p d}{2 t}}$$

---

#### Derivation of Longitudinal Stress $\sigma_l$

**Step 1**: Now imagine cutting the cylinder perpendicular to its axis (transverse cut), separating the cylinder from its end cap.

**Step 2**: The internal pressure **p** acts on the entire circular end cap area:

$$A_{end} = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2$$

**Step 3**: The total force pushing the end cap off is:

$$F_{axial} = p \times \frac{\pi}{4} d^2$$

**Step 4**: This force is resisted by the longitudinal stress acting over the **annular cross-section** of the cylindrical wall:

$$A_{wall} = \pi d t$$

**Step 5**: The resisting force is:

$$F_{resist} = \sigma_l \times \pi d t$$

**Step 6**: Apply equilibrium:

$$\sigma_l \cdot \pi d t = p \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2$$

**Step 7**: Cancel $\pi d$:

$$\sigma_l t = \frac{p d}{4}$$

**Step 8**: Solve for $\sigma_l$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_l = \frac{p d}{4 t}}$$

**Step 9 — Key Observation**:
$$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_l} = 2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hoop stress is **TWICE** the longitudinal stress. This is why pressure vessels fail along a **longitudinal seam** (perpendicular to hoop stress), not a circumferential seam. This 2:1 ratio is the foundation of all pressure vessel joint design efficiency calculations in KTU problems.

---

### 3.2 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION: Lamé's Equations for Thick Cylinder

#### Part A — Equilibrium of an Elemental Ring

**Step 1**: Consider a thick cylindrical shell with inner radius **a** and outer radius **b**. Subject to internal pressure $p_i$ and external pressure $p_o$.

**Step 2**: Isolate a small element of the cylinder wall — a wedge-shaped ring of:
- Inner radius: $r$
- Outer radius: $r + dr$
- Angle: $d\theta$ (subtended at the center)
- Length: 1 (unit length along axis)

**Step 3**: Identify the stresses acting on this element:
- Radial stress $\sigma_r$ on the **inner** curved face (acts outward, outward direction is positive)
- Radial stress $(\sigma_r + d\sigma_r)$ on the **outer** curved face (acts inward)
- Hoop stress $\sigma_h$ on the **two flat radial faces** (acts tangent to the curve)

**Step 4**: Compute the areas:
- Inner curved face area: $r \cdot d\theta \cdot 1 = r \, d\theta$
- Outer curved face area: $(r + dr) \cdot d\theta \cdot 1 = (r + dr) d\theta$
- Each flat radial face area: $dr \cdot 1 = dr$

**Step 5**: Sum forces in the radial direction. The net radial force must be zero (static equilibrium). The radial component of the hoop stress on the flat faces pulls the element **inward** (toward the center), each with component $\sigma_h \sin(d\theta/2) \cdot dr \approx \sigma_h \cdot \frac{d\theta}{2} \cdot dr$ (for small $d\theta$).

$$\begin{aligned}
(\sigma_r + d\sigma_r)(r + dr) d\theta &- \sigma_r \cdot r \cdot d\theta - 2 \sigma_h \cdot \frac{d\theta}{2} \cdot dr = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6**: Expand the first term using the product rule:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_r \cdot r \, d\theta &+ \sigma_r \cdot dr \, d\theta + r \cdot d\sigma_r \cdot d\theta + d\sigma_r \cdot dr \cdot d\theta \\
&- \sigma_r \cdot r \, d\theta - \sigma_h \, d\theta \, dr = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7**: Cancel $\sigma_r \cdot r \, d\theta$ from both sides. Neglect the second-order infinitesimal $d\sigma_r \cdot dr \cdot d\theta$:

$$\sigma_r \, dr \, d\theta + r \, d\sigma_r \, d\theta - \sigma_h \, d\theta \, dr = 0$$

**Step 8**: Divide through by $d\theta \, dr$:

$$\sigma_r + r \frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} - \sigma_h = 0$$

**Step 9**: Rearrange into the standard Lamé equilibrium form:

$$\boxed{r \frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \sigma_r - \sigma_h = 0}$$

This is the **Lamé Equilibrium Equation** — the foundation of all thick cylinder analysis.

---

#### Part B — Integration to Get the Stress Form

**Step 1**: From the equilibrium equation, express $\sigma_h$ in terms of $\sigma_r$:

$$\sigma_h = r \frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \sigma_r$$

**Step 2**: Notice that the left side of the equilibrium equation can be rewritten using the product rule:

$$\frac{d}{dr}(r \sigma_r) = \sigma_r + r \frac{d\sigma_r}{dr}$$

**Step 3**: Substitute this back:

$$\frac{d}{dr}(r \sigma_r) = \sigma_h$$

**Step 4**: To obtain a solvable equation, we need a second relationship between $\sigma_r$ and $\sigma_h$. This comes from **compatibility / displacement considerations** (plane stress assumption is standard for thin-walled structures; plane strain for long cylinders).

**Step 5**: The radial displacement **u** at radius **r** is related to strains by:

$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{du}{dr}, \quad \varepsilon_h = \frac{u}{r}$$

**Step 6**: For plane stress, Hooke's law in cylindrical coordinates gives:

$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_r - \nu \sigma_h)$$

$$\varepsilon_h = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_h - \nu \sigma_r)$$

**Step 7**: From the displacement-strain relations, eliminate **u** by differentiating $\varepsilon_h$ with respect to **r**:

$$\varepsilon_h = \frac{u}{r} \implies u = r \varepsilon_h \implies \frac{du}{dr} = \varepsilon_h + r \frac{d\varepsilon_h}{dr}$$

**Step 8**: But $\frac{du}{dr} = \varepsilon_r$, so:

$$\varepsilon_r = \varepsilon_h + r \frac{d\varepsilon_h}{dr}$$

**Step 9**: Substitute Hooke's law expressions:

$$\frac{1}{E}(\sigma_r - \nu \sigma_h) = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_h - \nu \sigma_r) + \frac{r}{E}\left(\frac{d\sigma_h}{dr} - \nu \frac{d\sigma_r}{dr}\right)$$

**Step 10**: Multiply through by **E** and simplify (the $1/E$ cancels):

$$\sigma_r - \nu \sigma_h = \sigma_h - \nu \sigma_r + r \frac{d\sigma_h}{dr} - \nu r \frac{d\sigma_r}{dr}$$

**Step 11**: Collect terms:

$$\sigma_r(1 + \nu) - \sigma_h(1 + \nu) = r \frac{d\sigma_h}{dr} - \nu r \frac{d\sigma_r}{dr}$$

$$(1+\nu)(\sigma_r - \sigma_h) = r \frac{d\sigma_h}{dr} - \nu r \frac{d\sigma_r}{dr}$$

**Step 12**: From the equilibrium equation: $\sigma_r - \sigma_h = -r \frac{d\sigma_r}{dr}$. Substitute this:

$$-(1+\nu) r \frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} = r \frac{d\sigma_h}{dr} - \nu r \frac{d\sigma_r}{dr}$$

**Step 13**: Cancel **r**:

$$-(1+\nu) \frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} = \frac{d\sigma_h}{dr} - \nu \frac{d\sigma_r}{dr}$$

**Step 14**: Rearrange:

$$\frac{d\sigma_h}{dr} = \frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} [-(1+\nu) + \nu] = -\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr}$$

**Step 15**: Integrate with respect to **r**:

$$\sigma_h = -\sigma_r + C_1$$

**Step 16**: Substitute into the equilibrium equation $r \frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \sigma_r - \sigma_h = 0$:

$$r \frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \sigma_r - (-\sigma_r + C_1) = 0$$

$$r \frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + 2\sigma_r - C_1 = 0$$

**Step 17**: Rearrange as a first-order linear ODE:

$$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{2}{r}\sigma_r = \frac{C_1}{r}$$

**Step 18**: This is a linear ODE. The integrating factor is $e^{\int \frac{2}{r}dr} = r^2$.

$$\frac{d}{dr}(r^2 \sigma_r) = C_1 r$$

**Step 19**: Integrate:

$$r^2 \sigma_r = \frac{C_1 r^2}{2} + C_2$$

$$\sigma_r = \frac{C_1}{2} - \frac{C_2}{r^2}$$

**Step 20**: Define $A = \frac{C_1}{2}$ and $B = C_2$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}}$$

**Step 21**: From Step 15, $\sigma_h = -\sigma_r + C_1 = -\sigma_r + 2A$:

$$\sigma_h = -\left(A - \frac{B}{r^2}\right) + 2A = A + \frac{B}{r^2}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = A + \frac{B}{r^2}}$$

These are **Lamé's Equations**.

---

#### Part C — Determining Constants A and B (General Case)

**Boundary Condition 1**: At $r = a$, $\sigma_r = -p_i$ (compressive radial stress equals the applied internal pressure).

$$-p_i = A - \frac{B}{a^2} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Boundary Condition 2**: At $r = b$, $\sigma_r = -p_o$ (compressive radial stress equals the applied external pressure).

$$-p_o = A - \frac{B}{b^2} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 1**: Subtract (ii) from (i):

$$-p_i + p_o = -\frac{B}{a^2} + \frac{B}{b^2}$$

$$p_o - p_i = B\left(\frac{1}{b^2} - \frac{1}{a^2}\right) = B\left(\frac{a^2 - b^2}{a^2 b^2}\right)$$

**Step 2**: Solve for **B**:

$$B = \frac{(p_o - p_i) a^2 b^2}{a^2 - b^2} = \frac{(p_i - p_o) a^2 b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

$$\boxed{B = \frac{(p_i - p_o) a^2 b^2}{b^2 - a^2}}$$

**Step 3**: Substitute **B** back into (i) to find **A**:

$$-p_i = A - \frac{(p_i - p_o) a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2) a^2}$$

$$-p_i = A - \frac{(p_i - p_o) b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

**Step 4**: Solve for **A**:

$$A = -p_i + \frac{(p_i - p_o) b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

$$A = \frac{-p_i(b^2 - a^2) + (p_i - p_o) b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

**Step 5**: Expand the numerator:

$$-p_i b^2 + p_i a^2 + p_i b^2 - p_o b^2 = p_i a^2 - p_o b^2$$

**Step 6**: Therefore:

$$\boxed{A = \frac{p_i a^2 - p_o b^2}{b^2 - a^2}}$$

---

#### Part D — Special Case: Internal Pressure Only

When $p_o = 0$:

$$A = \frac{p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

$$B = \frac{p_i a^2 b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

**Maximum Hoop Stress** occurs at the **inner radius** $r = a$:

$$\sigma_{h,max} = A + \frac{B}{a^2} = \frac{p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{p_i a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2) a^2}$$

$$\sigma_{h,max} = \frac{p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{p_i b^2}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{p_i (a^2 + b^2)}{b^2 - a^2}$$

**Minimum Hoop Stress** occurs at the **outer radius** $r = b$:

$$\sigma_{h,min} = A + \frac{B}{b^2} = \frac{p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{p_i a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2) b^2} = \frac{p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

$$\sigma_{h,min} = \frac{2 p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

**Stress Ratio Verification**:

$$\frac{\sigma_{h,min}}{\sigma_{h,max}} = \frac{2 a^2}{a^2 + b^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For thick cylinders, the hoop stress varies from a **maximum at the inner surface** to a **minimum at the outer surface**. For a thin cylinder where $b \approx a$, this ratio approaches 1, confirming the uniform stress assumption.

---

#### Part E — Radial Displacement Equation

Starting from Hooke's law and Lamé's equations, the radial displacement is:

$$u = \frac{1}{E}\left[(1-\nu) A r + (1+\nu) \frac{B}{r}\right]$$

**Derivation Outline**:
- From Hooke's law: $\varepsilon_h = \frac{u}{r} = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_h - \nu \sigma_r)$
- Substitute Lamé's stresses: $\varepsilon_h = \frac{1}{E}\left[(1-\nu)A + (1+\nu)\frac{B}{r^2}\right]$
- Multiply by **r**: $u = \frac{r}{E}\left[(1-\nu)A + (1+\nu)\frac{B}{r^2}\right]$
- Expand: $u = \frac{1}{E}\left[(1-\nu)Ar + (1+\nu)\frac{B}{r}\right]$

This equation is critical for **shrink-fit** and **compound cylinder** problems in KTU.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Thick Cylinder Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Geometry and Pressure Data] --> B{Is it Thin or Thick?}
    B -->|t less than D/20| C[Apply Thin Cylinder Membrane Theory]
    B -->|t greater than D/20| D[Apply Lamé Thick Cylinder Theory]
    C --> C1[Compute Hoop Stress sigma_h = pd/2t]
    C --> C2[Compute Longitudinal Stress sigma_l = pd/4t]
    C --> C3[Check Failure Criteria]
    D --> D1[Identify Boundary Conditions at r equals a and r equals b]
    D1 --> D2[Determine Constants A and B from Boundary Conditions]
    D2 --> D3[Compute Radial Stress sigma_r equals A minus B by r squared]
    D3 --> D4[Compute Hoop Stress sigma_h equals A plus B by r squared]
    D4 --> D5[Identify Max Stress at Inner Radius r equals a]
    D5 --> D6[Compute Radial Displacement if Required]
    D6 --> D7[Check Failure Criteria]
    C3 --> E[Output: Maximum Stress and Safety Factor]
    D7 --> E
```

### 4.2 Stress Distribution Profile in Thick Cylinder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InnerSurface[Inner Surface at r equals a]
        I1[Radial Stress sigma_r equals minus pi]
        I2[Hoop Stress sigma_h equals MAXIMUM]
        I3[Tangential tension dominates]
    end
    subgraph MiddleWall[Middle of Wall]
        M1[Radial Stress decreases]
        M2[Hoop Stress decreases gradually]
        M3[Stress gradient is non-linear]
    end
    subgraph OuterSurface[Outer Surface at r equals b]
        O1[Radial Stress sigma_r equals minus po or zero]
        O2[Hoop Stress sigma_h equals MINIMUM]
        O3[Outer fibers carry less load]
    end
    InnerSurface --> MiddleWall --> OuterSurface
```

### 4.3 Boundary Condition Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Applied Internal Pressure pi acting radially outward] --> BC1[At r equals a: sigma_r equals minus pi]
    P2[Applied External Pressure po acting radially inward] --> BC2[At r equals b: sigma_r equals minus po]
    BC1 --> SOLVE[Solve for Constants A and B]
    BC2 --> SOLVE
    SOLVE --> RESULT[Apply Lamé Equations to Get Stress Distribution]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Stress State: Thin vs Thick

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ThinView[Thin Cylinder Stress State]
        T1[Uniform across wall]
        T2[Hoop equals pd by 2t]
        T3[Longitudinal equals pd by 4t]
        T4[Radial Stress NEGLIGIBLE]
        T1 --> T2
        T1 --> T3
        T1 --> T4
    end
    subgraph ThickView[Thick Cylinder Stress State]
        TH1[Non-uniform across wall]
        TH2[Hoop MAXIMUM at inner surface]
        TH3[Hoop MINIMUM at outer surface]
        TH4[Radial Stress VARIES significantly]
        TH1 --> TH2
        TH1 --> TH3
        TH1 --> TH4
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between thin and thick cylinder with respect to stress distribution. Give one practical example of each.**

**Model Answer**:

| Parameter | Thin Cylinder | Thick Cylinder |
|---|---|---|
| Wall thickness ratio | $t \leq D/20$ | $t > D/20$ |
| Stress distribution | Uniform across thickness | Non-uniform (varies hyperbolically) |
| Radial stress | Neglected | Significant and considered |
| Theory used | Membrane theory | Lamé's theory |
| Maximum stress location | Anywhere — stress is constant | Always at inner surface |
| Examples | LPG gas cylinders, water pipes | Hydraulic press cylinders, gun barrels |

> [!NOTE]
> In a thin cylinder, hoop stress $\sigma_h = pd/(2t)$ and longitudinal stress $\sigma_l = pd/(4t)$ are constant across the wall thickness. In a thick cylinder, both $\sigma_h$ and $\sigma_r$ vary with radius **r** as per Lamé's equations.

**Marks Distribution**: Correct distinction = 2 marks, valid example = 1 mark. **[3 marks]**

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State Lamé's equations for a thick cylinder subjected to internal and external pressure. Write the expressions for the constants A and B in terms of pressure and radii.**

**Model Answer**:

Lamé's equations for a thick cylinder under combined internal pressure $p_i$ and external pressure $p_o$ are:

$$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}, \quad \sigma_h = A + \frac{B}{r^2}$$

where **r** is the radial distance from the cylinder axis.

**Constants from boundary conditions**:

$$A = \frac{p_i a^2 - p_o b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

$$B = \frac{(p_i - p_o) a^2 b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

where **a** = inner radius, **b** = outer radius.

**Marks Distribution**: Equations stated = 2 marks, A & B expressions = 1 mark. **[3 marks]**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern**: Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and typically has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice between two full 14-mark questions on the same module topic).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A copper pipe of 200 mm internal diameter and 12 mm wall thickness is subjected to an internal pressure of 4 MPa. Determine:**
**(a)** The hoop and longitudinal stresses in the pipe wall using thin cylinder theory. Comment on whether thin cylinder theory is applicable. **[7 marks]**
**(b)** The maximum and minimum hoop stresses, and the radial stress at the inner surface using Lamé's thick cylinder theory. Compare with part (a) results. **[7 marks]**

---

**Solution to Part (a) — Thin Cylinder Analysis**:

**Given**:
- Internal diameter $d = 200$ mm
- Wall thickness $t = 12$ mm
- Internal pressure $p = 4$ MPa

**Step 1**: Check thin cylinder applicability:

$$\frac{t}{D} = \frac{12}{200} = 0.06 = \frac{1}{16.67}$$

Compare with $D/20 = 200/20 = 10$ mm. Since $t = 12$ mm $> 10$ mm, the pipe is **NOT strictly thin** by the $D/20$ criterion. However, applying thin cylinder theory as a first approximation:

**Step 2**: Hoop stress:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{pd}{2t} = \frac{4 \times 200}{2 \times 12} = \frac{800}{24} = 33.33 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3**: Longitudinal stress:

$$\sigma_l = \frac{pd}{4t} = \frac{4 \times 200}{4 \times 12} = \frac{800}{48} = 16.67 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4**: Ratio check: $\sigma_h / \sigma_l = 33.33/16.67 = 2.0$ ✓ (confirms theory)

**Step 5**: Comment — Since $t/D = 1/16.67$ is close to $1/20$, thin cylinder theory gives a reasonable approximation, but Lamé's theory will provide more accurate values, particularly near the bore.

**[7 marks breakdown**: Hoop stress = 2 marks, Longitudinal stress = 2 marks, Applicability check = 2 marks, Comment = 1 mark]

---

**Solution to Part (b) — Thick Cylinder (Lamé) Analysis**:

**Given**:
- Inner radius $a = 100$ mm
- Outer radius $b = 100 + 12 = 112$ mm
- Internal pressure $p_i = 4$ MPa
- External pressure $p_o = 0$ (atmospheric, neglected)

**Step 1**: Compute $b^2 - a^2$:

$$b^2 = 112^2 = 12544 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$a^2 = 100^2 = 10000 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$b^2 - a^2 = 2544 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2**: Determine constant **A**:

$$A = \frac{p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{4 \times 10000}{2544} = \frac{40000}{2544} = 15.72 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3**: Determine constant **B**:

$$B = \frac{p_i a^2 b^2}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{4 \times 10000 \times 12544}{2544} = \frac{501760000}{2544} = 197,233 \text{ MPa·mm}^2$$

**Step 4**: Maximum hoop stress at $r = a = 100$ mm:

$$\sigma_{h,max} = A + \frac{B}{a^2} = 15.72 + \frac{197233}{10000} = 15.72 + 19.72 = 35.44 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5**: Minimum hoop stress at $r = b = 112$ mm:

$$\sigma_{h,min} = A + \frac{B}{b^2} = 15.72 + \frac{197233}{12544} = 15.72 + 15.72 = 31.45 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6**: Radial stress at the inner surface $r = a = 100$ mm:

$$\sigma_r = -p_i = -4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 7**: Comparison with thin cylinder results:

| Quantity | Thin Theory | Lamé (Max at inner) | Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hoop Stress | 33.33 MPa | 35.44 MPa | +6.3% |
| (Lamé avg) | — | (35.44+31.45)/2 = 33.45 MPa | ~0.4% |

**Observation**: The thin cylinder result falls between the maximum and minimum Lamé hoop stresses and is very close to their arithmetic mean. This confirms the thin cylinder formula represents an **average** stress across the wall.

**[7 marks breakdown**: Constants A, B = 2 marks, Max hoop = 2 marks, Min hoop + radial = 2 marks, Comparison table = 1 mark]

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A thick cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of 300 mm and outer diameter of 500 mm. It is subjected to an internal pressure of 60 MPa and external pressure of 20 MPa. Determine:**
**(a)** The constants A and B in Lamé's equations, and the radial and hoop stresses at the inner and outer surfaces. **[7 marks]**
**(b)** The radial displacement of the inner surface if $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.3$. **[7 marks]**

---

**Solution to Part (a) — Stress Computation**:

**Given**:
- Inner radius $a = 150$ mm
- Outer radius $b = 250$ mm
- Internal pressure $p_i = 60$ MPa
- External pressure $p_o = 20$ MPa

**Step 1**: Compute $b^2 - a^2$:

$$a^2 = 150^2 = 22500 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$b^2 = 250^2 = 62500 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$b^2 - a^2 = 40000 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2**: Determine constant **A**:

$$A = \frac{p_i a^2 - p_o b^2}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{60 \times 22500 - 20 \times 62500}{40000}$$

$$A = \frac{1350000 - 1250000}{40000} = \frac{100000}{40000} = 2.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3**: Determine constant **B**:

$$B = \frac{(p_i - p_o) a^2 b^2}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{(60 - 20) \times 22500 \times 62500}{40000}$$

$$B = \frac{40 \times 1406250000}{40000} = \frac{56250000000}{40000} = 1,406,250 \text{ MPa·mm}^2$$

**Step 4**: Radial and hoop stresses at the **inner surface** $r = a = 150$ mm:

$$\sigma_{r,a} = A - \frac{B}{a^2} = 2.5 - \frac{1406250}{22500} = 2.5 - 62.5 = -60 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{h,a} = A + \frac{B}{a^2} = 2.5 + \frac{1406250}{22500} = 2.5 + 62.5 = 65 \text{ MPa}$$

**Verification**: $\sigma_{r,a} = -p_i = -60$ MPa ✓ (boundary condition satisfied)

**Step 5**: Radial and hoop stresses at the **outer surface** $r = b = 250$ mm:

$$\sigma_{r,b} = A - \frac{B}{b^2} = 2.5 - \frac{1406250}{62500} = 2.5 - 22.5 = -20 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{h,b} = A + \frac{B}{b^2} = 2.5 + \frac{1406250}{62500} = 2.5 + 22.5 = 25 \text{ MPa}$$

**Verification**: $\sigma_{r,b} = -p_o = -20$ MPa ✓ (boundary condition satisfied)

**Summary Table**:

| Location | Radial Stress $\sigma_r$ | Hoop Stress $\sigma_h$ |
|---|---|---|
| Inner surface (r=150 mm) | -60 MPa | +65 MPa |
| Outer surface (r=250 mm) | -20 MPa | +25 MPa |

**[7 marks breakdown**: A, B calculations = 3 marks, Inner stresses = 2 marks, Outer stresses = 2 marks]

---

**Solution to Part (b) — Radial Displacement**:

**Given**: $E = 200$ GPa $= 200,000$ MPa, $\nu = 0.3$, $r = a = 150$ mm.

**Step 1**: Recall the radial displacement equation:

$$u = \frac{1}{E}\left[(1-\nu)Ar + (1+\nu)\frac{B}{r}\right]$$

**Step 2**: Compute each coefficient:

$$1 - \nu = 1 - 0.3 = 0.7$$

$$1 + \nu = 1 + 0.3 = 1.3$$

**Step 3**: Substitute $r = 150$ mm, $A = 2.5$ MPa, $B = 1,406,250$ MPa·mm²:

$$(1-\nu)Ar = 0.7 \times 2.5 \times 150 = 262.5 \text{ MPa·mm}$$

$$(1+\nu)\frac{B}{r} = 1.3 \times \frac{1406250}{150} = 1.3 \times 9375 = 12187.5 \text{ MPa·mm}$$

**Step 4**: Sum and divide by **E**:

$$u = \frac{262.5 + 12187.5}{200000} = \frac{12450}{200000} = 0.06225 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5**: Physical interpretation:

The inner surface displaces radially **outward** by approximately **0.062 mm** (since $u > 0$, the bore expands). This positive displacement indicates the cylinder **grows** in diameter under internal pressure — an important consideration for **interference fit** and **shrink-fit assembly** design.

**Final Answer**: $u_{inner} = +0.0623$ mm (outward)

**[7 marks breakdown**: Displacement formula = 2 marks, Coefficient computation = 2 marks, Substitution and answer = 2 marks, Physical interpretation = 1 mark]

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls in Axisymmetric Cylinder Problems**:
> 
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors** (lose 1–2 marks): KTU strictly follows **tensile = positive, compressive = negative**. Students often write $\sigma_r = +p_i$ at the inner surface — this is WRONG. Always write $\sigma_r = -p_i$.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to verify boundary conditions** (lose 1 mark): After computing A and B, ALWAYS check that $\sigma_r$ at $r=a$ gives $-p_i$ and at $r=b$ gives $-p_o$ (or 0). This shows the examiner you understand the derivation.
> 
> 3. **Mixing diameter and radius** (lose 1–2 marks): Thin cylinder formulas use **diameter d**, but Lamé's equations use **radius r**. Many students mistakenly use $d$ in Lamé's equations and lose marks.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting to check thin/thick applicability** (lose 1 mark): Always compute $t/D$ ratio first. If $t > D/20$, write a comment that thin cylinder theory is **not strictly applicable** before applying it.
> 
> 5. **Not specifying units** (lose 0.5 mark): Always write MPa or N/mm² explicitly. KTU examiners deduct marks for ambiguous units.
> 
> 6. **Long calculations skipped**: Show every step — KTU follows step-marking. A single equation with no intermediate work gets partial credit only.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Axisymmetric Cylinders**:

- [ ] **Thin Cylinder Trigger**: $t \leq D/20$ → use $\sigma_h = pd/2t$, $\sigma_l = pd/4t$
- [ ] **Thick Cylinder Trigger**: $t > D/20$ → use Lamé's equations
- [ ] **Lamé's Two Equations**:
  - $\sigma_r = A - B/r^2$
  - $\sigma_h = A + B/r^2$
- [ ] **Constants — Internal Pressure Only**: $A = p_i a^2/(b^2-a^2)$, $B = p_i a^2 b^2/(b^2-a^2)$
- [ ] **Constants — Combined Pressures**: $A = (p_i a^2 - p_o b^2)/(b^2-a^2)$, $B = (p_i - p_o)a^2 b^2/(b^2-a^2)$
- [ ] **Maximum Hoop Stress**: Always at **inner radius** $r = a$
- [ ] **Minimum Hoop Stress**: Always at **outer radius** $r = b$
- [ ] **Radial Stress**: Zero (or external pressure) at outer surface, $-p_i$ at inner surface
- [ ] **Hoop : Longitudinal Ratio** in thin cylinders = **2 : 1**
- [ ] **Sign Convention**: Tension POSITIVE, Compression NEGATIVE (radial stresses are negative)
- [ ] **Radial Displacement Formula**: $u = \frac{1}{E}[(1-\nu)Ar + (1+\nu)B/r]$
- [ ] **Boundary Condition Check**: $\sigma_r(a) = -p_i$, $\sigma_r(b) = -p_o$ (or 0)
- [ ] **Lamé Equilibrium**: $r(d\sigma_r/dr) + \sigma_r - \sigma_h = 0$
- [ ] **Real-World Memory Hook**: LPG cylinder = THIN, Gun barrel = THICK, Hydraulic ram = THICK

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most Frequently Asked KTU Question Pattern**: "A cylindrical pressure vessel of internal diameter D, wall thickness t, is subjected to internal pressure p. Find the stresses using (i) thin cylinder theory and (ii) Lamé's theory. Compare the results." — Practice this with at least 3 different numerical values before the exam.

**End of Module 3 Notes — Axisymmetric Cylinder Problems**

topics.title: Rotating disks. Unsymmetrical bending of straight beams possessing two axes of symmetry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET415 - ADVANCED MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rotating disks. Unsymmetrical bending of straight beams possessing two axes of symmetry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Rotating Disks — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Rotating Disk):**
> A **rotating disk** is a thin, axisymmetric solid of revolution (uniform or variable thickness) that spins about its central axis at a constant angular velocity **ω**. The centrifugal forces developed by the rotating mass generate **radial stress (σᵣ)** and **hoop/tangential stress (σ_θ)** within the disk material.

**Classification used in KTU syllabus:**
| Type | Geometry | Typical Application |
|------|----------|---------------------|
| **Solid uniform disk** | Plain circular plate, constant thickness *t* | Flywheels, turbine rotors |
| **Disk with central hole** | Annular plate, inner radius *b*, outer radius *a* | Turbine disks, brake rotors |
| **Variable-thickness disk** | Thickness varies with radius | High-speed turbo-machinery (optimised) |

**Material assumption:** Isotropic, homogeneous, plane-stress condition (disk is thin: *t ≪ a*).

---

## 1.2 Unsymmetrical Bending — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Unsymmetrical Bending):**
> **Unsymmetrical bending** is a condition in which the **plane of loading (plane containing the applied loads and the beam axis) does NOT coincide with either of the two principal planes (planes of symmetry)** of the cross-section. The beam possesses **two axes of symmetry** (e.g., rectangular, I-section, circular), but the load is applied obliquely.

The applied moment must therefore be **resolved into components about the two principal centroidal axes**, producing bending in **two perpendicular planes simultaneously**.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

### 🌀 Rotating Disk Analogy
Think of a **spinning pizza dough** in the air. The dough stretches outward due to its own weight/centrifugal effect, and you can feel the material pulling radially. In a steel flywheel or turbine disk, the same physics occurs — every particle tries to fly outward, and the disk develops internal stresses to resist this. **The hoop stress is always greater than the radial stress**, just like a spinning ring stretches more around its circumference than along its radius.

### 📐 Unsymmetrical Bending Analogy
Imagine holding a **ruler (rectangular cross-section) flat on your palm** (load along its thin axis → easy to bend) versus holding it **on edge** (load along the wide axis → hard to bend). Now imagine loading it at a **45° tilt**. The ruler tries to bend in two directions at once and also tries to **twist sideways**. This sideways deflection + tilt of the neutral axis is the hallmark of **unsymmetrical bending**.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Quantities (Bold Standards)

- **ω** = Angular velocity of rotation [**rad/s**]
- **ρ** = Mass density of disk material [**kg/m³**]
- **ν (Poisson's ratio)** ≈ **0.3** for steel
- **a** = Outer radius of disk [**m**]
- **b** = Inner radius of disk [**m**] (for annular disks)
- **Iₓ, I_y** = Principal moments of inertia about centroidal axes [**m⁴**]
- **M** = Applied bending moment [**N·m**]

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Rotating Disk

### Governing Equations (Polar Coordinates)
For an axisymmetric rotating disk under plane stress, the **equilibrium equation** in polar coordinates reduces to:

$$
\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} + \rho\omega^2 r = 0
$$

The **compatibility / strain-displacement** equations yield a second relation:

$$
\frac{d}{dr}(r\sigma_\theta) - \sigma_r = \nu r \rho \omega^2 \cdot \text{(derived)} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; r\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \sigma_r - \sigma_\theta + r^2\rho\omega^2 = 0
$$

**General solution** of this system (Timoshenko, *Theory of Plates & Shells*):

$$
\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2} - \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 r^2
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^2} - \frac{1+3\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 r^2
$$

Constants **A** and **B** are found from **boundary conditions** (free surface → σᵣ = 0).

---

## 2.2 Boundary Conditions & Logic Flow

| Case | Boundary Condition 1 | Boundary Condition 2 | Result |
|------|--------------------|----------------------|--------|
| **Solid disk** | σᵣ finite at r = 0 → **B = 0** | σᵣ = 0 at r = a → A = (3+ν)/8 · ρω² a² | Two formulas below |
| **Disk with hole** | σᵣ = 0 at r = a | σᵣ = 0 at r = b | A and B both non-zero |
| **Variable thickness** | Same BCs + thickness profile *t(r)* | Centrifugal force becomes ρω² r · t(r) | More complex integration |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are mathematically verified and commonly tested in KTU ESE.**

| # | Configuration | Formula | Stress Location |
|---|--------------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Solid disk — Radial stress | $\sigma_r = \dfrac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2\left(a^2 - r^2\right)$ | Anywhere |
| 2 | Solid disk — Hoop stress | $\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 a^2 - \dfrac{1+3\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 r^2$ | Anywhere |
| 3 | Solid disk — Stress at centre (r = 0) | $\sigma_r = \sigma_\theta = \dfrac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 a^2$ | Centre |
| 4 | Solid disk — Stress at rim (r = a) | $\sigma_r = 0, \; \sigma_\theta = \dfrac{1-\nu}{4}\rho\omega^2 a^2$ | Rim |
| 5 | Disk with hole — Radial | $\sigma_r = \dfrac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2\!\left[a^2 + b^2 - \dfrac{a^2 b^2}{r^2} - r^2\right]$ | Anywhere |
| 6 | Disk with hole — Hoop | $\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2\!\left[a^2 + b^2 + \dfrac{a^2 b^2}{r^2}\right] - \dfrac{1+3\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 r^2$ | Anywhere |
| 7 | Disk with hole — At inner edge (r = b) | $\sigma_r = 0,\; \sigma_\theta = \dfrac{3+\nu}{4}\rho\omega^2 a^2 + \dfrac{1-\nu}{4}\rho\omega^2 b^2$ | Inner edge |
| 8 | Disk with hole — At outer edge (r = a) | $\sigma_r = 0,\; \sigma_\theta = \dfrac{1-\nu}{4}\rho\omega^2 a^2 + \dfrac{3+\nu}{4}\rho\omega^2 b^2$ | Outer edge |
| 9 | Unsymmetric bending — Bending stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{M_x\, y}{I_x} + \dfrac{M_y\, x}{I_y}$ | Point (x,y) |
| 10 | Neutral axis equation | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{I_x}{I_y}\tan\theta$ | Cross-section |
| 11 | Maximum stress distance | $r_{\max} = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}\bigg\vert_{\text{farthest from NA}}$ | Critical fibre |
| 12 | Slope of elastic curve (deflection) | $\dfrac{dy}{dz} \neq 0$ in both x-z and y-z planes | Both planes |

> **Legend:** σᵣ = radial stress [Pa], σ_θ = hoop stress [Pa], ν = Poisson's ratio, ω = angular velocity [rad/s], ρ = density [kg/m³], Mₓ, Mᵧ = moment components about principal axes, θ = angle of load plane, α = angle of neutral axis.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Concept | Industrial Application |
|---------|------------------------|
| Rotating disks | Steam/gas turbine rotors, flywheels, grinding wheels, centrifugal pump impellers, vehicle brake discs |
| Unsymmetrical bending | I-beam purlins under oblique wind/snow load, crane girders with eccentric trolley rails, shaft keys, gantry girders |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Solid Uniform Rotating Disk

**Setup:** A solid disk of radius *a*, thickness *t*, density ρ, rotates at angular velocity ω about its central axis (z-axis).

### Step 1 — Equilibrium in Polar Coordinates
Consider an annular element of radius *r*, thickness *dr*, unit thickness. The forces on it:
- Centrifugal body force = ρω² r · dr · dθ
- Radial stresses σᵣ on inner face and (σᵣ + dσᵣ) on outer face
- Hoop stresses σ_θ on side faces

Radial equilibrium gives:

$$
\sigma_r \cdot r\,d\theta - (\sigma_r + d\sigma_r)(r+dr)d\theta + 2\sigma_\theta\,dr\,\sin(d\theta/2) + \rho\omega^2 r\,dr \cdot r\,d\theta = 0
$$

For small *dθ*: sin(dθ/2) ≈ dθ/2. Neglecting higher-order terms:

$$
\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} + \rho\omega^2 r = 0 \quad \text{...(Eq. A)}
$$

### Step 2 — Compatibility (Strain–Displacement Relations)
In polar coordinates for axisymmetric case:

$$
\varepsilon_r = \frac{du}{dr}, \quad \varepsilon_\theta = \frac{u}{r}
$$

Plane-stress Hooke's law:

$$
\sigma_r = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}(\varepsilon_r + \nu\varepsilon_\theta), \quad \sigma_\theta = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}(\varepsilon_\theta + \nu\varepsilon_r)
$$

Eliminating *u* via compatibility (dε_θ/dr = (ε_r − ε_θ)/r):

$$
r\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \sigma_r - \sigma_\theta = -\nu r^2\rho\omega^2 \quad \text{...(wait, full result below)}
$$

Actually, eliminating *u* carefully yields:

$$
\frac{d}{dr}(r\sigma_\theta) - \sigma_r = \nu r^2\rho\omega^2
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\sigma_\theta - \sigma_r + r\frac{d\sigma_\theta}{dr} = \nu r^2\rho\omega^2 \quad \text{...(Eq. B)}
$$

> Note: Some texts combine Eq. A and Eq. B differently. The key result is that *both* σᵣ and σ_θ are of the form **A − B/r² − C·r²**.

### Step 3 — Solve the System
Adding the two equilibrium-equivalent equations after suitable manipulation (Timoshenko's classical result):

$$
\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2} - \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 r^2
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^2} - \frac{1+3\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 r^2
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Boundary Conditions
- **BC1:** σᵣ must be finite at r = 0 → **B = 0** (otherwise σᵣ → ∞ at centre)
- **BC2:** Free surface at r = a → **σᵣ = 0 at r = a**

$$
0 = A - \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 a^2 \;\Rightarrow\; A = \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 a^2
$$

### Step 5 — Final Expressions

$$
\boxed{\sigma_r = \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2\left(a^2 - r^2\right)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_\theta = \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 a^2 - \frac{1+3\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 r^2}
$$

### Step 6 — Verify at Centre (r = 0)

$$
\sigma_r(0) = \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 a^2, \quad \sigma_\theta(0) = \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 a^2
$$

Both equal → **biaxial equal tension at centre** ✓

### Step 7 — Verify at Rim (r = a)

$$
\sigma_r(a) = 0 \;\checkmark, \quad \sigma_\theta(a) = \frac{(3+\nu) - (1+3\nu)}{8}\rho\omega^2 a^2 = \frac{2(1-\nu)}{8}\rho\omega^2 a^2 = \frac{1-\nu}{4}\rho\omega^2 a^2 \;\checkmark
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Disk with Central Hole

**Setup:** Annular disk with inner radius *b*, outer radius *a*. Same equilibrium (Eq. A) and compatibility (Eq. B).

### Step 1 — General Solution (same as before)

$$
\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2} - \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 r^2
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^2} - \frac{1+3\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 r^2
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Two Boundary Conditions
- **BC1:** σᵣ = 0 at r = a:

$$
0 = A - \frac{B}{a^2} - \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 a^2 \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

- **BC2:** σᵣ = 0 at r = b:

$$
0 = A - \frac{B}{b^2} - \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 b^2 \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for A and B
Subtract (ii) from (i):

$$
0 = -B\!\left(\frac{1}{a^2} - \frac{1}{b^2}\right) - \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2(a^2 - b^2)
$$

$$
B\cdot\frac{a^2 - b^2}{a^2 b^2} = \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2(a^2 - b^2)
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \boxed{B = \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 a^2 b^2}
$$

Substitute B back into (ii):

$$
A = \frac{B}{b^2} + \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 b^2 = \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 a^2 + \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 b^2
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \boxed{A = \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2\left(a^2 + b^2\right)}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Expressions

$$
\boxed{\sigma_r = \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2\left[a^2 + b^2 - \frac{a^2 b^2}{r^2} - r^2\right]}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_\theta = \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2\left[a^2 + b^2 + \frac{a^2 b^2}{r^2}\right] - \frac{1+3\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 r^2}
$$

### Step 5 — Verify Boundary Values

At r = b (inner rim):

$$
\sigma_r(b) = \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2\left[a^2 + b^2 - a^2 - b^2\right] = 0 \;\checkmark
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta(b) = \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2(a^2 + b^2 + a^2) - \frac{1+3\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 b^2
$$

$$
= \frac{3+\nu}{4}\rho\omega^2 a^2 + \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 b^2 - \frac{1+3\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 b^2
$$

$$
= \frac{3+\nu}{4}\rho\omega^2 a^2 + \frac{2 - 2\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 b^2 = \frac{3+\nu}{4}\rho\omega^2 a^2 + \frac{1-\nu}{4}\rho\omega^2 b^2 \;\checkmark
$$

At r = a (outer rim):

$$
\sigma_r(a) = \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2\left[a^2 + b^2 - b^2 - a^2\right] = 0 \;\checkmark
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta(a) = \frac{1-\nu}{4}\rho\omega^2 a^2 + \frac{3+\nu}{4}\rho\omega^2 b^2 \;\checkmark
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Unsymmetrical Bending

**Setup:** A straight beam with cross-section having two perpendicular axes of symmetry (principal axes *X* and *Y* through the centroid). The load *P* acts in a plane that makes an angle **θ** with one of the principal planes (say, the *X–Z* plane). The bending moment at a section is *M*.

### Step 1 — Resolve the Moment about Principal Axes
The applied moment *M* (acting about the z-axis perpendicular to the cross-section) can be thought of as producing two bending actions:

- Component that bends the beam in the **Y–Z plane** (about the X-axis): causes stress varying with **y**
- Component that bends the beam in the **X–Z plane** (about the Y-axis): causes stress varying with **x**

Resolution:

$$
M_x = M\sin\theta \quad \text{(moment about X-axis, bending in Y-Z plane)}
$$

$$
M_y = M\cos\theta \quad \text{(moment about Y-axis, bending in X-Z plane)}
$$

### Step 2 — Superpose Bending Stresses
For symmetric bending in each principal plane separately (simple bending theory, valid because each plane is a plane of symmetry):

- Stress due to M_x (varies linearly with y):

$$
\sigma_1 = \frac{M_x \cdot y}{I_x}
$$

- Stress due to M_y (varies linearly with x):

$$
\sigma_2 = \frac{M_y \cdot x}{I_y}
$$

### Step 3 — Combine by Superposition
Because the material is elastic and the geometry linear, the **principle of superposition** applies:

$$
\boxed{\sigma = \frac{M_x\, y}{I_x} + \frac{M_y\, x}{I_y} = \frac{M\sin\theta}{I_x}\,y + \frac{M\cos\theta}{I_y}\,x}
$$

### Step 4 — Locate the Neutral Axis
The neutral axis is the locus of points where **σ = 0**:

$$
\frac{M_x\, y}{I_x} + \frac{M_y\, x}{I_y} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{y}{x} = -\frac{M_y\, I_x}{M_x\, I_y}
$$

Taking magnitudes and substituting M_y/M_x = cot θ:

$$
\frac{y}{x} = \frac{I_x}{I_y}\tan\theta
$$

If **α** is the angle the neutral axis makes with the X-axis:

$$
\boxed{\tan\alpha = \frac{I_x}{I_y}\tan\theta}
$$

### Step 5 — Key Observation
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since **Iₓ ≠ I_y** in general, we have **α ≠ θ**.
> **The neutral axis is NOT perpendicular to the plane of loading.**
> The deflection of the beam is also NOT in the plane of loading — it has components in *both* x and y directions. This is the defining feature of unsymmetrical bending.

### Step 6 — Maximum Stress Location
The bending stress σ varies linearly across the section. The **maximum stress** occurs at the point on the cross-section **farthest from the neutral axis**. For symmetric cross-sections (I, rectangle), this is typically at a corner.

The maximum distance *r_max* from the centroid to the boundary, measured perpendicular to the neutral axis, determines σ_max:

$$
\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_x\, y_{\max}}{I_x} + \frac{M_y\, x_{\max}}{I_y}
$$

### Step 7 — Equivalent Result Using Principal Moments
Defining **M_principal** as the moment expressed about principal axes:

$$
M = \sqrt{M_x^2 + M_y^2}
$$

The neutral axis passes through the centroid and is inclined at angle α where **tan α = (Iₓ/I_y) tan θ**, while the plane of loading is inclined at angle θ.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Symmetric Bending | Unsymmetric Bending |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Plane of loading | Coincides with principal plane | Oblique to principal planes |
| Neutral axis | ⊥ to load plane | **NOT** ⊥ to load plane |
| Deflection | In load plane | Out of load plane (both x & y) |
| Stress formula | σ = My/I | σ = Mₓy/Iₓ + Mᵧx/Iᵧ |
| Number of moment components | 1 | 2 |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> *Physical stress-distribution plots are best drawn freehand or in CAD; here, professional Mermaid block-level schematics map the analytical interactions for both topics.*

## 4.1 Rotating Disk — Analysis & Stress Distribution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rotating Disk Input: a, b, t, rho, omega, nu] --> B{Disk Type?}
    B -->|Solid| C[Apply BC: sigma_r finite at r=0]
    B -->|With Hole| D[Apply BC: sigma_r=0 at r=a AND r=b]
    B -->|Variable Thickness| E[Integrate with t r profile]
    C --> F[Compute A constant]
    D --> G[Compute A and B constants]
    E --> H[Numerical or graphical solution]
    F --> I[sigma_r formula]
    F --> J[sigma_theta formula]
    G --> I
    G --> J
    I --> K[Evaluate at critical radii]
    J --> K
    K --> L{Critical Location}
    L -->|Centre r=0| M[Equal biaxial tension]
    L -->|Inner rim r=b| N[Max hoop stress for hole]
    L -->|Outer rim r=a| O[Max hoop for solid disk]
    M --> P[Compare with allowable stress]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q{Safe?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Design OK]
    Q -->|No| S[Reduce omega or increase a or change material]
```

    end

    end

## 4.3 Unsymmetrical Bending — Resolution & Neutral Axis Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Beam with Two Axes of Symmetry X, Y] --> B[Load P at Angle theta from Principal Plane]
    B --> C[Resolve into Components]
    C --> D[Px = P sin theta: Bends about Y-axis]
    C --> E[Py = P cos theta: Bends about X-axis]
    D --> F[Bending Stress sigma_y from My about y-axis]
    E --> G[Bending Stress sigma_x from Mx about x-axis]
    F --> H[Superpose sigma_x and sigma_y]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Total sigma at point x,y]
    I --> J{sigma equals zero?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Neutral Axis: tan alpha = Ix/Iy tan theta]
    J -->|No| L[Identify point with max sigma]
    K --> M[Note: alpha NEQ theta. NA NOT perp to load plane]
    L --> N[Check sigma_max vs allowable]
    N --> O{Safe?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Design Acceptable]
    O -->|No| Q[Reorient or resize section]
```
