# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the expressions for radial and hoop stresses in a uniform rotating solid disk and write down their values at the centre and at the rim.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a uniform solid rotating disk of outer radius *a*, density ρ, angular velocity ω, and Poisson's ratio ν:

$$
\sigma_r = \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2(a^2 - r^2)
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta = \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 a^2 - \frac{1+3\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 r^2
$$

- At centre (r = 0): **σᵣ = σ_θ = (3+ν)/8 · ρω²a²** (equal biaxial tension)
- At rim (r = a): **σᵣ = 0** and **σ_θ = (1−ν)/4 · ρω²a²**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Define unsymmetrical bending. Why is the neutral axis not perpendicular to the plane of loading in unsymmetrical bending?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Unsymmetrical bending occurs when the plane of loading of a beam does not coincide with any principal plane of its cross-section. The bending moment is resolved into components about the two principal centroidal axes, causing simultaneous bending in two perpendicular planes.

**Reason:** Since the cross-section has different moments of inertia (Iₓ ≠ Iᵧ) about its two principal axes, the neutral axis equation **tan α = (Iₓ/Iᵧ) tan θ** gives α ≠ θ. The neutral axis is therefore inclined at a different angle than the load plane, and not perpendicular to it.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Rotating Disk Focus

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

A solid steel disk of diameter **400 mm** and thickness **20 mm** rotates at **3000 rpm**. Density of steel = **7850 kg/m³** and Poisson's ratio = **0.3**. Determine:

**(a)** The radial and hoop stresses at a radius of **60 mm** from the centre. (7 marks)

**(b)** The maximum hoop stress and its location. Also find the stresses at the centre. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution A(a):

**Given:** Diameter = 400 mm → a = 0.2 m, N = 3000 rpm, t = 20 mm, ρ = 7850 kg/m³, ν = 0.3, r = 60 mm = 0.06 m

**Step 1 — Angular velocity:**

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} = 100\pi \approx 314.159\ \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute constant (3+ν)/8:**

$$
\frac{3+\nu}{8} = \frac{3+0.3}{8} = \frac{3.3}{8} = 0.4125
$$

**Step 3 — Compute (3+ν)ρω²/8:**

$$
\frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 = 0.4125 \times 7850 \times (314.159)^2
$$

$$
= 0.4125 \times 7850 \times 98696 = 3.196 \times 10^8\ \text{Pa} \approx 319.6\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Radial stress at r = 0.06 m:**

$$
\sigma_r = \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2(a^2 - r^2) = 319.6 \times 10^6 \times (0.04 - 0.0036)
$$

$$
= 319.6 \times 10^6 \times 0.0364 = 11.63 \times 10^6\ \text{Pa} \approx 11.63\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Hoop stress at r = 0.06 m:**

First compute (1+3ν)/8:

$$
\frac{1+3\nu}{8} = \frac{1+0.9}{8} = \frac{1.9}{8} = 0.2375
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta = \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 a^2 - \frac{1+3\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 r^2
$$

$$
= 319.6 \times 10^6 \times 0.04 - 0.2375 \times 7850 \times 98696 \times 0.0036
$$

$$
= 12.784 \times 10^6 - 6.624 \times 10^6 = 6.16 \times 10^6\ \text{Pa} \approx 6.16\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Result:** **σᵣ ≈ 11.63 MPa, σ_θ ≈ 6.16 MPa** at r = 60 mm.

---

#### Solution A(b):

**Maximum hoop stress location:** For a solid disk, σ_θ is maximum at **r = 0 (centre)** because σ_θ decreases with r².

**Step 1 — Hoop stress at centre (r = 0):**

$$
\sigma_\theta(0) = \sigma_r(0) = \frac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 a^2 = 319.6 \times 10^6 \times 0.04 = 12.784\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Hoop stress at rim (r = a):**

$$
\sigma_\theta(a) = \frac{1-\nu}{4}\rho\omega^2 a^2
$$

$$
\frac{1-\nu}{4} = \frac{1-0.3}{4} = 0.175
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta(a) = 0.175 \times 7850 \times 98696 \times 0.04 = 5.42\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison:**

| Location | r (m) | σᵣ (MPa) | σ_θ (MPa) |
|----------|------|---------|----------|
| Centre | 0.00 | **12.78** | **12.78** |
| Mid | 0.06 | 11.63 | 6.16 |
| Rim | 0.20 | 0 | 5.42 |

**Result:** **Maximum hoop stress = 12.78 MPa at the centre (r = 0)**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Unsymmetrical Bending Focus

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, Internal Choice]**

A simply supported rectangular beam of cross-section **120 mm × 80 mm (depth × width)** and span **3 m** carries a concentrated load of **5 kN** at the mid-span. The load acts in a vertical plane that makes an angle of **30°** with the plane of the web (i.e., with the Y-Z principal plane).

**(a)** Resolve the load into components along the principal axes and determine the maximum bending stress in the beam. (7 marks)

**(b)** Determine the angle of the neutral axis with respect to the X-axis. Also find the location of the neutral axis from the centroid. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution B(a):

**Given:** 
- Cross-section: 120 mm (Y-direction, depth) × 80 mm (X-direction, width)
- Span L = 3 m, Load P = 5 kN at mid-span, angle θ = 30° with Y-Z plane

**Step 1 — Resolve Load:**

$$
P_x = P\sin\theta = 5 \times 0.5 = 2.5\ \text{kN}
$$

$$
P_y = P\cos\theta = 5 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 4.330\ \text{kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Bending Moments (for simply supported with central point load: M_max = PL/4):**

$$
M_x = M_y^{\text{(component)}} = \frac{P_y L}{4} = \frac{4.330 \times 3}{4} = 3.2475\ \text{kN·m}
$$

$$
M_y = M_x^{\text{(component)}} = \frac{P_x L}{4} = \frac{2.5 \times 3}{4} = 1.875\ \text{kN·m}
$$

> Note: M_x bends about X-axis (stress varies with y); M_y bends about Y-axis (stress varies with x).

**Step 3 — Principal Moments of Inertia:**

For rectangle about centroidal axes (b = 80 mm, d = 120 mm):

$$
I_x = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{80 \times 120^3}{12} = 11.52 \times 10^6\ \text{mm}^4 = 11.52 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^4
$$

$$
I_y = \frac{d b^3}{12} = \frac{120 \times 80^3}{12} = 5.12 \times 10^6\ \text{mm}^4 = 5.12 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^4
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Bending Stress:**
Maximum stress occurs at the corner farthest from the neutral axis. For a rectangle, the critical corner is where both *x* and *y* are maximum and of same sign contribution.

At corner (x = ±40 mm, y = ±60 mm):

$$
\sigma = \frac{M_x y}{I_x} + \frac{M_y x}{I_y}
$$

Choosing corner that gives maximum tension (positive):

At (x = +40 mm, y = +60 mm):

$$
\sigma = \frac{3.2475 \times 10^3 \times 0.060}{11.52 \times 10^{-6}} + \frac{1.875 \times 10^3 \times 0.040}{5.12 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
= \frac{194.85}{11.52 \times 10^{-6}} + \frac{75}{5.12 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
= 16.91 \times 10^6 + 14.65 \times 10^6 = 31.56 \times 10^6\ \text{Pa} = 31.56\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Result:** **σ_max ≈ 31.56 MPa (tensile)** at the corner (40, 60) mm.

---

#### Solution B(b):

**Step 1 — Angle of Neutral Axis with X-axis:**

$$
\tan\alpha = \frac{I_x}{I_y}\tan\theta
$$

$$
\tan\alpha = \frac{11.52 \times 10^{-6}}{5.12 \times 10^{-6}} \times \tan 30° = 2.25 \times 0.5774 = 1.299
$$

$$
\alpha = \tan^{-1}(1.299) \approx 52.4°
$$

**Step 2 — Equation of Neutral Axis:**
The neutral axis passes through centroid and has slope tan α = 1.299:

$$
y = 1.299\, x
$$

**Step 3 — Verify Against Load Plane:**
Load plane makes 30° with Y-axis (or 60° with X-axis). Neutral axis is at 52.4° with X-axis. They are **not perpendicular** (52.4° + 60° = 112.4° ≠ 90°), confirming unsymmetrical bending.

**Step 4 — Distance from Centroid:**
For rectangle 120×80, the NA passes through (0, 0). It intersects the rectangle boundary at:

Solving y = 1.299 x with x = 40 mm:

$$
y = 1.299 \times 40 = 51.96\ \text{mm} \quad (\text{less than } 60\ \text{mm})
$$

So NA exits through the top edge (y = +60 mm) at x = 60/1.299 = 46.2 mm? But x = 40 mm is the limit. Let's check: at x = 40, y = 51.96 mm < 60 mm ✓. So NA hits the right edge first.

**Intersection points of NA with rectangle boundary:**
- Right edge (x = 40): y = 51.96 mm ✓
- Top edge (y = 60): x = 60/1.299 = 46.2 mm (out of range, x would need to exceed 40 mm)
- Therefore NA exits through **right edge at (40, 51.96) mm**

**Distance from centroid to NA at point (40, 51.96):**

$$
d = \frac{|40 \times \tan\alpha|}{\sqrt{1+\tan^2\alpha}} = \frac{40 \times 1.299}{\sqrt{1+1.687}} = \frac{51.96}{\sqrt{2.687}} = \frac{51.96}{1.639} = 31.7\ \text{mm}
$$

**Result:** **α ≈ 52.4°**, NA passes through centroid, intersects right edge at y = **51.96 mm**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
>
> 1. **Wrong angular velocity:** Students write ω = 2πN (forgetting /60). Always convert RPM to rad/s.
> 2. **Confusing hoop and radial stress:** Remember σᵣ ≤ σ_θ always. The hoop stress is the governing design stress.
> 3. **Sign errors in unsymmetrical bending:** Sign of Mₓ·y/Iₓ and Mᵧ·x/Iᵧ must be tracked; maximum stress is the *algebraic* sum of both.
> 4. **Forgetting ν in rotating disk formulas:** Most students drop Poisson's ratio. KTU examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Wrong BC for solid disk:** A common error is to apply σ_r = 0 at r = 0 instead of finiteness. Centre has equal biaxial stress, NOT zero.
> 6. **Units mismatch in final answer:** Use SI (Pa, m, kg, rad/s) throughout. Mixing mm and m costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 7. **In unsymmetrical bending, students often compute α = θ:** This is only true when Iₓ = Iᵧ (i.e., square/circular cross-section). Always use tan α = (Iₓ/Iᵧ) tan θ.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rotating Disks — Rapid Revision Checklist
- ✅ Two governing equations: **equilibrium + compatibility**
- ✅ General solution form: σᵣ = A − B/r² − (3+ν)/8·ρω²r²; σ_θ = A + B/r² − (1+3ν)/8·ρω²r²
- ✅ Solid disk: **B = 0**, A = (3+ν)/8·ρω²a²
- ✅ Disk with hole: **A and B both non-zero**, A = (3+ν)/8·ρω²(a²+b²), B = (3+ν)/8·ρω²a²b²
- ✅ Maximum hoop stress in solid disk: at **centre** = (3+ν)/8·ρω²a²
- ✅ Maximum hoop stress in disk with hole: at **inner edge** = (3+ν)/4·ρω²a² + (1−ν)/4·ρω²b²
- ✅ Radial stress is **zero at all free surfaces** (rim and inner edge for annular disk)
- ✅ Always convert **N (RPM) to ω (rad/s)** using ω = 2πN/60

### Unsymmetrical Bending — Rapid Revision Checklist
- ✅ Plane of loading is **oblique** to both principal planes
- ✅ Resolve moment M into **Mₓ and Mᵧ about principal axes**
- ✅ Combined stress: **σ = Mₓy/Iₓ + Mᵧx/Iᵧ**
- ✅ Neutral axis: **tan α = (Iₓ/Iᵧ) tan θ** where θ is angle of load plane
- ✅ Neutral axis is **NOT perpendicular to load plane** (key distinguishing feature)
- ✅ Deflection has components in **both x and y directions**
- ✅ Maximum stress at the point **farthest from neutral axis** (usually a corner)
- ✅ Beam will bend **sideways** even though load is vertical — this is the giveaway sign of unsymmetrical bending

### Quick Diagnostic Table
| If you see... | The problem is about... |
|----------------|------------------------|
| Disk with RPM/density/Poisson's ratio | **Rotating disk stresses** |
| Disk with inner and outer radius | **Disk with central hole** |
| Load at angle on I-beam/rectangular section | **Unsymmetrical bending** |
| "Find neutral axis location" | **Unsymmetrical bending (NA equation)** |
| "Maximum hoop stress" | **Rotating disk (check r=0 or r=b)** |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shear centre
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET415 - ADVANCED MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shear centre

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shear Centre (SC):** It is the point on the cross-section of a beam through which the transverse load must be applied in order that the beam bends *without any twisting (torsion)* of the cross-section. Equivalently, it is the point about which the **resultant of the transverse shear forces** in the section acts, producing **zero twisting moment** about the longitudinal axis of the beam.

The shear centre is, in general, a **property of the cross-section geometry only**. For sections possessing **two axes of symmetry** (e.g., I-section, rectangular, circular), the shear centre **coincides with the centroid (G)**. For sections with **only one axis of symmetry** (e.g., channel, T, Z, unequal-flange I), the shear centre lies **on that axis of symmetry** but is **displaced from the centroid**. For completely unsymmetric sections, the shear centre lies at an arbitrary point not on any symmetry axis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Balanced Finger" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Think of the cross-section as a flat piece of cardboard held edge-on by your fingers. If you push your fingers through the **centroid**, the cardboard tends to **twist** (rotate) because the internal shear forces on the two sides of the push do not balance. But if you push your fingers through a *different* point — the **shear centre** — the cardboard translates sideways **without twisting**, like a balanced rudder on an aircraft tail. On a real aeroplane, the **rudder hinge line passes through the shear centre** of the rear fuselage/tail section so that aerodynamic side-loads bend the structure but never twist it.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used

- The shear centre location is expressed as a **distance (in mm or m)** from a reference point (usually the centroid, the web mid-thickness, or an outer edge).
- The shear-flow magnitude $q$ has units of **N/m** (force per unit length along the centre line of the wall).
- The shear stress in a thin wall is $\tau = \dfrac{q}{t}$, with units of **N/m² = Pa**.
- The twisting moment of the applied load about the shear centre must equal **zero** for pure bending:

$$M_T^{SC} \;=\; 0$$

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exams:** *"Centroid is for axial loads; Shear Centre is for transverse loads."* Centroid = no bending, no twist from axial pull/push. Shear Centre = bending yes, twist no.

## 1.4 Why the Shear Centre Exists — The Underlying Physics

When a transverse load $V$ is applied at some point $P$ on a cross-section, the internal shear flow $q(s)$ along the wall(s) of the section creates a system of **distributed shear forces**. Each elemental shear force $dF = q(s)\,ds$ acts parallel to the wall's tangent at that point. The **resultant** of these distributed forces must equal the applied $V$, and its **line of action** defines the shear centre.

If the load is applied at a point different from the SC, the line of action of the resultant shear force will be offset from the load line by a distance $e$, producing a **torsional moment** $T = V \cdot e$ that twists the section.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanics of Shear Flow in Thin-Walled Open Sections

For a thin-walled open section, the **shear flow** at any section cut located at a distance $s$ from the free edge is given by the classical Bredt-type formula:

$$q(s) \;=\; \dfrac{V \cdot Q_x(s)}{I_x}$$

where:
- $V$ = transverse shear force on the section (acting through the SC)
- $Q_x(s)$ = **first moment of area** of the portion of the section lying *beyond* the cut (i.e., on the free-edge side) about the **neutral axis (x-axis)**
- $I_x$ = second moment of area of the *entire* section about the neutral axis

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention for $q$:** Positive shear flow on a wall is conventionally drawn in the direction that causes a **clockwise** rotation of the cut-out piece, consistent with a downward shear force $V$ on a positive face.

The **shear stress** at a thin wall of thickness $t$ is:

$$\tau(s) \;=\; \dfrac{q(s)}{t(s)} \;=\; \dfrac{V \cdot Q_x(s)}{I_x \cdot t(s)}$$

## 2.2 Locating the Shear Centre — The Equivalence Principle

The general procedure has **three core steps**:

1. **Choose a reference load application point** (e.g., the centroid, the web centre, or a corner). Let the applied load be $V$.
2. **Compute the shear-flow distribution** $q(s)$ in each wall branch using $q = V Q_x / I_x$.
3. **Take moments** of all elemental shear forces about a convenient point, equate the result to the moment $V \cdot e$ of the applied load about the shear centre, and solve for $e$.

The governing scalar equilibrium for shear centre location on a section symmetric about the $x$-axis is:

$$\sum (q_i \cdot b_i) \cdot d_i \;=\; V \cdot e$$

where $b_i$ is the projected length of wall $i$ in the load direction, and $d_i$ is the moment arm from the chosen reference point to the line of action of the shear force in that wall.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table** (Memorize these for KTU 2024 ESE.)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Assumptions |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Shear flow (open section) | $q(s) = \dfrac{V \cdot Q_x(s)}{I_x}$ | N/m | Thin-wall assumption |
| 2 | Shear stress in thin wall | $\tau(s) = \dfrac{q(s)}{t(s)}$ | Pa | $t(s)$ is local thickness |
| 3 | First moment of cut area | $Q_x(s) = \int_{s}^{s_{end}} y \, t(\xi)\, d\xi$ | m³ | Integrate from cut to free edge |
| 4 | Shear centre offset (channel) | $e = \dfrac{b^2 h^2 t}{4 I_x}$ | m | $b$ = flange width, $h$ = web depth, $t$ = uniform thickness |
| 5 | Channel section $I_x$ | $I_x = \dfrac{t h^3}{12} + \dfrac{b t (h+t)^2}{2}$ | m⁴ | Two flanges, one web |
| 6 | Resultant shear force in a wall | $F_i = \int q_i(s)\, ds = \dfrac{V Q_{x,\max}}{I_x} \cdot \text{(centroidal moment)}$ | N | Use mean $Q_x$ for linearly varying $q$ |
| 7 | Twist equilibrium | $\sum F_i \cdot d_i = V \cdot e$ | N·m | Solve for $e$ |
| 8 | Symmetric I-section (equal flanges) | $e = 0$ (SC $\equiv$ Centroid) | — | $G = SC$ on the symmetry axes |
| 9 | Equal-thickness channel result | $F_1 = \dfrac{V b h t}{4 I_x}\bigl(2 h t + b t\bigr) \approx \dfrac{V b h t}{4 I_x} \cdot b$ (simplified) | N | Force in one flange |
| 10 | Shear centre in thin-walled closed section (Bredt) | $q = \dfrac{T}{2 A_m}$ where $T = V \cdot e$ | N/m | Used for box sections |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, all vertical bars that would normally denote absolute value or "magnitude of" have been rendered as **non-pipe** equivalents to keep the markdown safe. If a printed answer requires the notation $\vert x \vert$, write it as `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX, *not* as `$|x|$`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** The wing and tail of an aircraft are thin-walled open sections. The aerodynamic lift and side-loads must act through (or very near) the shear centre, otherwise the wing would twist and flutter dangerously.
- **Civil:** Long-span cold-formed steel purlins, girts, and lipped channels are designed so that loads transferred from cladding pass through the SC, eliminating torsional restraint demands on the connections.
- **Mechanical:** Machine tool frames, crane girders, and automotive chassis members are checked for SC eccentricity to prevent serviceability failures due to twisting.
- **Ship-building:** Longitudinal stiffeners of hulls are designed with awareness of SC offset to minimize twisting of plating under wave loads.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following two derivations are the **most frequently asked** KTU 14-mark questions on this topic. Work through every algebraic transition — the KTU evaluator awards marks for intermediate steps, not just the final answer.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — Shear Centre of a Thin-Walled Channel Section

**Section geometry (assumptions):**
- Uniform wall thickness $t$ (same for web and both flanges)
- Flange width $b$ (each flange)
- Web height $h$ (clear distance between flanges, measured centreline-to-centreline)
- Section is symmetric about the horizontal centroidal $x$-axis
- Apply a vertical downward shear force $V$ through the shear centre (to be located)

> [!NOTE]
> Since the section is symmetric about the $x$-axis, the shear centre **must lie on this axis**. We only need to determine its **horizontal distance $e$ from the web's mid-thickness line**.

### Step 1 — Compute $I_x$ about the centroidal $x$-axis

Treat each flange as a thin rectangle of area $b t$ located at distance $(h/2 + t/2)$ from the $x$-axis. Treat the web as a thin rectangle $h t$ centred on the $x$-axis.

$$\begin{aligned}
I_x &= I_{x,\text{web}} \;+\; 2\, I_{x,\text{flange}} \\[4pt]
    &= \dfrac{t h^3}{12} \;+\; 2 \left[ \dfrac{b t^3}{12} + b t \left(\dfrac{h+t}{2}\right)^{\!2} \right]
\end{aligned}$$

Since $t \ll h$ and $t \ll b$, the term $\dfrac{b t^3}{12}$ (own moment of inertia of flange) is negligible. Drop it:

$$I_x \;\approx\; \dfrac{t h^3}{12} \;+\; \dfrac{b t (h+t)^2}{2}$$

This is the standard KTU-accepted form.

### Step 2 — Shear flow in the flanges

Cut the section at a distance $s$ from the **free (outer) tip** of the upper flange. The cut-off area is a small rectangle of width $s$ and thickness $t$, located at a distance $(h+t)/2$ from the $x$-axis.

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_x(s) &= (s \cdot t) \cdot \left(\dfrac{h+t}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

Shear flow in the flange (note: shear flow must be *constant* across the cut since the flange is straight and $Q_x$ varies linearly — actually it varies linearly with $s$, so $q$ is linear):

$$q_f(s) \;=\; \dfrac{V}{I_x} \cdot s t \cdot \dfrac{(h+t)}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{V t (h+t)}{2 I_x}\, s$$

The shear flow is **zero at the free tip** ($s=0$) and **maximum at the flange–web junction** ($s = b$):

$$q_{f,\max} \;=\; \dfrac{V\, t\, (h+t)\, b}{2\, I_x}$$

The **resultant force** in one flange is obtained by integrating $q_f(s)$ over the flange length $b$:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_f &= \int_0^b q_f(s)\, ds \;=\; \int_0^b \dfrac{V t (h+t)}{2 I_x}\, s \, ds \\[4pt]
    &= \dfrac{V t (h+t)}{2 I_x} \cdot \left[\dfrac{s^2}{2}\right]_0^b \\[4pt]
    &= \dfrac{V t (h+t)\, b^2}{4 I_x}
\end{aligned}$$

This resultant acts **horizontally** (along the flange), at the mid-length of the flange because $q$ varies linearly — the centroid of a triangle lies at $2b/3$ from the tip, so the line of action is at $b/2$ from the web. (For a linear $q$ the line of action of the resultant is at the mid-point of the wall, i.e., $b/2$ from the web centreline.)

### Step 3 — Shear flow in the web

Cut the web at a distance $y$ from the **top** of the web. The cut-off area consists of the upper flange (full) plus a portion of the web of height $y$.

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_x(y) &= b t \cdot \dfrac{(h+t)}{2} \;+\; (y \cdot t) \cdot \left(\dfrac{h}{2} - \dfrac{y}{2}\right) \\[4pt]
       &= \dfrac{b t (h+t)}{2} \;+\; \dfrac{t y (h-y)}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

Shear flow in the web:

$$q_w(y) \;=\; \dfrac{V}{I_x}\left[ \dfrac{b t (h+t)}{2} + \dfrac{t y (h-y)}{2} \right]$$

At the top of the web ($y=0$):

$$q_w(0) \;=\; \dfrac{V b t (h+t)}{2 I_x} \;=\; q_{f,\max}$$

✓ **Continuity is satisfied** — the shear flow at the flange–web junction is the same on both sides, as required by equilibrium.

At the centroid ($y = h/2$):

$$q_w(h/2) \;=\; \dfrac{V}{I_x}\left[\dfrac{b t (h+t)}{2} + \dfrac{t \cdot (h/2) \cdot (h/2)}{2}\right] \;=\; \dfrac{V}{I_x}\left[\dfrac{b t (h+t)}{2} + \dfrac{t h^2}{8}\right]$$

The **web resultant** equals the entire applied $V$ (by vertical equilibrium), and acts through the SC itself.

### Step 4 — Moment equilibrium to find $e$

Take moments about the **mid-thickness line of the web** (point O, located at the centre of the web). Each flange force $F_f$ has a moment arm equal to $(h+t)/2$ vertically. The web's resultant $V$ passes through the SC, so its moment arm about O is $e$ (still unknown).

By the rule: *sum of moments due to internal shear flows = moment of applied load about SC*:

$$V \cdot e \;=\; 2 \cdot F_f \cdot \dfrac{(h+t)}{2}$$

(There are **two** flanges, each contributing $F_f$, both with vertical lever arm $(h+t)/2$ to point O.)

$$\begin{aligned}
V \cdot e &= 2 \cdot \left[\dfrac{V t (h+t)\, b^2}{4 I_x}\right] \cdot \dfrac{(h+t)}{2} \\[4pt]
V \cdot e &= \dfrac{V t (h+t)^2 b^2}{4 I_x}
\end{aligned}$$

Cancel $V$ from both sides:

$$\boxed{\,e \;=\; \dfrac{b^2 h^2 t}{4\, I_x}\,}$$

(using the engineering approximation $h+t \approx h$ in the numerator when $t \ll h$, and the full $I_x$ in the denominator).

Substituting $I_x \approx \dfrac{t h^3}{12} + \dfrac{b t h^2}{2}$ (again, dropping $t$ inside $(h+t)^2$ relative to $h$):

$$e \;\approx\; \dfrac{b^2 h^2 t}{4\left(\dfrac{t h^3}{12} + \dfrac{b t h^2}{2}\right)} \;=\; \dfrac{b^2 h^2}{4 t \left(\dfrac{h^2}{12} + \dfrac{b h}{2}\right)} \;=\; \dfrac{3 b^2}{h + 6 b}$$

This is the **standard simplified KTU result** for a channel of uniform thickness. It shows that the shear centre lies **outside the web** on the side of the larger $b$ (which is the open side of the channel).

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Shear Centre of an Equal-Flange I-Section

For an I-section with **equal flanges** and a **web**, by symmetry the shear centre coincides with the **centroid** at the intersection of the two axes of symmetry. The shear-flow distribution in the web and flanges, however, is still an important exam question.

**Shear flow in the upper flange (measured from free tip, $s$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_x(s) &= s t \cdot \dfrac{h}{2} \\[4pt]
q_f(s) &= \dfrac{V}{I_x} \cdot \dfrac{s t h}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Maximum shear flow in flange** (at the flange–web junction, $s=b$):

$$q_{f,\max} \;=\; \dfrac{V b t h}{2 I_x}$$

**Resultant in upper flange:**

$$F_f \;=\; \int_0^b q_f(s)\, ds \;=\; \dfrac{V b^2 t h}{4 I_x}$$

**Shear flow in the web** (at distance $y$ from the top of the web):

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_x(y) &= b t \cdot \dfrac{h}{2} + y t \cdot \left(\dfrac{h}{2} - \dfrac{y}{2}\right) \\[4pt]
q_w(y) &= \dfrac{V}{I_x} \left[ \dfrac{b t h}{2} + \dfrac{t y (h-y)}{2} \right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Maximum shear stress in the web** (at the neutral axis, $y = h/2$):

$$q_{w,\max} \;=\; \dfrac{V}{I_x}\left[\dfrac{b t h}{2} + \dfrac{t h^2}{8}\right]$$

For the equal-flange I-section, $e = 0$ by symmetry; the SC and centroid coincide.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** The 14-mark question often asks the student to *prove* that the shear centre coincides with the centroid for symmetric sections, by showing that the moment sum is identically zero. The steps above form the backbone of that proof.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Shear Centre of Common Sections

| Section Type | Symmetry | SC Location | Sign of offset (from centroid) |
|--------------|----------|-------------|---------------------------------|
| Rectangular (solid) | Two axes | $\equiv$ centroid | 0 |
| Solid circular | Infinite | $\equiv$ centroid | 0 |
| I-section (equal flanges) | Two axes | $\equiv$ centroid | 0 |
| I-section (unequal flanges) | One axis | On symmetry axis, between flanges | 0 (vertically) — but offset from web depends on flange areas |
| Channel | One axis | On symmetry axis, **outside** the web | Yes, on the open side |
| T-section | One axis | On symmetry axis, **outside** the section | Yes, on the flange-free side |
| Z-section (equal legs) | Centroidal symmetry (180° rot.) | At intersection of web and centroid | At centroid, but SC ≠ G for thin walls |
| Thin-walled closed box | Two axes | $\equiv$ centroid | 0 (for symmetric box) |
| Unequal angle | One axis | Outside the section | Yes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU mistake:** Students often confuse the **elastic centroid** (the point through which the load must pass for *uniform* stress) with the **shear centre**. They are equal only for doubly-symmetric sections. For unsymmetric sections, the centroid and SC are at different points.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below are rendered using Mermaid block-level functional flows to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 schematic constraints. Each diagram maps the *physical force flow* through the cross-section.

## 4.1 Block Flow — Concept of Shear Centre in a Channel Section

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Applied Vertical Load V at arbitrary point P] --> B{Does P coincide with SC?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Pure Bending - No Twist]
    B -- No --> D[Eccentric Offset e exists]
    D --> E[Internal Shear Flow q in flanges Ff]
    D --> F[Internal Shear Flow q in web Fw]
    E --> G[Twisting Moment T equals V times e]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Cross section twists about longitudinal axis]
    C --> I[Bending stress only sigma equals My over I]
    H --> J[Combined bending plus warping torsion]
```

## 4.2 Block Flow — Algorithm to Locate Shear Centre

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Start with section geometry] --> S2[Identify axes of symmetry]
    S2 --> S3{Two axes of symmetry?}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[SC coincides with centroid G - DONE]
    S3 -- No --> S5[One axis of symmetry]
    S5 --> S6[SC lies on the symmetry axis]
    S6 --> S7[Choose reference point on symmetry axis]
    S7 --> S8[Apply unit virtual load V at SC location]
    S8 --> S9[Compute shear flow q equals V Qx over Ix in each wall]
    S9 --> S10[Resolve shear flow into elemental forces dF equals q ds]
    S10 --> S11[Take moments of dF about the reference point]
    S11 --> S12[Equate to V times e and solve for e]
    S12 --> S13[Report SC distance e from reference]
```

## 4.3 Block Flow — Force Distribution Inside a Channel Under Pure Shear

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[Flange 1: Shear force Ff1 horizontal] --> M1[Acts at mid-flange height h plus t over 2]
    L2[Web: Shear force Fw vertical equals V] --> M2[Acts through SC at distance e from web]
    L3[Flange 2: Shear force Ff2 horizontal] --> M1
    M1 --> N1[Sum of moments about web mid line equals V times e]
    M2 --> N1
    N1 --> O[Solve e equals b squared h squared t over 4 Ix]
```

## 4.4 Block Flow — Engineering Use-Case Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    U1[Engineering Application] --> U2{What is the load type?}
    U2 -- Transverse load V --> U3{Cross section type?}
    U2 -- Axial load N --> U4[Centroid governs - no SC issue]
    U3 -- Doubly symmetric --> U5[SC equals G - no special design]
    U3 -- Singly symmetric --> U6[Compute SC offset e]
    U3 -- Unsymmetric --> U7[Compute SC location - may not be on any axis]
    U6 --> U8[Design load path through SC]
    U7 --> U8
    U8 --> U9[Aerospace wing torsion - route load through SC]
    U8 --> U10[Cold formed steel purlin - align bearing through SC]
    U8 --> U11[Bridge girder diaphragm - provide lateral restraint at SC]
```

## 4.5 Schematic Notation Reference

| Symbol | Meaning | SI Unit |
|--------|---------|---------|
| $V$ | Transverse shear force | N |
| $q(s)$ | Shear flow along wall centre line | N/m |
| $\tau(s)$ | Shear stress in wall | Pa |
| $I_x$ | Second moment of area about $x$ axis | m⁴ |
| $Q_x(s)$ | First moment of cut area about $x$ axis | m³ |
| $e$ | Eccentricity of load from SC | m |
| $T$ | Twisting moment | N·m |
| $b$ | Flange width of channel | m |
| $h$ | Web depth of channel | m |
| $t$ | Wall thickness (uniform) | m |
| $G$ | Centroid of section | — |
| $SC$ | Shear centre | — |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are written in the **exact KTU 2024 End-Semester Examination (ESE) style**. Marks are split as per the official KTU pattern. Use the model solutions as your self-study reference.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
*Define the term "Shear Centre". Explain why the shear centre does not coincide with the centroid for a channel section.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The shear centre is defined as the point on the cross-section of a beam through which the transverse load must be applied in order that the beam bends *without twisting*. It is a property of the cross-section geometry alone.

**Reason for non-coincidence (1 mark):** A channel section possesses only **one axis of symmetry** (the horizontal centroidal axis). The shear flow in the two flanges produces a couple that, when summed with the web's shear force, gives a resultant that does **not** pass through the centroid. By the equivalence of moments, this resultant must pass through the SC, which therefore lies on the symmetry axis but is **displaced horizontally** from the centroid (typically on the open side of the web). For doubly-symmetric sections (e.g., I-section with equal flanges), the two flange couples cancel by symmetry and the SC coincides with the centroid.

---

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
*State the formula for shear flow in a thin-walled open section. Mention its limitations.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Formula (2 marks):**
$$q(s) \;=\; \dfrac{V \cdot Q_x(s)}{I_x}$$
where $V$ is the transverse shear force, $Q_x(s)$ is the first moment of the area lying *beyond* the cut (on the free-edge side) about the neutral axis, and $I_x$ is the second moment of area of the entire section.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
1. Valid only for **thin-walled** sections, where the wall thickness is small compared to other dimensions ($t \le b/10$ typically).
2. Assumes the section is **open** (not closed).
3. Assumes **linear variation of shear stress** across the wall thickness (uniform $\tau$ across $t$).
4. Does not account for **shear lag** in wide flanges or for **torsional warping** in non-compact sections.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme: Module 3 contains internal choice. Both Question A and Question B carry **equal marks** (14 each) and students answer **any one**. We provide both with full worked solutions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**
*Find the location of the shear centre of a thin-walled channel section of uniform thickness $t$, with flange width $b$ and web depth $h$. Also compute the maximum shear stress in the section under a vertical shear force of magnitude $V$ applied at the shear centre.*

**Sub-part (a) — Location of Shear Centre (7 marks):**

By symmetry the SC lies on the horizontal centroidal axis. Let $e$ be the horizontal distance of the SC from the mid-thickness line of the web (measured outward, towards the open side).

**Second moment of area of the section about the centroidal $x$-axis:**

$$I_x \;=\; \dfrac{t h^3}{12} \;+\; \dfrac{b t (h+t)^2}{2}$$

For thin walls ($t \ll h$), drop the $t^3$ term in the flange's own moment of inertia. Then:

$$I_x \;\approx\; \dfrac{t h^3}{12} \;+\; \dfrac{b t h^2}{2} \quad \text{(using } h+t \approx h \text{ in the lever arm squared)}$$

**Resultant shear force in each flange:**

Shear flow in the flange (measured from outer tip, distance $s$):

$$q_f(s) \;=\; \dfrac{V\, t\, (h+t)}{2 I_x}\, s$$

Resultant in one flange (integration over $s = 0 \to b$):

$$F_f \;=\; \dfrac{V t (h+t)\, b^2}{4 I_x}$$

**Moment equilibrium about the mid-line of the web:**

The two flange forces form a couple of magnitude $2 F_f \cdot \dfrac{(h+t)}{2}$, which must equal the moment $V \cdot e$ of the applied load about the SC:

$$V \cdot e \;=\; 2 F_f \cdot \dfrac{(h+t)}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{V t (h+t)^2 b^2}{4 I_x}$$

Cancelling $V$ and simplifying:

$$\boxed{\,e \;=\; \dfrac{b^2 h^2 t}{4\, I_x}\,} \quad \text{(general)}$$

Substituting $I_x = \dfrac{t h^3}{12} + \dfrac{b t h^2}{2}$:

$$e \;=\; \dfrac{b^2 h^2 t}{4 t \left(\dfrac{h^3}{12} + \dfrac{b h^2}{2}\right)} \;=\; \dfrac{3 b^2}{h + 6 b} \quad \text{(simplified form)}$$

**Sub-part (b) — Maximum Shear Stress (7 marks):**

Maximum shear stress occurs in the **web at the neutral axis**. Shear flow in the web (at distance $y$ from the top of the web):

$$q_w(y) \;=\; \dfrac{V}{I_x}\left[\dfrac{b t (h+t)}{2} + \dfrac{t y (h-y)}{2}\right]$$

At the neutral axis, $y = h/2$:

$$q_{w,\max} \;=\; \dfrac{V}{I_x}\left[\dfrac{b t h}{2} + \dfrac{t h^2}{8}\right] \;=\; \dfrac{V t h}{8 I_x}\bigl(4 b + h\bigr)$$

Maximum shear stress (in the web, at NA):

$$\tau_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{q_{w,\max}}{t} \;=\; \dfrac{V h (4 b + h)}{8 I_x}$$

**Numerical check (optional):** For $b = 60\,\text{mm}$, $h = 120\,\text{mm}$, $t = 6\,\text{mm}$, $V = 10\,\text{kN}$:
- $I_x = \dfrac{6 \cdot 120^3}{12} + \dfrac{60 \cdot 6 \cdot 120^2}{2} = 864000 + 2592000 = 3.456 \times 10^6\,\text{mm}^4$
- $e = \dfrac{60^2 \cdot 120^2 \cdot 6}{4 \cdot 3.456 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{3.1104 \times 10^8}{1.3824 \times 10^7} \approx 22.5\,\text{mm}$
- $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{10000 \cdot 120 \cdot (240 + 120)}{8 \cdot 3.456 \times 10^6} \approx 15.6\,\text{MPa}$ ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, Internal Choice]**
*An unequal-flange I-section has the following dimensions:*
- *Top flange: width $b_1 = 80\,\text{mm}$, thickness $t_1 = 10\,\text{mm}$*
- *Bottom flange: width $b_2 = 120\,\text{mm}$, thickness $t_2 = 10\,\text{mm}$*
- *Web: depth $h = 200\,\text{mm}$ (clear), thickness $t_w = 8\,\text{mm}$*
- *Vertical shear force $V = 25\,\text{kN}$ acts at the shear centre.*

*Determine (a) the position of the shear centre from the web's mid-line, and (b) the maximum shear stress in the section.*

**Sub-part (a) — Shear Centre Location (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Find the centroid $\bar{y}$ from the bottom flange (datum).**

Areas and centroidal distances from the bottom of the section:

- Bottom flange: $A_1 = 120 \times 10 = 1200\,\text{mm}^2$, $\bar{y}_1 = 5\,\text{mm}$
- Web: $A_2 = 8 \times 200 = 1600\,\text{mm}^2$, $\bar{y}_2 = 10 + 100 = 110\,\text{mm}$
- Top flange: $A_3 = 80 \times 10 = 800\,\text{mm}^2$, $\bar{y}_3 = 10 + 200 + 5 = 215\,\text{mm}$

Total area: $A = 1200 + 1600 + 800 = 3600\,\text{mm}^2$

$$\bar{y} \;=\; \dfrac{1200 \cdot 5 + 1600 \cdot 110 + 800 \cdot 215}{3600} \;=\; \dfrac{6000 + 176000 + 172000}{3600} \;=\; \dfrac{354000}{3600} \;\approx\; 98.33\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 2: Compute $I_x$ about the centroidal axis.**

Using parallel axis theorem:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_x &= \left[\dfrac{120 \cdot 10^3}{12} + 1200 (98.33 - 5)^2\right] \\
    &\quad + \left[\dfrac{8 \cdot 200^3}{12} + 1600 (110 - 98.33)^2\right] \\
    &\quad + \left[\dfrac{80 \cdot 10^3}{12} + 800 (215 - 98.33)^2\right] \\[4pt]
    &= [10000 + 1200 \cdot 8721.4] + [5333333 + 1600 \cdot 136.2] + [6667 + 800 \cdot 13619.0] \\[4pt]
    &\approx 1.0466 \times 10^7 + 5.5513 \times 10^6 + 1.0895 \times 10^7 \\[4pt]
    &\approx 2.69 \times 10^7\,\text{mm}^4
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Shear flow in the top flange (cut at distance $s$ from the free tip):**

$$q_{top}(s) \;=\; \dfrac{V \cdot s t_1 \cdot (h_{top})}{I_x}$$

where $h_{top} = 215 - 98.33 = 116.67\,\text{mm}$ is the distance of the top flange's centroid from the NA.

**Resultant in top flange:**

$$F_{top} \;=\; \dfrac{V t_1 h_{top} b_1^2}{4 I_x} \;=\; \dfrac{25000 \cdot 10 \cdot 116.67 \cdot 80^2}{4 \cdot 2.69 \times 10^7} \;\approx\; 1735\,\text{N}$$

**Step 4: Resultant in bottom flange (similarly):**

Distance of bottom flange from NA: $h_{bot} = 98.33 - 5 = 93.33\,\text{mm}$.

$$F_{bot} \;=\; \dfrac{V t_2 h_{bot} b_2^2}{4 I_x} \;=\; \dfrac{25000 \cdot 10 \cdot 93.33 \cdot 120^2}{4 \cdot 2.69 \times 10^7} \;\approx\; 3123\,\text{N}$$

**Step 5: Shear centre location.**

By symmetry about the vertical axis, the SC lies on the vertical line through the centroid. Its **horizontal position coincides with the web mid-line** (since $I_y$ symmetry is preserved). For a singly-symmetric section (only the $x$-axis is an axis of symmetry), the SC lies on the $x$-axis itself. So the **horizontal offset from the web mid-line is zero** for this I-section (the $y$-axis is also a symmetry axis because the I-section has two axes of symmetry, even with unequal flanges — the I-shape preserves a vertical centre-line).

$$\boxed{\,e \;=\; 0\,\text{(SC lies on the vertical web centre-line)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key clarification:** An I-section has **two axes of symmetry** *regardless of whether the flanges are equal or unequal* — the $y$-axis (vertical, through the web centre-line) is always a symmetry axis. Therefore the SC always lies on the $y$-axis. The $x$-axis being a horizontal axis of symmetry also holds. So both centroid and SC lie at the intersection of the two axes of symmetry for any I-section.

**Sub-part (b) — Maximum Shear Stress (7 marks):**

The maximum shear stress in an I-section occurs in the **web at the neutral axis**.

The shear flow at the NA is the sum of the contributions of the larger flange (bottom, here) and the half-web above NA:

$$q_{w,\max} \;=\; \dfrac{V}{I_x} \left[ F_{bot-contribution} + \dfrac{A_w^{above}}{1} \cdot \bar{y}_{above} \right]$$

Where the area of web above the NA: $A_w^{above} = 8 \cdot (110 - 98.33) \cdot ... $ — re-derive cleanly using $Q$ at NA:

$$Q_{NA} \;=\; A_{bot} \cdot h_{bot} \;+\; A_{w,above} \cdot \bar{y}_{w,above}$$

- $A_{bot} \cdot h_{bot} = 1200 \cdot 93.33 = 1.12 \times 10^5\,\text{mm}^3$
- $A_{w,above} = 8 \times (110 - 98.33) = 93.36\,\text{mm}^2$, $\bar{y}_{w,above} = 5.84\,\text{mm}$
- $A_{w,above} \cdot \bar{y}_{w,above} = 545\,\text{mm}^3$
- $Q_{NA} \approx 1.125 \times 10^5\,\text{mm}^3$

$$\tau_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{V \cdot Q_{NA}}{I_x \cdot t_w} \;=\; \dfrac{25000 \cdot 1.125 \times 10^5}{2.69 \times 10^7 \cdot 8} \;\approx\; 13.07\,\text{MPa}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on Shear Centre problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the symmetry argument** (2–3 marks lost). Always state explicitly that the SC lies on every axis of symmetry. Failing to do this costs the "conceptual" portion of the answer.
> 2. **Sign error in the moment equation.** When taking moments, *all* flange forces should be entered with the correct rotational sense. For a channel, both flanges' moment contributions are in the *same* direction (both push the resultant towards the open side).
> 3. **Mixing up $Q_x$ convention.** The integral is taken from the **cut** to the **free edge**, not from the cut to the other end. Reversing this changes the sign of $q$ and hence the direction of the SC offset.
> 4. **Including $t$ in the wrong places.** For thin walls ($t \le b/10$), the standard KTU-accepted form is $I_x \approx \dfrac{t h^3}{12} + \dfrac{b t h^2}{2}$, *not* the exact $I_x$ with $(h+t)^2$. Examiners usually accept the approximate form, but stating the assumption is essential.
> 5. **Not verifying continuity at the wall junction.** At every junction (e.g., flange–web interface), the shear flow value from the flange side must equal the shear flow value from the web side. Skipping this check costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting units in the final answer.** Always express $e$ in **mm** (or as per the data), and $\tau$ in **MPa** or **N/mm²**.
> 7. **Confusing "shear centre" with "centre of twist".** The SC is the *load application point* for no-twist bending; the centre of twist is a different concept related to non-uniform torsion.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Shear Centre**

- **Definition to memorize:** Shear centre = point of load application for **bending without twist**; property of cross-section geometry alone.
- **Sign of $q$:** Use the convention that $q$ on the wall is positive if it points in the direction such that the cut-out piece tends to rotate clockwise under a downward $V$.
- **Formula triad (must memorize):**
  - Shear flow: $q = VQ_x / I_x$
  - Shear stress: $\tau = q / t$
  - Twist equilibrium: $V e = \sum F_i d_i$
- **Symmetry rule (very high yield):**
  - 2 axes of symmetry → SC $\equiv$ Centroid
  - 1 axis of symmetry → SC lies on that axis
  - 0 axes of symmetry → SC at general point
- **Key derived result for a channel of uniform thickness:**
  $$e = \dfrac{b^2 h^2 t}{4 I_x} \quad \text{or} \quad e \approx \dfrac{3 b^2}{h + 6 b} \text{ (simplified)}$$
- **The SC of a channel lies on the open side of the web** (i.e., away from the web into the gap), for any non-degenerate $b$ and $h$.
- **Continuity of shear flow at wall junctions** is a self-check — if it fails, the assumption of a single load point is invalid.
- **The web carries the entire vertical shear** in I- and channel-sections (for vertical $V$); flanges carry horizontal shear that forms a couple balancing the moment of the web's shear about the SC.
- **Units sanity check:** $V$ in N, $I_x$ in mm⁴, $Q_x$ in mm³, $t$ in mm → $\tau$ in MPa. Always verify dimensional consistency.
- **Assumption stack to declare in every solution:**
  1. Thin-walled ($t \le b/10$, $t \le h/10$)
  2. Uniform thickness (unless stated otherwise)
  3. Linear shear stress across wall thickness
  4. Plane sections remain plane (Euler–Bernoulli)
  5. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic
- **Real-world link:** Aircraft tail, cold-formed purlins, crane girders — all designed with the SC criterion to prevent twisting.
- **Common formula variants to remember:**
  - For a thin rectangular strip: shear centre is at the mid-point of the long edge.
  - For a narrow rectangular section: SC is at the mid-height (centroid).
  - For a Z-section: SC is at the centroid, but with a horizontal offset from the web (for unequal legs).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Study Tip:** Solve at least 3–4 previous-year KTU questions on channel sections and I-sections. The algebraic pattern is identical — only the geometry numbers change. Master the channel derivation once, and you can solve 80% of KTU Shear Centre questions in under 12 minutes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Winkler Bach theory for bending of curved beams (with rectangular cross-section)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET415 - ADVANCED MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Winkler Bach theory for bending of curved beams (with rectangular cross-section)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Winkler-Bach Theory (Elastic Theory of Curved Beams):** A rigorous elastic analysis proposed by **E. Winkler (1858)** and later refined by **C. Bach (1870)**, which determines the exact distribution of bending stresses in **curved beams** (members whose initial radius of curvature is comparable to, or smaller than, the depth of the cross-section). Unlike the simple flexure formula $\sigma = \dfrac{My}{I}$ (which is invalid for curved members), the Winkler-Bach theory correctly predicts the **hyperbolic (non-linear) variation of stress** across the cross-section and provides a method to locate the true position of the **neutral axis**, which does **not** pass through the centroid for curved beams.

### 1.2 When is Curved Beam Theory Necessary?

The straight-beam flexure formula gives accurate results **only** when the radius of curvature is very large compared to the depth of the section. A convenient indicator is the **Curvature Ratio** $R/\bar{y}$ (or $R/c$):

| Condition | Applicable Theory | Stress Distribution |
|---|---|---|
| $R/c \geq 10$ | Straight beam (Euler-Bernoulli) | Linear |
| $R/c < 10$ | **Curved beam (Winkler-Bach)** | **Hyperbolic** |

where $R$ = radius of centroidal axis, and $c$ = distance from centroidal axis to the extreme fibre.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world intuition — "The Hook Analogy":**
> Imagine lifting a heavy bucket using a **curved iron hook**. The inner fibre of the hook (closer to the centre of curvature) is highly compressed, while the outer fibre is under tension. If you apply the simple flexure formula $M y/I$, you will find the inner-fibre stress is *lower* than the outer-fibre stress. But actual experiments (and 3D photo-elasticity) reveal the opposite — **the inner fibre carries a much higher stress** than the straight-beam formula predicts.
>
> Why? Because in a curved beam, the **fibre lengths are not equal**. The inner fibres are short and stiff, so they absorb more load per unit area. The outer fibres are long and flexible, so they elongate more but carry comparatively less stress. This **geometric crowding of fibres** near the inner radius causes the non-linear (hyperbolic) stress distribution that Winkler-Bach captures mathematically.

### 1.4 Fundamental Assumptions of Winkler-Bach Theory

1. The material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (Hooke's Law valid).
2. **Plane sections remain plane** after bending (kinematic assumption identical to Euler-Bernoulli) — i.e., cross-sections rotate as rigid discs about the neutral axis.
3. The radial distribution of stress is represented by a **pure bending** stress field, no independent radial stress (this is a mild inconsistency corrected by the "exact" theory of Timoshenko, but Winkler-Bach is the **standard KTU-prescribed** approximate theory).
4. The **strains vary linearly with the distance from the neutral axis** (just like straight beams), but because stress is non-linearly related to strain when passing through a variable-thickness element, the resulting **stress distribution is hyperbolic**.

### 1.5 Key Geometry for a Rectangular Cross-Section

For a rectangular section of width $b$ and depth $h$ (radial direction):

$$
r_i = \text{inner radius},\qquad r_o = r_i + h = \text{outer radius}
$$

$$
R = \frac{r_i + r_o}{2} = r_i + \frac{h}{2} = \text{centroidal radius}
$$

$$
A = b \cdot h = \text{area of the cross-section}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Symbol — Eccentricity (e):** The distance between the **centroidal axis** and the **neutral axis** is denoted $e = \vert R - r_n \vert$. This is *always* positive and is the single most important geometric parameter in curved-beam stress analysis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Winkler-Bach Theory

The derivation proceeds in a structured, deductive sequence. Each step answers a specific "Why":

- **Step 1 — Strain compatibility:** Justify the **plane sections remain plane** assumption. The fibres in a curved beam must, by compatibility, experience strains that vary **linearly** with their distance from the neutral axis:

  $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{r_n} \quad \text{(for small curvature variations)}$$

  where $y$ is the distance from the neutral axis.

- **Step 2 — Hooke's Law conversion:** Apply $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ to convert the linear strain field into a stress field that is *apparently* linear in $y$.

- **Step 3 — The "geometric" correction:** Because we are now operating in **polar (curvilinear) coordinates**, the length of a fibre at radius $r$ is **not constant** across the width $dy$. A fibre of thickness $dr$ has length $b \cdot r \cdot d\theta$, which is proportional to $r$. Hence the *actual* force carried by a fibre of width $dr$ is $\sigma(r) \cdot b \cdot r \cdot d\theta$, not $\sigma \cdot b \cdot dy$.

- **Step 4 — Equilibrium of forces in the cross-section:** The integral of the elementary force over the section must equal **zero** (no net axial force from pure bending):

  $$\int_A \sigma \, dA = 0$$

  Substituting and integrating, this yields the **location of the neutral axis**.

- **Step 5 — Equilibrium of moments:** The moment of the stress distribution about the neutral axis must equal the **applied bending moment** $M$. This yields the **final stress formula**.

- **Step 6 — Closed-form integration for rectangular section:** Because the cross-section is bounded by constant radii $r_i$ and $r_o$, the integrals have closed-form solutions involving logarithms.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula** you are expected to know for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations on this topic. Memorise them — they appear almost verbatim in past papers.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Radius of neutral axis (rectangular section)** | $r_n = \dfrac{h}{\ln(r_o/r_i)} = \dfrac{r_o - r_i}{\ln(r_o/r_i)}$ | length (m, mm) |
| 2 | **Eccentricity (rectangular section)** | $e = R - r_n$ | length; always $> 0$ here |
| 3 | **Centroidal radius** | $R = \dfrac{r_i + r_o}{2}$ | length |
| 4 | **Area (rectangular)** | $A = b\,h = b(r_o - r_i)$ | length² |
| 5 | **Winkler-Bach bending stress at radius $r$** | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M}{A\,e}\cdot\dfrac{R - r}{r}$ | Pa (or N/mm²) |
| 6 | **Alternative form (measured from neutral axis)** | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M\,y}{A\,e\,(r_n - y)}$ | valid for $r_i \le r \le r_o$ |
| 7 | **Direct (axial) stress from normal force $N$** | $\sigma_d = \dfrac{N}{A}$ | uniform over section |
| 8 | **Combined stress at radius $r$** | $\sigma = \dfrac{N}{A} + \dfrac{M}{A\,e}\cdot\dfrac{R - r}{r}$ | sign convention: tension +ve |
| 9 | **Inner-fibre stress** | $\sigma_i = \dfrac{M}{A\,e}\cdot\dfrac{R - r_i}{r_i}$ | usually max compressive |
| 10 | **Outer-fibre stress** | $\sigma_o = \dfrac{M}{A\,e}\cdot\dfrac{R - r_o}{r_o}$ | usually max tensile |
| 11 | **Equivalent straight-beam stress** (for comparison) | $\sigma_{\text{straight}} = \dfrac{M\,c}{I}$ | shown for error estimation |
| 12 | **Geometric property (rectangular only)** | $K = \dfrac{1}{A}\int_r \dfrac{dr}{r} = \dfrac{1}{b\,h}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_o}{r_i}\right)$ | used to find $r_n$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity-check tip for exams:** $\sigma_i$ and $\sigma_o$ in a curved beam are **opposite in sign** and $|\sigma_i| > |\sigma_o|$. The straight-beam formula always gives $|\sigma_o| > |\sigma_i|$. The discrepancy can exceed **50 %** when $R/c < 3$.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of crane hooks, chain links, C-clamps and shackles** — all are heavily curved members.
- **Pelton turbine buckets** in hydroelectric power stations.
- **Curved arch ribs** in bridges and civil structures.
- **Connecting arms of forging tongs**, **hinges**, and **split bearings**.
- **Curved fillet welds** in pressure vessel nozzle reinforcements.

In all such cases, the **Winkler-Bach theory** gives a far more realistic and safe estimate of the peak stress than the straight-beam formula.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Position of the Neutral Axis for a Rectangular Section

**Objective:** Show that the neutral axis of a curved rectangular section is at radius $r_n = \dfrac{h}{\ln(r_o/r_i)}$.

**Starting Hypothesis (plane sections remain plane):** The strain in a fibre at distance $y$ from the neutral axis is

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{y}{r_n}
$$

By Hooke's Law, the bending stress is

$$
\sigma_b = E\,\varepsilon = \frac{E\,y}{r_n}
$$

A fibre at distance $y$ from the neutral axis corresponds to a radius $r$ such that $y = r_n - r$ (measuring $y$ positively inward). Hence

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{E\,(r_n - r)}{r_n} = E\left(1 - \frac{r}{r_n}\right)
$$

**Equilibrium of axial force** (no net axial load from pure bending, $\int \sigma_b\, dA = 0$):

$$
\int_{r_i}^{r_o} \sigma_b \cdot (b\,dr) = 0
$$

Substitute the expression for $\sigma_b$:

$$
\int_{r_i}^{r_o} \left[E\left(1 - \frac{r}{r_n}\right)\right] b\,dr = 0
$$

Pull out constants $E$ and $b$:

$$
E b \int_{r_i}^{r_o} \left(1 - \frac{r}{r_n}\right) dr = 0
$$

Expand the integrand:

$$
E b \int_{r_i}^{r_o} 1\, dr - \frac{E b}{r_n}\int_{r_i}^{r_o} r\, dr = 0
$$

Evaluate the two elementary integrals:

$$
E b \left[r\right]_{r_i}^{r_o} - \frac{E b}{r_n}\left[\frac{r^2}{2}\right]_{r_i}^{r_o} = 0
$$

$$
E b\,(r_o - r_i) - \frac{E b}{r_n}\cdot\frac{r_o^2 - r_i^2}{2} = 0
$$

Divide through by $E b$:

$$
(r_o - r_i) - \frac{1}{r_n}\cdot\frac{r_o^2 - r_i^2}{2} = 0
$$

Use the difference-of-squares identity $r_o^2 - r_i^2 = (r_o - r_i)(r_o + r_i)$:

$$
(r_o - r_i) - \frac{1}{r_n}\cdot\frac{(r_o - r_i)(r_o + r_i)}{2} = 0
$$

Divide by $(r_o - r_i) \neq 0$:

$$
1 - \frac{r_o + r_i}{2\,r_n} = 0
$$

Recognise that $R = \dfrac{r_o + r_i}{2}$:

$$
1 - \frac{R}{r_n} = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\,r_n = R\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This result $r_n = R$ is WRONG for curved beams** — it would mean the neutral axis coincides with the centroidal axis, which is true only for *straight* beams. **Where did we go wrong?**
>
> The error lies in **Step 1**: the area element. In a curved beam, the strip of width $dr$ has area $b\,dr$ — *correct* — but the **moment of the stress about the neutral axis** depends on the *length of the lever arm* in the deformed state, not the original. The "plane sections remain plane" assumption is too restrictive. We must modify the stress field itself so that equilibrium is satisfied, allowing $r_n \neq R$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Correct Winkler-Bach Stress Function

**Hypothesis (Winkler-Bach kinematic assumption):** Cross-sections rotate as rigid planes, but the strain is measured relative to the *centroid of rotation* (the pole), which is taken to lie on the **neutral axis**. The strain at radius $r$ for a small rotation $d\theta$ is

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{(r_n - r)\,d\theta}{r_n\,d\theta} = \frac{r_n - r}{r_n}
$$

The *modified* stress field that satisfies the polar geometry takes the form

$$
\sigma_b(r) = \frac{k}{r} - c
$$

where $k$ and $c$ are constants to be determined from the two equilibrium equations.

**Condition 1 — Zero net axial force:**

$$
\int_{r_i}^{r_o} \sigma_b(r)\, b\, dr = 0
$$

$$
\int_{r_i}^{r_o} \left(\frac{k}{r} - c\right) b\, dr = 0
$$

$$
b\left[k\,\ln r - c\,r\right]_{r_i}^{r_o} = 0
$$

$$
b\left[k\,\ln\!\left(\frac{r_o}{r_i}\right) - c\,(r_o - r_i)\right] = 0
$$

Since $b \neq 0$:

$$
k\,\ln\!\left(\frac{r_o}{r_i}\right) = c\,(r_o - r_i) = c\,h
$$

Therefore:

$$
k = \frac{c\,h}{\ln(r_o/r_i)} = c\,r_n \quad \text{(using the definition of } r_n\text{)}
$$

**Condition 2 — Moment equilibrium:** The moment of the stress about the neutral axis equals $M$. Since the moment arm is $(r - r_n)$:

$$
M = -\int_{r_i}^{r_o} \sigma_b(r)\,(r - r_n)\,b\, dr
$$

(The negative sign reflects the sign convention — when the moment tends to *decrease* the curvature, fibres with $r > r_n$ are in tension.)

Substitute $\sigma_b = \dfrac{k}{r} - c = \dfrac{c\,r_n}{r} - c$:

$$
M = -b\int_{r_i}^{r_o} \left(\frac{c\,r_n}{r} - c\right)(r - r_n)\, dr
$$

Expand $(r - r_n)$:

$$
M = -b\,c\int_{r_i}^{r_o} \left(\frac{r_n}{r}(r - r_n) - (r - r_n)\right) dr
$$

$$
M = -b\,c\int_{r_i}^{r_o} \left(r_n - \frac{r_n^2}{r} - r + r_n\right) dr
$$

$$
M = -b\,c\int_{r_i}^{r_o} \left(2\,r_n - r - \frac{r_n^2}{r}\right) dr
$$

Integrate term by term over $[r_i,\,r_o]$:

$$
\int_{r_i}^{r_o} 2\,r_n\, dr = 2\,r_n(r_o - r_i) = 2\,r_n\,h
$$

$$
\int_{r_i}^{r_o} r\, dr = \frac{r_o^2 - r_i^2}{2} = \frac{(r_o - r_i)(r_o + r_i)}{2} = h\,R
$$

$$
\int_{r_i}^{r_o} \frac{r_n^2}{r}\, dr = r_n^2 \ln\!\left(\frac{r_o}{r_i}\right) = r_n^2 \cdot \frac{h}{r_n} = r_n\,h
$$

Combine:

$$
M = -b\,c\left[2\,r_n\,h - h\,R - r_n\,h\right]
$$

$$
M = -b\,c\,h\left[2\,r_n - R - r_n\right]
$$

$$
M = -b\,c\,h\,(r_n - R)
$$

Since $R > r_n$ (centroid is *outside* the neutral axis for a typical curved beam), the bracket is negative, so $M$ comes out positive as it should (we can absorb the sign by redefining $c$ positive when stress increases as $r$ decreases). Letting $e = R - r_n$:

$$
M = b\,c\,h\,e = A\,c\,e
$$

Solve for $c$:

$$
c = \frac{M}{A\,e}
$$

Recall $k = c\,r_n$, hence

$$
k = \frac{M\,r_n}{A\,e}
$$

**Final stress expression:**

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_b(r) = \frac{M}{A\,e}\left(\frac{r_n}{r} - 1\right) = \frac{M}{A\,e}\cdot\frac{r_n - r}{r}\;}
$$

Equivalently, using $R - r = (R - r_n) + (r_n - r) = e - (r - r_n)$:

$$
\sigma_b(r) = \frac{M}{A\,e}\left(\frac{R - r}{r}\right)
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Closed-Form Expression for $r_n$ (Rectangular)

From Condition 1 above, the requirement $k = c\,r_n$ **defines** the neutral axis:

$$
r_n = \frac{h}{\ln(r_o/r_i)} = \frac{r_o - r_i}{\ln(r_o/r_i)}
$$

This is a **logarithmic mean** of the inner and outer radii, not the arithmetic mean.

**Sanity checks:**

- When $h \ll R$ (i.e. $r_o \to r_i \to R$): $\ln(r_o/r_i) = \ln(1 + h/r_i) \approx h/r_i$, so $r_n \approx r_i \approx R$. ✓
- Numerical example: $r_i = 20\,\text{mm},\, r_o = 30\,\text{mm} \Rightarrow R = 25\,\text{mm},\, h = 10\,\text{mm},\, r_n = 10/\ln(1.5) = 10/0.4055 = 24.66\,\text{mm}$. So $e = 25 - 24.66 = 0.34\,\text{mm}$ — a small but *critical* value.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Maximum Fibre Stresses

**At the inner fibre** ($r = r_i$):

$$
\sigma_i = \frac{M}{A\,e}\cdot\frac{R - r_i}{r_i} = \frac{M}{A\,e}\cdot\frac{h/2}{r_i}
$$

Since $h/2 = R - r_i$:

$$
\sigma_i = \frac{M}{A\,e}\cdot\frac{R - r_i}{r_i}
$$

**At the outer fibre** ($r = r_o$):

$$
\sigma_o = \frac{M}{A\,e}\cdot\frac{R - r_o}{r_o} = -\frac{M}{A\,e}\cdot\frac{R - r_o}{r_o}
$$

The negative sign indicates that $\sigma_o$ is opposite in nature to $\sigma_i$ (one tensile, one compressive) — characteristic of pure bending.

**Ratio of magnitudes:**

$$
\left\vert\frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_o}\right\vert = \frac{r_o}{r_i}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **very clean result** and a favourite KTU 14-marker sub-question. For example, a hook with $r_o = 30\,\text{mm}$ and $r_i = 20\,\text{mm}$ has $|\sigma_i|/|\sigma_o| = 1.5$, i.e., the inner fibre carries **50 % more stress** than the outer fibre.

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> **Problem:** A curved rectangular bar has $b = 40\,\text{mm},\, h = 60\,\text{mm},\, r_i = 80\,\text{mm}$. It is subjected to a bending moment $M = 6\,\text{kN·m}$ that tends to straighten the bar (i.e., increase the radius of curvature). Determine the stresses at the inner and outer fibres using (a) the Winkler-Bach theory and (b) the straight-beam formula. Comment on the comparison.

**Step 1 — Compute geometric quantities:**

$$
r_o = r_i + h = 80 + 60 = 140\,\text{mm}
$$

$$
R = \frac{r_i + r_o}{2} = \frac{80 + 140}{2} = 110\,\text{mm}
$$

$$
A = b \cdot h = 40 \cdot 60 = 2400\,\text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $r_n$ and $e$:**

$$
r_n = \frac{h}{\ln(r_o/r_i)} = \frac{60}{\ln(140/80)} = \frac{60}{\ln(1.75)} = \frac{60}{0.5596} = 107.21\,\text{mm}
$$

$$
e = R - r_n = 110 - 107.21 = 2.79\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Winkler-Bach stresses:**

$$
\sigma_i = \frac{M}{A\,e}\cdot\frac{R - r_i}{r_i} = \frac{6 \times 10^6}{2400 \cdot 2.79}\cdot\frac{110 - 80}{80}
$$

$$
\frac{M}{A\,e} = \frac{6 \times 10^6}{6696} = 896.0\,\text{N/mm}^2 \text{ (intermediate factor)}
$$

$$
\sigma_i = 896.0 \cdot \frac{30}{80} = 896.0 \cdot 0.375 = +336.0\,\text{N/mm}^2
$$

$$
\sigma_o = -\frac{M}{A\,e}\cdot\frac{R - r_o}{r_o} = -896.0 \cdot \frac{110 - 140}{140} = -896.0 \cdot \frac{-30}{140} = +192.0\,\text{N/mm}^2
$$

> **Hmm — both are positive?** This is because, with the chosen sign convention (moment increasing radius of curvature), the bar tends to *uncoil*. The fibres near the centre of curvature are stretched, and the outer fibres are *also* on the same side of the neutral axis. To get a cleaner picture, one would take $M$ as reducing the curvature; signs then flip appropriately. The magnitude analysis is what matters for KTU.

**Magnitudes:** $|\sigma_i| = 336\,\text{MPa}$, $|\sigma_o| = 192\,\text{MPa}$. Ratio: $336/192 = 1.75 = r_o/r_i$ ✓

**Step 4 — Straight-beam (flexure) formula for comparison:**

$$
I = \frac{b\,h^3}{12} = \frac{40 \cdot 60^3}{12} = 720{,}000\,\text{mm}^4
$$

$$
c = h/2 = 30\,\text{mm}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{straight}} = \frac{M\,c}{I} = \frac{6 \times 10^6 \cdot 30}{720{,}000} = 250\,\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:**

| Method | Inner fibre $\sigma_i$ | Outer fibre $\sigma_o$ |
|---|---|---|
| Straight-beam (Euler-Bernoulli) | $-250\,\text{MPa}$ | $+250\,\text{MPa}$ |
| **Winkler-Bach (curved beam)** | $-336\,\text{MPa}$ | $+192\,\text{MPa}$ |
| **% Error in straight-beam** | **+25.6 % under-prediction** | **+30.2 % over-prediction** |

> **Conclusion:** Ignoring curvature under-predicts the maximum (inner) stress by 25.6 %, which is **unsafe for design**. Winkler-Bach theory is essential here.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture Flow — Winkler-Bach Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["STEP A: IDENTIFY GEOMETRY"] --> stepB["Read r_i, r_o, b, h, M, N"]
    stepB --> stepC["Compute R, A, h"]
    stepC --> stepD["STEP B: LOCATE NEUTRAL AXIS"]
    stepD --> stepE["r_n = h / ln(r_o / r_i)"]
    stepE --> stepF["Compute e = R - r_n"]
    stepF --> stepG["STEP C: APPLY WINKLER-BACH STRESS LAW"]
    stepG --> stepH["sigma_b = M / Ae x R - r over r"]
    stepH --> stepI["Compute sigma_i at r = r_i"]
    stepH --> stepJ["Compute sigma_o at r = r_o"]
    stepI --> stepK["STEP D: COMBINE WITH AXIAL LOAD"]
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> stepL["sigma_total = N/A + sigma_b"]
    stepL --> stepM["STEP E: VALIDATE AND REPORT"]
    stepM --> stepN["Check sign convention"]
    stepM --> stepO["Compare with straight-beam value"]
    stepM --> stepP["Compute percent error for design margin"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisC["CENTROIDAL AXIS at R"] --- neutral["NEUTRAL AXIS at r_n"]
    inner["INNER FIBRE r_i"] --- outer["OUTER FIBRE r_o"]
    inner --> innerL["r_i (smaller radius)"]
    outer --> outerL["r_o (larger radius)"]
    neutral --> neutralL["r_n = h / ln(r_o / r_i)"]
    axisC --> axisCL["R = r_i + h / 2"]
    innerL --> stressH["HIGH stress sigma_i"]
    outerL --> stressL["LOW stress sigma_o"]
    stressH --- note["HYPERBOLIC stress profile: stress varies as 1/r"]
    stressL --- note
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STRAIGHT["STRAIGHT BEAM (Euler-Bernoulli)"]
        s1["Neutral Axis at centroid"]

    end
    subgraph CURVED["CURVED BEAM (Winkler-Bach)"]

        c2["Hyperbolic 1/r stress distribution"]

    end
    STRAIGHT -->|R/c less than 10| CURVED
```
